rule: API documentation and payloads should be semantically accurate, future-proof, and include human-readable fields. Avoid implementation-specific details in docs, ensure required fields are never silently empty, and use consistent PATCH semantics.
trigger: When writing or modifying API schemas, documentation, PATCH endpoints, or audit event payloads
rationale: Implementation-specific docs break when internals change. Silently empty required fields confuse API consumers. Missing human-readable names force users to do extra lookups. Reviewers consistently flag these across nucleus, apps, and extensions.

rule: When a method encounters an unsupported type, impossible state, or unresolvable required field, throw an explicit error rather than silently returning empty/no-op results or continuing execution.
trigger: When handling unexpected types in match/switch expressions, resolving required fields that may be absent, or encountering states that should be impossible
rationale: Silent returns mask bugs and make debugging harder. Three instances across two PRs from different reviewers show this is a strongly-held team convention. Reviewers explicitly asked for throws over silent skips.

rule: Sorting, pagination, and relational lookups should be performed in database queries, not in application memory. Avoid N+1 query patterns by combining lookups into single query pipelines.
trigger: When implementing list endpoints with sorting or pagination, joining related entities, or fetching collections that are filtered or sorted after retrieval
rationale: In-memory sorting of unbounded collections causes performance degradation at scale. N+1 queries multiply latency. Reviewers explicitly asked to push sorting/paging into persister methods and combine lookups into single query pipelines.

rule: Routine operational messages should use DEBUG, not INFO. Warnings should only fire on genuinely unexpected conditions, and log messages must accurately reflect the condition that triggers them.
trigger: When adding or modifying log statements, especially when choosing between INFO/DEBUG/WARN levels or writing log message text
rationale: Overly verbose INFO logs create noise in production. Warnings on legitimate conditions cause alert fatigue. Inaccurate log messages mislead on-call engineers. Reviewers enforce logging discipline across multiple PRs.

rule: When code looks up an entity that may not exist (deleted, archived, never created), handle the absence explicitly — clean up stale references, provide fallback behavior, or defend against null returns.
trigger: When fetching entities by ID that could be deleted, archived, or not yet created, or when processing collections that reference other entities
rationale: Unhandled absent entities cause NullPointerExceptions or stale data in indexes. Reviewers flagged cases where archived apps remained in search indexes and where missing instances or commits had no fallback handling.

rule: TODOs must reference tracked tickets. Forward-looking assumptions must be documented inline. Interface-layer docs must not reference implementation details from other modules.
trigger: When adding TODO comments, writing docstrings on interface-layer classes, or making assumptions about current system behavior that may change
rationale: Untracked TODOs are never resolved. Undocumented assumptions become invisible tech debt. Cross-module implementation details in interface docs create coupling and break when internals change.

rule: Error messages shown to users should explain the probable cause in plain language and suggest resolution steps. Don't surface raw internal errors.
trigger: When writing error messages that will be displayed in the UI or returned in user-facing API responses
rationale: Raw internal errors confuse users and generate support tickets. Reviewers asked for messages like 'User hasn't logged in yet' instead of technical errors, so users can self-resolve.

rule: Fields required for operation should be non-optional types. When fields are nullable at creation but resolved at runtime, resolve and persist them before execution to ensure reproducibility.
trigger: When defining case classes or data models where fields are Optional but always expected to have values at execution time
rationale: Optional types for required fields push validation to every call site. Unresolved nullable fields at execution time break reproducibility. Reviewer asked to make non-optional fields actually non-optional in case classes and resolve all such fields before launching.

rule: When introducing new allowed behaviors or wrapping service calls in NonFatal recovery, add explicit tests for those specific scenarios.
trigger: When adding new state transitions, changing allowed behaviors between entities, or adding error recovery wrappers around service calls
rationale: New behaviors without tests are invisible regressions waiting to happen. Reviewers specifically asked for tests covering new allowed version transitions and fallback behavior when taxonomy service fails.

rule: Variable and map names must precisely describe their key-value semantics; rename when the actual key type differs from what the name implies (e.g., containerName vs imageName).
Do not match on null in Scala code; use Option types and idiomatic Scala constructs instead of null checks carried over from Java interop.
Move computations inside the conditional branch where their result is actually used, rather than computing eagerly outside the branch.
Extract the result of a method call into a variable when it is used multiple times; use pattern matching on the extracted value instead of calling the method again.
Add inline comments explaining the purpose and meaning of parameters or expressions when the code is non-obvious, especially for future-proofed or indirectly mapped values.
Remove all debugging statements (print, log, temporary outputs) from production code before submitting a PR for review.
When testing new fields or computed values, assert that the values are correct, not just that they exist or are non-null.
When fixing or updating logic in one code path, search for and apply the same change to all analogous code paths in the codebase.
Add comments explaining behavioral invariants and design decisions (e.g., why a value is not updated once set) so future developers understand the intent.
Keep test code inline and straightforward; do not extract test logic into separate helper functions unless it is reused across multiple test cases.
Use non-optional collection types (e.g., Map instead of Option[Map]) with empty defaults unless None carries distinct semantic meaning different from an empty collection.

When reading optional collection fields from external sources (databases, APIs), convert them to non-optional with getOrElse(empty) at the domain boundary rather than propagating Option throughout the domain model.
Replace deeply nested map/flatMap/match chains with for-comprehensions for readability, especially when chaining multiple Optional value extractions.
Never call .get on Option types; use flatMap, map, getOrElse, or pattern matching to safely handle the None case.
When transforming a Map's entries, call .map directly on the Map with a partial function instead of converting to a List first with .toList.map.
When the same literal value is used in multiple places within a file, extract it into a named constant defined at module or function scope to avoid duplication and ease future changes.
Always include a descriptive label or title in print and log statements so that output can be easily identified when debugging.
API calls in the frontend must include error handling that surfaces feedback to the user (such as an error toast) when the call fails, rather than failing silently.
When the same computed or literal value appears multiple times in a function, extract it into a named variable to avoid repetition and improve readability.
When styling UI components, prefer using theme helpers and design tokens over hardcoded values so that styles remain consistent and centrally maintainable.
Use a consistent import style for shared dependencies (e.g., React) across all files in a module to maintain codebase uniformity.
Use theme helper tokens and design system constants for styling values (colors, font sizes, spacing) instead of hardcoding raw pixel or hex values.
Control font-family at the global/theme level rather than setting it in individual component styles, so font changes (e.g., during a visual refresh) propagate automatically.
Remove commented-out code from test scripts and production files to improve legibility and reduce confusion during triage.
Before creating new test fixture files, check if an equivalent fixture already exists in the test suite and reuse it to avoid duplication.
When adding new API-based test steps to a feature file, check whether they can replace existing UI-based or duplicated steps in the same file to reduce redundancy.
Do not add retry logic for operations that are already retried at a lower layer, as this compounds retry attempts and can cause excessive delays or cascading failures.
When a code block is repeated across multiple call sites, extract it into a shared utility function to reduce duplication and improve maintainability.
Keep retry counts and backoff configurations consistent across the codebase; when a standard exists (e.g., 5 retries), new retry policies should match unless there is a documented reason to differ.
When introducing a new shared policy, utility, or abstraction, migrate existing callers that should use it rather than leaving them on the old approach.
Use specific, descriptive function names that convey intent (e.g., createStatusMismatchException) rather than generic names (e.g., createException) especially when multiple overloads exist.
Use a query string library (e.g., qs) to build URL search parameters instead of complex manual string interpolation, which is error-prone and hard to read.
Add initialization logging to services so that their deployment placement (which pod/node they run on) can be verified, especially when services are moved between modules.
Avoid hardcoding validation rules for third-party models and endpoints that change frequently; prefer open-ended validation or keep the list externally configurable.

When modifying dependency injection bindings (e.g., adding @Named annotations), verify that the change does not silently swap the implementation injected into consumers, and test that the affected component still behaves correctly.
When a component fetches data on mount, initialize the loading state to true immediately (or before the first render) to prevent a brief flash of the error or empty state before data arrives.

Adding a new shared library or provider (e.g., React Query) to a high-level component should be discussed at the team/architecture level first to agree on conventions, key management, and integration strategy.

Use React Hook Form's useWatch (or equivalent) to observe form field values in child components instead of setting dynamic form items at a high level, which causes unnecessary re-renders of the entire form tree.
In E2E tests, use a retry-with-timeout pattern (poll until a condition is met within a deadline) instead of a fixed sleep, so tests are both faster on success and resilient to timing variation.
Do not embed domain-specific logic (e.g., HTML escaping for files) in a generic controller that serves multiple resource types; instead, place that logic in the appropriate domain service layer.
Before reading a stream fully into memory, check or limit its size to prevent out-of-memory kills from unexpectedly large payloads.

Before adding a new configuration flag, search for existing flags with similar intent; if one exists, extend or properly deprecate it with release notes and a removal timeline rather than creating a near-duplicate.
When replacing a configuration flag, add a release note announcing the replacement, document the migration path, and schedule removal for the next minor release so customers have time to migrate.

When adding a new variant of an existing configuration or test target, follow the established naming convention to keep the system predictable (e.g., if standard has a vcluster variant, the new target should too).
After modifying API dependencies or package versions, run the project's code-generation or update scripts to ensure all downstream package.json files and generated types stay in sync.

In E2E tests, verify the absence of an element by first confirming the page has fully loaded and then asserting the element is not present, rather than using a wait-to-not-see timeout which is slow and unreliable.
Use a shared noop utility function instead of inline empty arrow functions for default or placeholder callbacks to improve readability and avoid creating new function instances on every render.
Expose service endpoints through the shared API package (@domino/api) rather than making ad-hoc HTTP calls from individual components.
When introducing random delays or jitter in loops that process many items, calculate the aggregate worst-case time to ensure it remains acceptable at production scale.
Name methods, classes, and variables to accurately describe their actual behavior, not a legacy or loosely related concept.
When iterating over a collection to perform side effects (e.g., publishing events), catch errors per item inside the loop so that a failure for one item does not prevent processing the rest.
Long-lived objects such as actors or background workers must have an explicit cleanup or shutdown mechanism to prevent memory leaks, rather than relying on implicit garbage collection.
In E2E tests, navigate to a new URL within the existing browser session instead of opening a new browser window, unless the test specifically requires a fresh session.
In E2E tests, add an explicit wait or assertion step (e.g., 'I wait to see' or 'I wait to click') before interacting with UI elements to avoid race conditions from asynchronous rendering.
Use clearly distinct variable and directory names in tests to avoid confusion when the same value is reused in different contexts, even if it is technically correct.
When modifying a shared or reusable component, verify that the change does not break or degrade other views that consume the same component.
When rendering data that is fetched asynchronously, guard against undefined or loading states to prevent displaying 'undefined' strings in the UI.
Use existing global utility functions (e.g., getAppName) instead of reimplementing the same logic inline to ensure consistency and reduce duplication.
Components should not rely on knowing the internal implementation details of callback props (e.g., assuming a callback will never throw) when determining control flow such as success/error feedback.
Never use the `any` type in TypeScript; use the actual data type, a specific interface, or `unknown` when the type is not known.
Remove state variables (and their setters) that are declared but never read, as they add unnecessary complexity and can mislead future readers.
Assign safe default values (e.g., empty array `[]`) to props or destructured parameters that may be undefined according to their type definition.
Do not reset submitting or loading flags until all dependent async operations (including data refetches) have completed, to prevent users from operating on stale data.
Use feature flags (FF) for short-lived, temporary behavioral changes and configuration constants (CC) for permanent configuration choices.
Use a consistent approach for constructing API base URLs across the codebase (e.g., always use `window.location.origin` or never use it) rather than mixing patterns.
Use consistent term ordering and phrasing in test step definitions so that steps for similar concepts are predictable and discoverable.
Keep terminology in code (function names, variable names, test steps) aligned with the current product naming conventions, updating references when product terms change.
Before creating new mock data or test fixtures, check for existing mock generators and data factories in the codebase and reuse them to avoid duplication.
Do not mix API version prefixes (e.g., v1 and v4) in the same endpoint path; keep versioning consistent within each API surface.
Prefer spec-first API design (manually defining the OpenAPI/Swagger YAML) over code-first generation, as it makes code reviews easier and provides an extra layer of validation.
Design API endpoints using RESTful resource hierarchy patterns (collection/id/collection) and model capabilities as subresources rather than ad-hoc endpoints.
Prefer classes over singleton objects for service-like components so they can be easily mocked and tested in unit tests.
Place each class or major type definition in its own file, named after the class, to improve discoverability and keep files small.
Name modules and packages with descriptive terms that convey their intent (e.g., 'utils' or 'commons') rather than generic names like 'lib'.

REST API endpoints returning collections must use a consistent wrapper format with totalCount, paginationDetails, and items array, even when pagination is not needed, so all Domino APIs share the same structure for consumers.
API clients with rate limiting must be injected in singleton scope so all threads share the same instance and respect the rate limit collectively.
When introducing deprecated function replacements, create a follow-up ticket to remove usages of the deprecated functions in a future release cycle.
Do not import components from another team's or domain's section directory; instead, extract shared functionality into a common reusable package to avoid unintended coupling and breaking changes.
Do not import from internal or transitive dependencies that are not explicitly listed in the package's package.json; create custom types or add the dependency explicitly instead.
When adding new API calls to a rate-limited external service, evaluate whether other services or pods share the same rate limit and could cause contention.
API filter parameters should default to returning all results (no filter) when no filter value is specified, rather than requiring explicit opt-in to see everything.
Access other modules' services through adapter interfaces rather than taking direct dependencies on their internal implementations, preserving module boundary separation.
Extract utility functions and helper logic out of component files into dedicated utils files to keep components focused and helpers reusable.
Use existing authorization methods (e.g., canRetrieveProject) rather than implementing custom permission checks, to ensure consistent authorization rules across endpoints.
Access authorization through service-layer APIs that bundle it with data retrieval, rather than calling lower-level authorizers directly, to ensure consistent permission enforcement.
When converting class components to functional components, wrap event handler functions in useCallback to prevent unnecessary re-renders of child components.
Use established utility functions like Ramda's pick for selecting a subset of object properties instead of writing manual property extraction logic.
Wrap expensive or derived computations in useMemo to avoid recalculating on every render when the inputs have not changed.
Always include the JIRA ticket number in TODO comments so they remain traceable and do not become orphaned technical debt.
When multiple functions perform related but distinct operations, their names must clearly convey the difference to avoid confusion for other developers.
Return a spinner or empty placeholder while async data is loading rather than rendering the component with incomplete or undefined data.
Do not mix analytics or tracking logic directly inside UI components; extract it into a dedicated hook or utility function to avoid duplication and keep components focused on rendering.
Extract each React component into its own file rather than defining multiple components in a single file.
Avoid using mutable object references as useEffect dependencies; extract the specific primitive value you depend on into a variable and use that as the dependency instead.
Do not use template literal syntax when there is no string interpolation; use a plain string or direct function call instead.
Split E2E test specifications into multiple files when cumulative wait times and execution durations risk exceeding CI timeout limits.
Use full terms or define abbreviations on first use in test descriptions and feature files; do not assume readers know domain-specific acronyms.
Use idiomatic empty collection expressions (e.g., List.empty or Nil in Scala) instead of constructing empty instances manually.
Represent fixed sets of operation types (CREATE, UPDATE, DELETE, etc.) as enums rather than raw strings to get compile-time safety and exhaustiveness checks.
When a query method filters out certain records (e.g., archived items), make the filtering behavior explicit in the method name to avoid ambiguity.
Extract shared query filter clauses (e.g., isArchived checks) into reusable variables and compose them with query-specific criteria to avoid duplicating filter logic across methods.

In E2E tests, use explicit wait-then-act steps (e.g., wait to click) after any action that changes the visible page content, rather than immediately interacting with elements that may not yet be rendered.
Remove redundant or orphaned code comments that no longer serve a purpose rather than leaving them in place.
Delete unused code rather than leaving it in the codebase for potential future use; if follow-up is planned, reference a ticket in a comment.
Verify that test fixtures, hooks, and framework callbacks actually execute at the expected lifecycle phase; unused teardown logic that never runs is a silent bug.
When adding retry logic, filter on specific exception types or messages rather than catching all exceptions, to avoid masking real intermittent failures.
Session-scoped pytest fixtures must have unique names across the test suite; duplicate names with different implementations cause unpredictable behavior across modules.
Do not add ts-ignore or ts-expect-error directives when the type error can be resolved by providing the correct arguments or types.
Before creating a new component or utility, check if an existing one already provides the needed functionality, even if minor adaptation is required.
When updating shared configuration values like image tags, ensure the same value is updated in all repositories and charts that reference it to maintain congruence.
Use unit tests instead of end-to-end system tests when the behavior can be verified without exercising the full application, as e2e tests are expensive to run and maintain.
Keep PRs focused on a single concern; tooling, scripts, and infrastructure changes should be in a separate PR from feature or bug fix changes for clearer change tracking.

Services should have clear responsibility boundaries; avoid adding methods that bypass the service's core abstraction layer (e.g., direct DB queries in a search service meant to abstract over Elasticsearch).
Use string interpolation instead of string concatenation for constructing strings with dynamic values, as it improves readability.
Every BDD/Gherkin scenario must include explicit assertion steps using 'Then' keywords that verify expected outcomes, not just action steps using 'And' or 'When'.
In BDD scenarios, clicking a button or performing a UI action is a 'When' progression step, not an assertion; use 'Then' steps to verify expected text or state after actions.
When a legacy template system (e.g., Scala HTML templates) is being phased out, avoid adding new content to it; instead, place new content in the replacement system (e.g., React components).
Use the CSS `gap` property for spacing between flex/grid children instead of margin-based approaches, and consider adding a linting rule to enforce consistent gap usage.
Do not wrap JSX in a React Fragment (<>...</> or <Fragment>...</Fragment>) when there is only a single child element; return the child directly.
Use existing shared utility functions and hooks (e.g., date formatting helpers like `getFromNowString` from `useDate`) instead of reimplementing the same logic in new components.

When a client needs both a list of entities and a related aggregate (e.g., total count), return them together in a single API response rather than requiring a separate call to the service and database.
API list endpoints should default to sorting by creation timestamp in descending order (newest first) rather than having no default sort, providing a predictable and useful ordering out of the box.
When a function returns multiple related values, define a named type or case class instead of returning a tuple, because positional access (e.g., `._1`) harms readability.

Avoid using numeric indexes to select UI elements in E2E tests; instead, use stable selectors (data-testid, ARIA roles, or meaningful text) and file UI tickets to improve element testability when needed.

Tag E2E feature files and scenarios with component tags (e.g., @projects, @dfs, @workspace) and team tags (e.g., @team-develop) so tests can be triggered selectively by PR scope and owned by the responsible team.
Prefer unit tests and API-level system tests over expensive E2E/UI tests for backend logic like permissions; reserve E2E tests for scenarios that genuinely require browser-level validation.
Name test helper functions that verify conditions with an `assert_` prefix so their intent is immediately clear.
Extract resource creation and setup logic out of test functions into separate helper functions to keep tests focused on assertions.

A microservice's local config file should only contain settings required to bootstrap the service; all other settings must be defined in the parent platform (nucleus) so they can be overridden via central config.
Include the exception type or error category in error/log messages so that different failure modes can be distinguished from each other.
Name configuration settings and variables to clearly indicate which domain entity they belong to, preventing ambiguity with similarly named concepts elsewhere.
Rely on the framework's built-in error reporting (e.g., exceptions with key names) instead of writing verbose manual detection code for missing configuration keys.
When naming default values, include the entity they apply to so readers do not mistake them for generic or service-level defaults.
Do not hardcode values that may need to change; extract them into configuration settings so they can be adjusted without code changes.
Name boolean flags with general, intent-based names like `isEnabled` rather than implementation-specific names like `isInScope` that leak internal details.
Error messages should clearly describe what went wrong using complete, human-readable sentences rather than terse or ambiguous phrasing.
When adding new parameters to existing methods, provide default values to maintain backward compatibility so existing callers are not broken.
Default container image references should point to the organization's own registry (e.g., quay.io/domino/) rather than upstream third-party images.
Replace magic numbers (especially z-index values, pixel sizes, and thresholds) with named constants, and add a comment explaining why the specific value was chosen.
Method names should accurately reflect their actual scope and behavior; if a function only works for a specific provider or context, the name should indicate that restriction.

Do not catch exceptions only to rethrow them without adding context or handling; either handle the exception meaningfully, wrap it in the appropriate type (e.g., Left for Either), or let it propagate naturally.
When multiple test scenarios share identical setup steps, extract those steps into a shared fixture or Background block to reduce duplication and simplify maintenance.
When tests involve asynchronous operations like deletion or state transitions, consider potential race conditions and order operations to minimize timing-dependent failures.
Break tests that validate multiple independent behaviors into separate test cases so that failures can be isolated and one failing assertion does not mask others.
Tag test scenarios with the owning team's label (e.g., @team-data) so that test failures can be quickly routed to the responsible team.
Error messages must accurately describe the specific operation that failed; do not copy-paste error messages between handlers without updating them to reflect the actual context.
When authorization is deliberately delegated to another layer (e.g., a proxy), add a code comment explaining the delegation and why the current layer does not perform the full check.
Use union types or enums to constrain string attributes (such as ARIA roles) to their set of valid values, preventing typos and invalid usage at compile time.
Use java.time.Instant for timestamp fields rather than DateTime or other date-time types to ensure consistency and precision across the codebase.
Create shared, reusable test data factory functions in a common test utilities module instead of duplicating test object construction across individual test files.
Only allow service or superuser accounts to perform actions on behalf of other users; regular user requests must always use the authenticated principal's own identity to prevent impersonation risks.

Name error types and utility classes based on their general scope of applicability, not after the specific feature or module where they were first introduced, so they can be reused across the codebase.

Prefer positive test assertions that verify expected behavior over negation tests that wait for something to not happen, as negation tests incur timeout costs on every run and may pass incorrectly if a different failure occurs.
When combining multiple test cases into a single scenario, add section comments that label what each block verifies so readers can quickly understand the test structure.
Avoid installing packages from upstream repositories during every e2e test run; prefer pre-built Docker images or cached environments to prevent flakiness from upstream outages or breaking changes.

Use dedicated data-test attributes with descriptive, component-scoped names (e.g., data-test="billing-tag-add-button") as stable selectors for tests instead of relying on CSS classes or DOM structure.
Include key entity identifiers (such as names, titles, and IDs) in log messages to make debugging easier when scanning logs for specific items.
Name variables and parameters to accurately reflect the data they contain; do not name a field userIds if it holds organization IDs, as misleading names cause bugs and confusion.
Never use a user ID where an organization ID is expected or vice versa; mixing identity types across domain boundaries introduces authorization bugs and security vulnerabilities.
Service adapter classes that wrap external APIs or infrastructure should be declared as singletons to avoid unnecessary instantiation and ensure consistent state.
When multiple tests create the same domain objects, extract shared test data fixtures into a reusable TestUtil or test helper file rather than duplicating construction logic across test classes.

When using dictionary lookups for required mappings (e.g., test IDs, configuration keys), omit default values so that missing entries cause immediate failures rather than silently proceeding with incorrect data.

Delete operations on persisted entities should set an archived or soft-delete flag rather than performing hard deletes, and reviewers should verify the flag is actually being set in the implementation.
Test cleanup code (stopping services, deleting resources) must be placed in a try/finally block so that cleanup runs even when the test fails, preventing resource leaks in CI.

When managing pagination, sorting, or filter state that triggers data fetching, ensure that state changes during an in-flight request are not dropped or ignored -- do not gate state updates on a loading flag.

State variables should be named to accurately reflect all the data they contain; a state object holding pagination, sorting, and filter state should not be named just 'pagination' as it misleads readers about its scope.

Do not force-fit a complex abstraction (like a generic data-fetching hook) when the resulting workarounds outweigh the benefits; prefer simpler, direct implementations when the abstraction adds more complexity than value.
Do not commit empty or placeholder files (e.g., empty test files, scratch files) to the repository; remove them before merging.

When an API uses non-obvious parameter encodings (e.g., boolean true for ascending, false for descending, undefined for none), add an inline comment documenting the mapping so future readers do not have to guess.
Always verify that the rowKey property in table components is bound to a truly unique field to prevent rendering bugs and key collisions.
When a code change introduces a known follow-up task that is not blocking release, file a ticket immediately to ensure it is tracked and not forgotten.
Add unit tests for newly introduced or modified logic paths, especially when handling new data variants or edge cases.
Review IDE-generated import paths to ensure they follow the project's established import conventions before committing.
Reference defined constants instead of duplicating magic strings in test files to keep tests in sync with production code.
Define static configuration objects (such as table column definitions) outside the render path or memoize them to avoid unnecessary recomputation on every re-render.
Define dependency versions in a single variable and reference it everywhere to keep related tool versions synchronized and avoid drift.
Avoid using --break-system-packages for pip installs; use virtual environments instead to isolate Python dependencies from the system Python.
Install Python dependencies in virtual environments rather than at the system level to avoid implicit dependencies and ensure reproducibility.
Pass only the specific data needed (e.g., a user ID) rather than an entire rich object (e.g., AuthenticatedPrincipal) to reduce coupling and narrow the interface.
Always call throwIfNotAuthorized() (or the equivalent enforcement method) after performing a permission check to ensure authorization is actually enforced, not just evaluated.
Do not mark API fields as Optional when the enclosing context guarantees they will be present; align nullability with the actual domain invariants.
Use functional control flow (e.g., Either chaining or validated applicatives) to accumulate validation errors rather than throwing exceptions, so all field errors can be returned at once.
When storing or sending data to external services (e.g., Vault, databases), validate input length and size against the service's documented limits before making the call.
Use the framework's built-in query parameter binding mechanisms (e.g., QueryBindable in Play) instead of manually parsing query parameters in controllers.
Use existing domain utility methods (e.g., DominoId.fromString) for type conversions instead of reimplementing conversion logic inline.
Keep class and type names concise; avoid redundant qualifiers that duplicate the package namespace, and maintain consistent suffixes (e.g., Dto for API models) across the codebase.
When replacing system dependencies or tools (e.g., switching from wget to curl), verify that the new dependency is available in all supported environments to avoid backward incompatible changes.
Add section header comments in test scenarios to organize test steps into logical groups, making test failures easier to triage.
When fixing flaky or failing tests, ensure the fix does not reduce coverage or hide potential bugs in the system under test; prefer more specific test inputs over broadening acceptance criteria.
Only inject feature-specific configuration (volumes, env vars, config files) into runtime environments when the corresponding feature is enabled.
Before writing system-generated configuration files, consider whether users may have made their own modifications that should not be silently overwritten.
Follow the target language's established naming conventions (e.g., camelCase for Scala identifiers) consistently across the codebase.
Use early return (guard clause) patterns to exit functions early when preconditions are not met, instead of wrapping the entire function body in a conditional block.
Add comments explaining the rationale behind non-obvious priority or ordering logic in data structures like maps and lists, even if the code is somewhat self-documenting.
When extracting a new service to break cyclic dependencies, move all related operations (not just the new one) into the extracted service to maintain coherent responsibility boundaries.
Before creating a new enum or type definition, check for existing ones across modules to avoid duplication that will need later reconciliation.
Use an empty collection rather than Option[Collection] to represent the absence of items, as it simplifies consumer code and avoids nested unwrapping.
Variable names should clearly indicate their type; avoid names that sound like booleans (e.g., prefixed with 'is' or 'has') when they hold collections or other non-boolean values.
In RESTful APIs, name schema/DTO types as entities (e.g., 'NewComputeTier') not actions (e.g., 'ComputeTierCreation'), and ensure the name is appropriate for all operations that use it.
Use PATCH-based partial updates instead of PUT with full-object replacement to avoid concurrency issues where simultaneous requests overwrite each other's changes.
Trait fields that do not apply to all implementations should be declared as Optional rather than requiring implementations to use runtime exceptions (e.g., ???) as placeholders.
Search and index operations must handle archived or deleted entities gracefully (e.g., by removing stale index entries) and should include tests covering those states.
Use debug log level for routine, high-frequency operations like indexing individual records; reserve info level for significant state changes or events operators need to see.
Bulk data operations (e.g., reindexing all records) should use streaming or pagination rather than loading all entities into memory at once, to avoid memory issues at scale.
Disabled UI elements should display a message explaining why they are disabled and what the user can do to resolve the situation, enabling self-service without contacting support.
TODO comments in code must reference a tracking ticket or issue so that the technical debt is visible and prioritizable in the project tracker.

Variable names should accurately reflect the semantic state of the data they hold (e.g., 'mergedUser' for data that has been merged from multiple sources, not a generic name like 'user' or a misleading name like 'idpUser').
When a function only applies to a subset of input types, explicitly restrict or throw on unsupported types rather than silently returning nothing.
When a parameter is not actually used in a method's implementation, remove it from the method signature all the way through the call chain rather than leaving dead parameters.
Error messages that may be displayed in the UI should be written in clear, user-friendly language that describes both the problem and possible cause.
When fetching collections to sort and take a subset, push the sorting and limiting logic to the database query rather than loading all records into application memory.
When a method executes one query per item in a list (N+1 pattern), replace it with a batch query or join to avoid performance degradation as the list grows.
When fields are effectively required for an operation, make them non-optional in the type signature rather than leaving them as Option types that obscure which fields are truly required.
When a data model allows optional fields for flexibility but an operation requires them, validate and throw at the operation entry point rather than silently proceeding with missing data.
When code relies on an implicit assumption that may change in the future (e.g., only one active instance), add a comment documenting the assumption and what would need to change.
When a precondition (such as resolving an ID) must succeed for the operation to be meaningful, throw an error instead of logging a warning and continuing with incomplete data.
Push sorting, limiting, and pagination logic into database queries instead of fetching all records and processing in application memory, especially for endpoints that serve growing datasets.
When new behavior is introduced that was previously disallowed (e.g., starting an older version stops a newer one), add explicit tests that codify and document this newly permitted behavior.
When creating a versioned record, resolve all runtime-dependent fields (e.g., environment ID, commit ID) at creation time and persist the resolved values to ensure reproducibility.
Do not add validation checks that guard against states which are technically valid and do not affect correctness, as they add maintenance burden and complexity without meaningful safety benefit.

API documentation should describe behavior abstractly rather than referencing current implementation details (such as specific upsert keys or internal identifiers) that may change as the system evolves.
PATCH endpoint descriptions should concisely state what happens to absent fields and call out any non-standard merge semantics (such as upsert behavior for nested collections).
Method and function arguments in Scala should start with a lowercase letter, following standard Scala naming conventions.
When transformation logic is only used in one place, prefer keeping it inline rather than extracting it into a separate method, to reduce indirection and keep the code easier to follow.
When changing a DTO field from required to optional, verify backward compatibility with existing consumers by running end-to-end tests before merging.
When creating an actor system, always register a shutdown hook to ensure graceful shutdown, following the established pattern used elsewhere in the codebase.
Internal implementation details such as API client instances and invokers should be declared as private to prevent unintended external coupling.
Move feature flag checks and expensive service calls inside the branch that actually needs them, so they are skipped when the result would be unused (e.g., when a list is empty).
When an API schema marks fields as required, do not silently map missing values to empty strings or defaults; either ensure the values are present or make the schema reflect the actual optionality.
When a dependency call is wrapped in NonFatal exception handling, add a test that verifies the caller degrades gracefully (e.g., returns a default value) when the dependency throws.
Use None to represent 'unknown/legacy/not-applicable' and Some(Seq.empty) to represent 'intentionally empty' when modeling optional collection fields, and document the semantic distinction.

Utility and resolver methods should handle rare edge cases (such as null returns for empty repositories) defensively, especially if they may be reused in broader contexts beyond their original caller.
Log a warning when the system encounters an unexpected but non-fatal state (such as an archived project being resolved at runtime) to aid debugging and provide visibility into edge cases.
Keep docstrings focused on the module where they live; do not reference cross-module implementation details (such as callers from other packages) in interface-level documentation.
Throw an exception for supposedly impossible cases instead of silently skipping them, so broken assumptions surface immediately at runtime.
Docstrings in interface/API modules should describe the contract, not reference low-level implementation details or types from other modules.
Verify end-to-end behaviors like query parameter propagation through integration or e2e tests when unit tests cannot exercise the full request path.
Log warnings only for actual service failures, not for legitimately empty results, so operators can distinguish between 'no data found' and 'upstream call failed'.
When passing a potentially large list of IDs to a database $in operator, verify query size limits and ensure the target field is indexed for performance.
Use 'sortBy' rather than 'sortField' as the query parameter name for sort operations in REST APIs for clarity and consistency.
Keep status enums for durable lifecycle states (active, expired, revoked) and represent transient or computed conditions like 'expiring soon' as separate boolean fields.
Always include a human-readable name alongside entity IDs in audit trail events so that audit logs are meaningful without requiring additional lookups.
When accessing a field assumed to always be present (e.g., via .get on an Option), add defensive handling or document the invariant, since 'always present' assumptions can silently break at runtime.
When introducing temporary code or workarounds, add a comment with the follow-up ticket number so the cleanup does not get lost.
Log messages emitted on a recurring schedule (e.g., every few seconds) should use debug log level, not info or higher, to avoid noisy production logs.
Use a structured logger with an appropriate log level instead of println or stdout for diagnostic messages; println writes unconditionally and creates noisy logs.
When a security-sensitive behavior (e.g., disabling SSL verification) is conditionally applied, add a unit test that verifies the behavior is only active for the intended case.
Never leave SSL verification disabled without a tracked ticket for re-enabling it; disabling SSL opens the door to man-in-the-middle attacks and data exposure.
When removing a behavior or skipping a code path (e.g., disabling a connection check), add a regression test that verifies the old behavior does not resurface.
When a conditional check encodes domain knowledge that is not self-evident from the code, add a comment explaining the business reason and its relationship to the broader system.
Do not use `object` as a TypeScript type; instead, define an interface or type alias with the minimum set of required attributes.
Each PR should contain only changes related to its stated purpose; unrelated cleanup or refactoring should be split into a separate PR for clarity and reviewability.

When naming configuration properties or variables, prefer specific names that identify exactly what the value represents rather than vague umbrella terms that could refer to multiple things in the same domain.
Do not infer the URL scheme (HTTP vs HTTPS) from the port number; instead, store and propagate the scheme explicitly in configuration.
When constructing URLs by concatenating a base URL and a path, normalize the result to prevent malformed URLs with double slashes or other artifacts.
Extract repeated literal values in tests (such as URLs, IDs, or config strings) into named fixture attributes for reuse and maintainability.
Test files should have the same code ownership (CODEOWNERS) as the source files they test, so the owning team is notified of changes.
Name boolean parameters and flags with a verb prefix (e.g., `useJson`, `isEnabled`, `shouldRetry`) to make their intent immediately clear at call sites.
Do not add dependency injection annotations (such as @Named) unless they are required to disambiguate bindings; unnecessary annotations add noise and mislead readers.
Pass only the specific values a class needs (e.g., a boolean flag) rather than injecting an entire settings or configuration object, to reduce coupling and simplify testing.

Do not use environment-specific values (such as internal AWS account IDs) as hardcoded fallback defaults in application code, because they mask real configuration propagation failures in other environments.
When the same conditional check (e.g., feature-flag gating) appears in multiple places, extract it into a shared helper method to reduce duplication and ensure consistency.
Remove or reduce log statements that produce excessive output; verbose logging in hot paths degrades signal-to-noise ratio and can impact performance.
Keep log messages concise by abbreviating well-known terms (e.g., 'repo' instead of 'repository'), especially when the message will include long values like URIs.

When adding a feature flag or central config value, also guard the corresponding API endpoints in the same PR rather than leaving them unprotected; the exception thrown should indicate that the feature could be enabled but is not currently active.
When a feature is gated behind a config flag, add a unit test that verifies the correct behavior when the flag is disabled, not just when it is enabled.

Do not introduce direct dependencies from implementation modules to the monolith server package; use adapter classes or named injection to keep implementation code decoupled and preserve the ability to eventually remove monolith dependencies.

When adding a new configuration value, always provide a sensible default fallback in the application code and add the value to deployment chart configs; a missing config value should not cause the application to crash with an opaque error.

Mark deprecated code paths with WARN-level log messages that explain the deprecation, so that anyone encountering the log in production knows to investigate and can help confirm whether the path is still in use.

When objects are being drilled through several layers of classes to reach their point of use, prefer a dependency injection framework (e.g., Guice) over manual parameter passing to keep constructors clean and avoid coupling between layers.
Place configuration settings in the component that owns the decision they control, not in lower-level classes that merely execute the behavior.
Write test names that clearly describe the scenario and expected behavior, reading as a complete specification of what is being tested.
When a service restart triggers many concurrent operations (e.g., spawning actors for all active entities), stagger or jitter the initialization to avoid a thundering herd that spikes resource usage.
When renaming a class, update all related names (events, messages, methods, variables) that reference the old name to maintain naming consistency across the codebase.
Make behavioral constants (e.g., batch sizes, delays, thresholds) configurable rather than hardcoded, so that risky changes can be reverted via configuration without requiring a full code rollback.
When binding service classes in a Guice injection module, scope them as Singletons to avoid creating multiple instances of stateful or expensive service objects.

Use semantically correct HTTP status codes in API responses -- return 201 for resource creation and 200 for retrieval or updates -- so that clients can distinguish the type of operation that occurred.

Shared resources like ActorSystem should be injected via dependency injection rather than instantiated within individual classes, to avoid creating redundant instances and ensure proper lifecycle management.

Internal service-to-service callbacks and endpoints should be defined on internal API surfaces, not exposed through public APIs, to maintain clear API boundaries and avoid leaking implementation details.
Question and minimize dependencies between modules in build files; a module depending on 'server' or other large upstream modules may indicate a leaky abstraction that should be refactored.
Use a consistent version pinning strategy (e.g., caret ranges like ^29.7.0 vs exact pins) across all dependencies in a package manifest to avoid confusion about update behavior.
Name parameters using precise domain terminology; do not call a URL path segment 'url' when it is only a path component.
When wrapping or mirroring a well-known library's functionality, use the same parameter names that library uses so callers can transfer existing knowledge.
Never silently replace or override user-provided values; at minimum emit a warning so future developers can diagnose unexpected behavior.
When concatenating base URLs with path segments, normalize trailing and leading slashes to prevent subtle double-slash or missing-slash bugs.
Error messages should include the specific entity name, identifier, and relevant counts so operators can diagnose issues without additional lookups.
Do not wrap synchronous operations inside Future or async blocks when only the return type needs to be async; keep synchronous work outside the async boundary.
When querying paginated APIs, limit the page size to the minimum number of records needed rather than fetching default or maximum page sizes.
Do not use abbreviations that collide with existing domain terms; spell out or choose a distinct short form to prevent confusion.
Derive configuration values from existing data or context rather than adding new parameters; reduce the configuration surface area.
Replace hardcoded sleep calls with condition-based polling or explicit state checks, since sleeps provide no guarantee the awaited condition is met.
Use context managers (with-blocks) when temporarily changing state (e.g., window size, config overrides) so the original state is always restored, even on failure.
Rebase or merge from the base branch before pushing to ensure your PR does not accidentally revert changes recently landed by other engineers.
Keep UI state as close as possible to the component that uses it to avoid unnecessary prop drilling and reduce coupling between components.
Include units or type context in variable names (e.g., widthPx, timeoutMs) instead of relying on comments to convey that information.

Move static arrays, objects, and configuration data outside React components and use TypeScript's `as const` or explicit type annotations for better type inference and to avoid unnecessary re-creation on each render.

Use Ant Design's `Form.useWatch` hook instead of `formInstance.getFieldsValue()` to reactively track form field values, since `getFieldsValue()` is not reactive and will not trigger re-renders when values change.

Avoid the "previous value + useEffect" pattern for tracking state changes in React; instead follow the React team's recommended approach of computing derived values during rendering or using event handlers.
When modifying shared/base components (e.g., a common Modal wrapper), verify that the change does not break or visually alter existing consumers of that component across the codebase.
Before adding or re-adding code, check whether concurrent or recently merged PRs have intentionally removed or modified the same code to avoid reverting others' changes.
When tracking previous values or derived state, always update the tracked value unconditionally rather than gating the update behind a condition, to prevent stale state and logic bugs.
Ensure CODEOWNERS entries for test files and supporting code match the team that owns the production code being tested, so that the correct team reviews changes.

In switch statements or if-else chains, explicitly match all known cases rather than using a meaningful value as the default/fallback; return the raw value or throw for truly unknown cases to avoid silently masking unexpected inputs.
Keep PRs focused on the task at hand; avoid including unrelated reformatting or cosmetic changes that make the diff harder to review and may require additional reviewers.
When introducing a new function or API endpoint, add corresponding tests to verify its behavior rather than relying solely on tests for the old code path.
When writing files to a directory path, verify that the parent directory exists or will be created before the write operation to avoid runtime failures in edge cases.
Structure boolean expressions with the positive/enabling condition first to improve readability.
Add unit tests for functions containing complex filter or predicate logic, especially when the correctness of negation or matching is non-obvious.
Keep Scaladoc and API doc comments updated to accurately describe the current behavior and parameters of public service methods.
When upgrading a dependency to get new behavior, ensure the pinned version actually includes the feature you need by checking the dependency's changelog or release notes.
If a function parameter is always passed the same constant value at every call site, remove the parameter and inline the constant.
Extract repeated literal values in tests (such as IDs, strings, or configuration) into named variables or constants for reuse and clarity.
When fixing a styling issue that affects multiple instances of a component, apply the fix in the shared component definition rather than patching individual usages.
Use descriptive, user-facing aria-label values that accurately name the element for screen readers; do not repurpose aria-label for test identification or use generic values like 'test'.
Before removing or replacing data-test attributes or test selectors, verify that no existing end-to-end or integration tests depend on them.
Use existing shared library utilities (e.g., domino-pytk) for common operations like resource fetching and data traversal instead of writing custom implementations.
When querying for resources, use the most specific identifier available (e.g., execution ID) rather than broad identifiers (e.g., project name) to avoid ambiguity when multiple resources exist.
Remove commented-out code rather than leaving it in the codebase; if the code is not used, delete it entirely.
Do not assume build artifact directories (e.g., /dist) will always exist when configuring package links; validate or document the build dependency chain.
When injecting helpers or dependencies via dynamic module loading, evaluate whether race conditions can occur between modules loading and the helpers becoming available.
Never import mock or test utilities in production code files; this causes build issues and increases bundle size. Extract shared logic into a utility in a shared package instead.
Prefer defining default values via destructuring assignment in component props rather than using wrapper utilities like withDefaultProps.
Wrap function definitions passed as props or used in dependency arrays in React.useCallback() to prevent unnecessary re-creation on every render.

When the same TypeScript type is needed across packages, define it once in a shared package and import it rather than duplicating the definition. Prefer interface with extends over type aliases for extensibility.
Always add explicit error handling around API calls in frontend code so failures are caught and surfaced to users rather than silently ignored.
Lambda functions should be lightweight routing layers; push complex logic (config processing, branching) into downstream services or build steps that can be tested independently.
Distinguish between null and empty collections (e.g., Seq.empty) in Scala; they are not equivalent, and confusing them causes incorrect mock behavior and subtle bugs.
Use human-readable, screen-reader-friendly names for aria-label values (e.g., 'chart metric selector') rather than dash-separated ID-like strings (e.g., 'chart-metric-selector').
Place commonly-used fields for filtering and sorting at the top level of a domain model, and nest type-specific definitions in dedicated sub-fields, to enable efficient querying and pagination.
Use domain-level identifiers (e.g., DominoId) in service interfaces and keep persistence-specific details (e.g., MongoDB ObjectId) confined to the persistence implementation layer.
Remove annotations (such as @unused) that are no longer applicable after code changes to avoid misleading future readers.
Write Storybook stories using CSF3 format instead of deprecated MDX format to stay current with the framework and avoid migration debt.
Import from lodash-es instead of lodash to enable tree-shaking and reduce bundle size in ES module-based builds.
Name functions to accurately reflect all behaviors they perform; if a function both applies and removes tags, either rename it or split it into separate single-responsibility functions.
Do not add partial or poorly-implemented HATEOAS fields (like URI links) to API responses; remove unused API fields and add them properly when there is a concrete consumer need.

Create endpoints should produce deterministic results for the same payload; prefer upfront validation over silently skipping items, so two identical requests yield identical resources rather than different outcomes.
The service layer should not reference REST API DTOs; returning API-specific types from service methods couples all internal consumers to the API definition and propagates changes unnecessarily.
Place dependency injection bindings in the module they belong to (e.g., template bindings in TemplatesInjectionModule) rather than in a parent or sibling module to maintain cohesion.
Add log statements to new or modified operations so that behavior is observable in production.
When a file contains functions for multiple subsystems, include the subsystem name in each function name to make the scope obvious at the call site.
When a function's arguments and return types do not make its scope obvious, prefix the function name with the domain or subsystem it belongs to.
Remove all console.log statements before merging; use a proper logging framework instead of ad-hoc console output.
Review and remove TODO comments that are no longer valid before merging to prevent confusion for future readers.
Do not commit empty files; remove them if the contents have been moved or are no longer needed.
Place new tests in a dedicated test module rather than appending them to an unrelated test file, especially when they test a distinct feature area.
Variable and parameter names should accurately describe the value they hold; rename misleading identifiers (e.g., use 'env_image' instead of 'env_uri' when the value is a container image reference).
Functions should return their results rather than accepting mutable containers as parameters to be filled in; callers should not need to pre-allocate empty data structures.
When multiple tests perform the same comparison logic, extract a shared assertion function that each test calls with its specific inputs.
Break long lines into multiple lines for readability; do not let single expressions stretch beyond the project's line-length convention.
Avoid using `object` as a TypeScript type; use a specific interface or type alias that describes the expected shape.
Do not ignore or suppress useEffect dependency array warnings; refactor the effect or memoize dependencies instead of disabling the lint rule.
Avoid using @ts-ignore in test files; fix the underlying type mismatch or add proper type declarations instead.
Separate unrelated changes (e.g., API client generation, library upgrades) into distinct PRs to keep each PR focused and easier to review and bisect in history.
When naming variables that reference external resources like S3 buckets, include enough context to disambiguate which specific resource is being referenced.
Name variables to reflect their lifecycle state (e.g., `persistedDeployment` instead of a generic name) to make the code's intent clearer at the call site.
Never catch all Throwables; catch only NonFatal exceptions to avoid swallowing fatal JVM errors like OutOfMemoryError.

When deleting resources that span multiple systems, delete the database record only after external resource cleanup (e.g., Kubernetes deletes) succeeds, so the record is preserved for retry if cleanup fails.
Use idiomatic Option combinators like filter, map, and getOrElse instead of verbose if/else chains when working with optional values in Scala.
When hardcoding values or using temporary implementations, add a TODO comment explaining what the correct source should be and link to a tracking issue if available.
Follow the Databricks Scala style guide for curly brace usage in multi-line expressions and control structures.
Never use `.get` on an Option in Scala; use pattern matching, `.getOrElse`, or `.fold` with an explicit error to handle the None case safely.
Add comments to non-obvious dependency injection module bindings so that readers can understand why a binding exists without tracing through the entire dependency graph.
In Scala, catch only NonFatal exceptions rather than catching all exceptions indiscriminately, to avoid swallowing fatal JVM errors like OutOfMemoryError.
Add trace-level logging to all client/integration methods, including the method name and all parameters, to aid debugging of cross-service calls.
Use library-provided case-insensitive matching methods (e.g., enumeratum's withNameInsensitiveOption) instead of manually converting strings to uppercase or lowercase for comparison.
Use getOrElse with an explicit error thrown for required values rather than silently proceeding with a missing or default value.
Use idiomatic Scala collection methods like isEmpty instead of manually checking collection size or comparing to empty values.
When wrapping exceptions in custom exception types, always pass the original exception as the cause parameter to the parent Exception constructor to preserve the full stack trace.
Custom exception classes should name the wrapped exception parameter 'cause' (not 'ex') and pass it to the parent Exception constructor so the JVM exception chain is properly maintained.
Avoid hardcoding behavior for a specific provider type in shared service layers; prefer generic, extensible designs that support multiple providers without code changes.
Method names should accurately reflect all behaviors the method performs; if a method both creates and updates, name it createOrUpdate rather than just create.

When logging exceptions, pass the full exception object as a parameter (which includes the stack trace) and write a descriptive log message about the operation context, rather than just logging ex.getMessage.
Name variables that hold configuration values to match or closely mirror the configuration key they are sourced from, so the mapping between code and config is immediately obvious.
Keep persistence logic (e.g., database saves) in a consistent location for all code paths (create, update, delete) within a service to avoid confusion about where state changes happen.
Use separate try-catch blocks for independent operations that require different rollback logic, rather than grouping them in a single try-catch where partial failure handling becomes ambiguous.

A service method for a create or update operation should encapsulate its full lifecycle (database persistence and external resource management) rather than splitting responsibilities across the controller and service layers.
When using try-catch-finally patterns, ensure that fatal errors are handled distinctly from non-fatal errors so that finally blocks do not execute unintended logic after catastrophic failures.
Do not place success-only logic in finally blocks; put it in the try block after the operation that must succeed, since finally runs regardless of whether an exception occurred.
Follow the project's adopted language style guide for syntax conventions such as curly brace usage, even when the code compiles without them.
When refactoring or restructuring code, verify that all existing tests are preserved or re-added rather than silently dropped.
When a method can return None/null for a resource lookup, decide explicitly whether to throw an exception or handle the absence gracefully rather than silently ignoring it.
When the logic for create and update operations diverges significantly, split them into separate methods rather than using a single method with conditional branches.
Apply retry policies consistently to all external service calls (create, update, delete), not just selectively to some operations.
When adding authentication or authorization wrappers to components, update existing tests to mock the auth layer to prevent unintended API calls during testing.
Before moving or deleting a file during refactoring, check whether it had unit tests and ensure those tests are migrated or updated accordingly.
In end-to-end and integration tests, poll on status changes instead of using fixed sleep/delay calls, which are flaky and waste time.
When adding a new dependency to a BUILD file or build configuration, be prepared to explain why it is needed; unnecessary dependencies increase build times and coupling.
Use existing utility functions (e.g., getAppName) instead of hardcoding or re-implementing values that already have a canonical source.
When implementing object copy, fork, or clone operations, ensure all relevant attributes are transferred -- review the source model field-by-field to avoid missing properties.
Use dedicated accessor methods (e.g., getTemplateType) to simplify handling of optional fields rather than manually unwrapping Option types.
In REST API models, make new fields optional to maintain backward compatibility with existing clients; plan to make them required in a future API version.
Validation logic that applies to all code paths (e.g., billing tag validation for all project creation scenarios) should be centralized rather than duplicated across different branches.
Ensure that exception types map to the correct HTTP status codes; IllegalArgumentException should produce a 400 (Bad Request), not a 500 (Internal Server Error).
Service methods that look up entities should return Optional/Option types and let the caller decide how to handle absence, rather than throwing exceptions internally.
When multiple async operations are independent of each other, execute them in parallel rather than awaiting them sequentially to reduce latency.
When adding a new feature path that parallels an existing one, ensure it has equivalent observability (counters, metrics, logging) as the original path.
Use more specific pattern matches (e.g., Some(value) instead of raw Option) to simplify downstream code and avoid unnecessary map/foreach calls.
When modifying shared utility code (e.g., error handling utilities), run the broader test suite to catch regressions in all dependent sections.
Include a unique scenario ID or test identifier in dynamically created test resource names (e.g., workspace names) to avoid collisions and improve traceability.

Avoid asserting exact values for environment-dependent quantities in tests; instead assert relative conditions (e.g., greater than a baseline) or check units to avoid brittle tests that break across different infrastructure configurations.
When removing a code step or block, also remove any associated comments that reference the removed code to avoid stale documentation.

Remove unnecessary interfaces from implementation classes when you want to prevent other services from directly injecting the implementation; force consumers to use the higher-level service layer instead.
Domain entities must not reference API DTOs or models; the API is one presentation layer and domain should not depend on it, as this creates a coupling in the wrong direction.
When a file contains only a single class, name the file after the class for discoverability and consistency.

Domain entities and validation logic should not depend on web framework classes (e.g., Play Forms); extract validations into a framework-agnostic layer to avoid coupling the domain to a specific web framework.

Domain entities should not contain JSON serialization mappings; serialization concerns belong in the infrastructure or presentation layer to avoid constraining the domain with a specific serialized format.
When different entity subtypes have distinct sets of fields, use polymorphism (separate subclasses) instead of making all fields optional on a single type; this enforces correctness at compile time.
Validation helpers should live in a separate file from domain entities, scoped to the package that uses them and marked as package-private to prevent misuse elsewhere.

Database persistence records should be clearly readable from code and decoupled from other layers (API DTOs, domain entities); sharing types between persistence and API layers creates hidden coupling where changes in one layer unexpectedly break the other.

Remove fields from an entity or record that do not apply to that entity's type; storing inapplicable fields (always None/null) creates confusion and violates the principle that data models should accurately represent their domain.

When communicating between services, use dedicated DTOs rather than sending internal domain representations to avoid coupling internal models with external contracts that may have different lifecycles.
Place API-facing DTOs and internal mapping DTOs (e.g., remote hub models) in separate packages so that backward compatibility implications are immediately clear from the package path.
Do not introduce code that has no current usages; add it in the PR where it is actually needed to keep changes minimal and reviewable.
Domain and service layers should never depend on API-layer DTOs; use domain entities instead to prevent unrelated API changes from rippling into core business logic.
When adding authorization checks, follow the established patterns used by similar methods in the codebase rather than inventing new approaches, to maintain consistency and reviewability.
When fixing a bug in one location, search for structurally similar code nearby that may have the same issue and fix all instances together.

Use aria-label attributes instead of custom testId attributes for element identification in tests, which simultaneously improves accessibility and eliminates the need for custom test step definitions.
Aria-label values should use full, descriptive words (not abbreviations or IDs) because they are read aloud by screen readers for visually impaired users.
Use dict.get() with a default value parameter to simplify conditional logic instead of checking for key existence separately.
In Python, prefer @dataclass (or NamedTuple) for simple data containers over custom classes with manual __init__ methods; keep data structures explicit and avoid hiding logic behind wrapper classes.
Avoid using singletons or global mutable state unless there is a clear justification; consider thread-safety and testing implications before introducing shared state.
In Python, use list comprehensions for transforming collections instead of imperative loops with manual list building, as they are more idiomatic and concise.
When a utility function is almost always invoked alongside the same side effect (e.g., logging), encapsulate that side effect inside the function to reduce repetitive call-site boilerplate.
When recording a failure or error condition, always emit a corresponding log statement so that failures are visible in logs, not just in internal data structures.
Before removing or renaming environment variables from shared CI scripts, check whether other repositories or projects depend on those variables.
In CI scripts, reference shared environment variables instead of hardcoding values so that changes propagate consistently across all consumers.
Write BDD/feature test descriptions as user stories (As a [role], I'd like to [action], So that [benefit]) to clarify intent and maintain a user-centric perspective.
Name API client classes to reflect the actual API version they communicate with, not an internal or legacy version, to avoid confusion.
Name test fixtures after the resource or data they provide (e.g., `git_credentials`) rather than using generic or action-oriented names.
Move complex inline expressions into named utility functions to improve readability and testability.
Replace magic numbers with named constants (e.g., `MAX_GOALS_SHOWN`) and reuse the constant wherever the same value appears.
Always await async function calls inside try-catch blocks; unawaited async calls will not have their errors caught by the surrounding try-catch.
Ensure that the test description accurately reflects what the test actually validates; if it claims to test a behavior change, the test must trigger that change and assert the result.
In React components, consider memoizing expensive computed values or derived data to prevent unnecessary recalculations on re-render.
Use standard or existing utility classes (e.g., AutoRecordMapperCodec) for serialization instead of writing manual mapping code.
When processing events that trigger repeated lookups of the same data, introduce a low-complexity cache to avoid multiplying database queries per event.
In end-to-end tests, use API calls rather than UI interactions for test data setup (e.g., creating data sources) to make tests faster and more reliable.
When a function or hook accepts several parameters, group them into a single options object to improve readability and reduce confusion at the call site.
Move utility functions that do not depend on component state or props outside the React component body to improve readability and separation of concerns.
Define related constant values as an enum (or a structured type map) rather than loose string literals to improve type safety and discoverability.

Instead of using useEffect to synchronize state with changing props (which can cause extra renders and missing-dependency warnings), use the React pattern of tracking the previous prop value and updating state during render.

When spreading rest props onto nested elements, verify that accessibility attributes like aria-label are not unintentionally duplicated across wrapper and child elements, which can confuse screen readers.
When a component depends on asynchronously loaded data (e.g., locale configuration), consider delaying the render until the data is available to avoid rendering in an incomplete or incorrect state.
Domain modules must not import or reference API/web layer models (e.g., DTOs); the dependency direction should be from API layer toward domain, not the reverse.
When the backend has structured data (e.g., git provider name), expose it as an explicit field in the API response rather than requiring clients to parse or derive it from other fields like URIs.
Avoid chaining multiple useEffects to trigger data refreshes; instead extract the fetch function and call it imperatively from the event handler that requires the refresh.
Use imperative reload functions instead of boolean reload flags that trigger useEffect chains, as flag-based patterns cause unnecessary re-renders and unmount/remount cycles.
Do not use state flags that unmount and remount components just to trigger a data refresh; show a loading indicator within the existing component instead.
Use existing shared test utility helpers (e.g., layoutTestUtils.checkBreadcrumbs from @domino/test-utils) instead of reimplementing common test assertions inline.
Do not use deprecated or removed concept names (e.g., 'detwirl') when naming new variables, functions, or components; use current domain terminology.
Use React route links (e.g., Link from react-router) instead of plain anchor tags for navigation within the React single-page application to avoid full page reloads.
Do not spread-clone an entire props object just to inject a single additional property; pass the original props through and add the extra prop individually.
Use kebab-case for data-test (data-testid) attribute values to maintain consistency across the codebase.
Each React component should be in its own file; do not export multiple components with similar interfaces but different implementations from the same file, as it creates confusion.
