Do not prefix internal component names with third-party library names (e.g., 'Ant') as it couples the component identity to the underlying library.
When a prop is optional and has no default value, do not assume it will exist; add a null/undefined check or provide a default value.
Add comments or improved logging to explain non-obvious control flow decisions, especially around retry logic, forced termination, or safety mechanisms.
When building endpoints or features, design them to support all known future use cases (e.g., multiple backing store types) rather than hard-coding for a single case.
Never hard-code the product name (e.g., 'Domino') in UI strings; always use the app name getter to support white labelling.
Before introducing a new constant, check if an existing one already serves the same purpose and adjust its value if needed rather than creating a duplicate.
When multiple match/case branches share the same structure, extract the differing parts into a tuple or data object and handle the common logic once to reduce duplication.
Do not add explicit imports for types already covered by a wildcard import in the same scope.
Use domain-level abstractions (e.g., DominoId.newInstance) instead of implementation-specific types (e.g., MongoDB ObjectId) outside of persistence code to maintain proper layering.
Module-scoped or session-scoped pytest fixtures that are shared across tests should be extracted into a separate conftest or fixture file rather than defined inside a specific test file.
Before writing custom test setup logic, check for existing helper functions (e.g., create_user()) that already encapsulate the required setup.
Tests should clean up resources they create (e.g., archive projects, delete temporary data) to avoid polluting the test environment for subsequent runs.
When a variable is an alias for another concept (e.g., selectedRevision is actually activeRevision), add a comment explaining the relationship to prevent confusion.
Review error messages and log strings for typos before committing, as these are user-facing or debugging-critical text.
Define static configuration objects like table column definitions outside the React component body to avoid recreating them on every re-render.
Formatting and display utility functions should gracefully handle undefined or null inputs, returning a sensible fallback instead of rendering 'Invalid date' or similar broken output.
Write BDD test step definitions in natural, readable language that clearly conveys the action and expected outcome without ambiguity.
Use generated enum types from API specifications instead of hardcoded string values with @ts-ignore comments.
Use shared, white-label-aware UI components (e.g., wait spinners, loaders) instead of custom or non-themed implementations to ensure consistent branding across deployments.
Do not commit commented-out code or commented-out tests; remove dead code before merging.
Do not leave commented-out test cases in test files; either fix them or remove them entirely.
Avoid unnecessary type assertions and casts; if the API typing is correct, use the types as-is rather than forcing casts.
Extract complex inline logic into named functions to improve readability and reusability.
Use rem units instead of px for margin and padding values to support consistent scaling and accessibility.
Pass function references directly as callbacks instead of wrapping them in unnecessary arrow functions.
When a service already returns the needed type, use it directly rather than adding unnecessary conversion methods.
Do not place internal service models in API model packages; keep non-API entities in the package where they are consumed (e.g., alongside the service interface).
Service method signatures should separate pagination, sorting, and filtering into distinct parameters rather than bundling them into a single ambiguous object.
Verify that API parameters (filters vs. sort orders) accurately reflect the product design and requirements before implementing them.
Use a consistent, short parameter name (e.g., `q`) for free-text search across all API endpoints; internally map it to a descriptive name like `searchQuery`.
Name binder or helper objects/functions after the parameter they actually bind, not after unrelated concepts.
Disable IDE 'align when multiline' formatting rules to avoid excessive whitespace that creates noisy diffs and makes side-by-side code review harder.
Name API filter parameters to match the corresponding domain model attribute names so the mapping is immediately obvious to consumers.
Replace bare `.get` calls on Option/nullable values with `getOrElse` throwing an `IllegalStateException` with a clear message describing the unexpected state, even for cases that should never occur.
The totalCount field in paginated API responses must reflect the total number of items matching the filter in the database, not just the current page size.
Move reusable test fixture creators and builders into shared test utility modules (e.g., testlib) so they can be used across multiple test suites.
Use domain-specific types (e.g., DominoId) instead of raw primitives (e.g., String) even when implicit conversions would make primitives compile, to preserve type safety and intent.
Only use Provider-based (lazy) dependency injection when resolving circular dependencies; use direct injection for all other cases.
Start with the narrowest API parameter type that satisfies current requirements (e.g., single value instead of list); widening later is a non-breaking change.
Keep test-only utilities, injection modules, and fixtures in test source directories rather than in production source sets.
When a class is hard to name because it represents multiple concerns, it is a signal the class should be split into separate classes with clear single responsibilities.
Do not perform expensive IO on every request for data that changes rarely; use caching or a lightweight staleness check to avoid unnecessary database load.
Replace N individual database queries with a single bulk query (e.g., using $in) and perform filtering or diffing in memory to reduce IO overhead.
When checking or updating multiple records, consolidate into a single bulk query rather than issuing separate database calls per item.
Name fields and functions after the domain concept they represent, not a loosely related concept; using the wrong domain term creates confusion for other developers.
Use strict equality (=== instead of ==) to avoid unexpected type coercion in JavaScript and template languages.
When commenting out code or disabling tests, include a reference to the tracking ticket (e.g., Jira link) explaining why the code is disabled and when it should be re-enabled.
Define magic numbers (such as page sizes) as named constants rather than inlining them, so the value is documented and easy to change in one place.
When the same value (e.g., page size) is used in multiple places, reference a single shared constant to ensure consistency and prevent drift.
Review interface and type names for typos before merging; a misspelled name that resembles an existing name creates confusing collisions and hinders code discoverability.
Do not import the same module twice, and prefer named imports over wildcard ("* as") imports in React/TypeScript files.
Always use strict equality (===) instead of loose equality (==) for comparisons in JavaScript/TypeScript.
Extract reusable utility functions and logic out of component files into dedicated utility modules to keep components focused on rendering.
Separate styled-component definitions with an empty line between them for visual clarity and readability.
When introducing a new configuration dimension (e.g., deployment flavor), ensure it is captured in observability and reporting data so results can be traced back to their source.
Assign boolean literals to descriptively named variables before passing them as function arguments instead of passing raw True/False values.
Always provide a default value for environment variable expansions in shell scripts (e.g., ${VAR:-default}) to prevent failures when the variable is unset.
When modifying shared test steps or code paths, verify the change works correctly in all environments and test configurations that use it, not just the one you are targeting.
Always surface API errors to the user; ensure that components consuming async data display error states from failed API calls.
When reading from a cache, handle the case where the expected entry is missing -- ensure the operation degrades gracefully and will be retried on the next cycle rather than failing silently.
Choose descriptive, self-documenting variable names that communicate purpose; avoid generic or abbreviated names that require additional context to understand.
Align and format complex expressions (e.g., chained calls, multi-line parameters) so they are easy to scan and understand at a glance.
When working with Option types in Scala, prefer pattern matching on the value rather than using `.isDefined` checks followed by `.get`, as it leads to safer and more readable code.
Use the default import syntax (`import React from 'react'`) rather than named or namespace imports for React, as modern React and bundler configurations support this simpler form.
Extract reusable helper logic out of component or route files into dedicated utils files to improve reusability and keep route/component files focused.
Use the React fragment shorthand syntax (`<>...</>`) instead of `<React.Fragment>` or wrapping divs when no key or attributes are needed.
When refactoring removes or changes the behavior of a function, rename it to accurately reflect the new behavior and avoid confusing future readers.

Avoid hardcoding magic number constants (e.g., pixel widths) in child components that parent components are unaware of; instead, derive UI values from explicit boolean or enum state managed at the parent level.
When an optional API field has a destructive side effect (e.g., omitting a field removes an existing value), document this behavior explicitly in the API docs and add unit tests to cover it.
Remove unused variables, imports, and code artifacts to keep the codebase clean and avoid confusion about what is actively used.
Permission-denied error messages should clearly state what the user cannot do and direct them to contact an administrator for resolution, using a consistent format across the application.

When adding behavior to a shared/base component for a targeted fix, prefer creating a wrapper component that composes the original rather than modifying the shared component directly, to limit blast radius.
Do not commit empty test files; either write meaningful tests or remove the test file until tests are ready to be implemented.
Use trace-level logging for high-frequency operational messages (e.g., per-message processing) so logs are not too verbose in production but can be enabled when debugging.
Use a structured retry policy with backoff and max attempts instead of bare infinite loops for resilience logic, to avoid unbounded resource consumption and provide better observability.
Message consumers and event processors must include exception handling to prevent unhandled errors from crashing the consumer loop or losing messages silently.
In Scala, place constants and static-like values in a companion object rather than in the class body to separate instance state from shared values.
Name variables and parameters after the domain concept they represent in the current service, not after terminology borrowed from other services or systems.
Add unit tests to illustrate expected inputs and outputs, especially for non-trivial processing logic, so reviewers and future developers can quickly understand the behavior.
When defining status enums, include an explicit error state and ensure the corresponding error message is propagated so consumers can understand what went wrong.
Prefer explicit boolean props or render props over using optional function props to conditionally show or hide UI elements, as optional function props obscure intent and reduce readability.
Use the same term for the same concept across all APIs and services to avoid confusion; do not introduce synonyms (e.g., 'authors' vs 'owners') for the same entity.
For non-paginated endpoints, set the internal query limit high enough that it is effectively unreachable, to prevent items from being silently dropped from the response.
Make internal limits (such as max result sizes) configurable via parameters with sensible defaults, so tests can set low values to verify limit enforcement without needing large datasets.
Pass the full domain object to a validation method rather than individual fields, so the method can evolve its checks without requiring callers to know which fields are validated.
Use configurable variables or environment-derived values instead of hardcoding version tags, image references, or other values that may change over time in test fixtures.
In end-to-end tests, avoid placing long-running API steps before browser interactions, as the browser session may time out and cause unrelated failures.
When consuming internal packages, import from the dist (compiled) output rather than directly from source files to ensure proper module resolution and avoid compilation issues.
Remove console.log and other console statements before merging; do not use console logging as a workaround for unused variable errors.
Define table column configurations in or near the table component file rather than in a parent view, to keep rendering logic co-located with the component that uses it.
When using a global store (e.g., useStore), access it at the consuming component level rather than at the router level and passing values down as props.

Import UI components from the internal design system wrapper library rather than directly from the underlying component library (e.g., Ant Design) to maintain consistency and enable centralized customization.
When a render function or column renderer grows complex, extract it into its own standalone component rather than keeping it in a utility file.
When the same set of string literal values is used across multiple files, define a union type to centralize them and make future additions easier.
When logging service operations, include the filter or query parameters in the log message to aid debugging and traceability.
When adding new fields to a public API, check whether existing system or integration tests need to be updated to cover the new field.
Prefer using fake implementations over mocking frameworks in tests, as fakes provide more realistic behavior and are easier to maintain.
When adding log statements with contextual data, include all relevant fields rather than a subset, so that logs provide complete diagnostic information.
Use the correct semantic HTML element for interactive controls; do not use a Link component when the behavior is that of a button.
Do not commit empty files to the repository; remove them or add the intended content before merging.
Mark temporary workarounds, skipped functionality, or deferred work with a TODO comment so it is discoverable and trackable.
Use built-in framework components (e.g., react-router's Redirect) instead of writing custom replacements unless you have a documented reason the built-in cannot satisfy the requirement.
When testing multiple expected failures, assert each one individually rather than in a loop that stops at the first failure.
In E2E tests, use element-type-specific selectors (e.g., 'I wait to see the checkbox') rather than generic text selectors to ensure the correct UI element is present.
In E2E tests, verify that the element type in your assertion matches the actual rendered element; asserting a checkbox when the element is not a checkbox will cause a false failure.
Avoid using 'any' as a type in TypeScript; use proper typed interfaces or generics to maintain type safety.
Do not mark test functions as async unless the test body actually awaits something; unnecessary async adds confusion and may mask timing issues.
Maintain consistent import ordering: absolute/external imports should come before relative imports, and rely on auto-formatting tools like Prettier to enforce this.
Keep React state as close as possible to the component that uses it; do not lift state to a parent container unless the parent actually needs it.
When displaying code snippets in the UI, use the CodeEditor component in read-only mode rather than plain text or custom markup for consistent syntax presentation.
When removing or simplifying wrapper elements, verify that text overflow and ellipsis behavior still works correctly in constrained layouts.
Do not mark test functions as async if they contain no await expressions; unnecessary async adds confusion and overhead.
When migrating between SDK versions behind a feature flag, keep the old implementation unchanged so it remains a valid fallback; do not modify the old path with new SDK calls.
Do not expose vendor-specific version numbers (e.g., V2) in public API names; treat SDK version details as implementation internals.
Do not embed configuration or feature-flag values in domain data objects; keep configuration concerns separate from the data model.
When wrapping an input stream in a data object, store a factory function (lambda) to regenerate the stream on each access rather than holding a single stream reference that can only be read once.

In Scala, prefer case classes over regular classes with @unused annotations; case class constructor parameters are automatically public vals, eliminating the need for boilerplate getters and suppression annotations.
In Scala, use case classes with public vals instead of Java-style getter methods (getX); access fields directly by name following Scala naming conventions.
Place related types in the same package or directory even if they are in different build modules; do not scatter related abstractions across unrelated packages.
When renaming a field or method during a refactor, update all call sites to use the new name rather than adding compatibility aliases that obscure the actual type.
Do not add production API surface (methods, wrappers, interfaces) solely to support test code; move test-only utilities to the test module or use the underlying client directly in tests.

When designing a client wrapper, choose a consistent configuration strategy: either make the client a singleton with per-call parameters, or make one client instance per configuration set; avoid mixing constructor-level and method-level configuration for the same concern.
Unpack settings objects and pass only the specific settings a component needs rather than the entire settings object.
Place new configuration settings alongside existing related settings and follow the established pattern rather than creating new configuration locations.
Use enumerated types instead of boolean flags when a field represents more than two meaningful states to improve clarity.
When modifying API endpoints, preserve the old endpoint for backward compatibility rather than replacing it, especially when external consumers may depend on it.
Include negative test cases that verify behavior does not occur when it shouldn't, not just positive tests for expected behavior.
Use proper synchronization (e.g., AtomicReference) for mutable state that can be accessed by concurrent calls.
Prefer simple, direct implementations for straightforward operations rather than routing them through complex abstractions like state machines.
Document user-facing timeouts and configurable limits with comments explaining their full behavior and impact on users.
Update architecture documentation when introducing new build tools or infrastructure changes.
Import aliases should resolve to the correct type of code — aliases pointing to compiled output paths should not redirect to source code.
Use namespaced package imports (e.g., @org/package) to be explicit about the nature and origin of imports.
Do not store computed values in the database that can be derived from other fields; compute them on read instead.
When exposing configuration or internal data through API endpoints, review whether sensitive values (secrets, credentials) could leak outside their intended security boundary.
Add a blank line between logically distinct code blocks (e.g., before an if statement following variable assignments) to improve readability.
Conversion or mapping logic between representations should live on the domain class or its companion object, not in an unrelated service class.
Define traits (interfaces) that are clean of external library dependencies so they can be shared across modules without pulling in transitive dependencies.
Use relative imports for files within the same module or feature directory rather than absolute imports to keep modules self-contained and portable.
Before creating new mock data files, check if shared mocks already exist in a centralized location (e.g., api/__mocks__) and reuse them to avoid duplication.
When adding a new field or flag to a data model, update or add a test case that explicitly verifies the field gets set correctly.
After a mutative action (restore, update, archive) on a detail view, refetch the current entity's data rather than navigating the user away from the page.
When using API-imposed limits or magic numbers (e.g., max page size), add a comment explaining the constraint and its source so future developers understand why the value was chosen.
When transforming collections, verify the return type matches the expected shape (e.g., avoid accidentally nesting arrays by mapping into arrays).
Prefer a single .map transformation over chaining .map and .zip when both values can be computed in one pass.
Create domain-specific authorization roles rather than repurposing generic ones, to avoid unintended permission drift when the generic role evolves.
Always include all referenced values in React useEffect dependency arrays, especially after stabilizing callbacks.
Use optional parameters with sensible default values for commonly used arguments to simplify call sites.
Extract repeated string literals in test files into shared variables to reduce duplication and ease maintenance.
Do not assert on exact UI copy in end-to-end tests; prefer checking key content or semantic selectors to avoid brittle tests that break on copy changes.
Make data-testid attributes and button labels dynamic based on component state so they accurately describe the current action.
Verify that shared state modifications in tests do not impact other tests running in parallel.
Every new class should have a corresponding test spec file before merging.
Set pagination defaults based on real-world data volumes rather than arbitrary numbers, considering the largest known customer deployments.
Remove debug print statements (println, console.log) before merging code.
Run IDE project generation commands when adding new build targets to keep the development environment in sync.
Add comments explaining why security-sensitive operations (like user impersonation) are safe in their specific context.
Use eager singleton binding (.asEagerSingleton()) for dependency injection modules to ensure only one instance is created.
Dependencies must be injected via class constructor arguments rather than instantiated inside the class, to enable unit testing.
Add trace-level logging at service entry points to aid debugging without cluttering production logs.
Use structured logging with key-value pairs in API controllers, logging both success and failure with entity IDs and operation context.
Write unit tests that verify dependencies are called with the correct arguments, not just that they are called.
Review log statements to ensure they do not inadvertently log authentication secrets, tokens, or sensitive headers.
Catch NonFatal exceptions instead of RuntimeException to avoid swallowing fatal errors that should propagate.
Error log messages must include relevant entity IDs and the name of the operation that failed for effective debugging.
Avoid calling .get on Option types; refactor the return type or use safer alternatives like .getOrElse or pattern matching.
API response type names must be semantically accurate for all endpoints that use them; do not reuse a type name that only makes sense for one endpoint.
Pass the exception object as a parameter to the logger so that the full stack trace is logged, rather than only logging the message string.
Capitalize Domino-specific entity names (e.g., Workspace, Datasets) in UI copy but use lowercase for generic terms (e.g., published apps, docs).
Capitalize product feature names (e.g., 'Exports', 'Workspace') when referring to the named feature, but use lowercase when referring to the generic action.
Use a consistent format for help links and instructional copy across the application (e.g., 'Learn more about <feature>').
Do not create special-case handling for entities that can be treated uniformly with other entities of the same type.
Before implementing custom logic, check if an existing service method or API already provides the needed functionality and prefer reusing it.
Every TODO comment in the codebase must reference a tracked ticket (e.g., Jira) so it does not get lost or forgotten.
When a system or integration test fails, automatically collect debugging artifacts (e.g., support bundles, logs) to aid post-failure investigation.
In API and integration tests, explicitly assert the HTTP status code returned by the server, not just the response body.
Replace magic numbers (especially shared constants like sleep durations and timeouts) with named variables or configuration values.
Do not declare multiple conflicting version constraints for the same dependency in a single requirements file; consolidate them into a single compatible range.
When adding a feature flag or toggle, write unit tests covering the behavior under each flag state to prevent regressions.
Do not leave tests commented out or skipped without filing a tracking ticket to ensure they are re-enabled or properly removed.
In React components, use useRef instead of useState for values that do not need to trigger re-renders and have no reactive dependencies.
Components should inject their own known values into callbacks rather than requiring parent components to pass curried functions.
Do not add new exceptions to the useEffect dependency lint rule; always include all dependencies, especially for simple value types like booleans.
Use label="value" notation for JSX string attributes instead of template literals or expressions wrapping plain strings.
Wrap React context provider values in useMemo to prevent unnecessary re-renders of all consuming components.
Do not place React components in style files; components with their own logic belong in separate files, even if they started as styled components.
Reserve the 'use' prefix for React hooks; do not name component props or regular functions with the 'use' prefix.
React component names must accurately describe what the component is, not what it was derived from or an unrelated UI element.
Use NonFatal pattern matching in Scala catch blocks to avoid catching fatal errors that should crash the application.
Extract repeated string literals used as defaults across multiple locations into a shared constant.
Use semantically correct HTTP status codes in API responses (e.g., 403 for forbidden, not other 4xx codes).
Do not create container components unless they have heavy logic or backend calls; simple pass-through containers add unnecessary abstraction.
In BDD-style tests, use 'Then' for verification/assertion steps, not 'And' or other keywords.
Add comments to test steps whose purpose is not immediately obvious from the step description.
Use Option/Optional types (e.g., Option[String]) instead of empty strings to represent absent values, as this makes the absence of a value explicit and type-safe.
Extract entity IDs from API response payloads rather than parsing them out of URLs, which is fragile and couples tests to URL structure.
When introducing new configuration values that affect routing or paths, default to the safe/backward-compatible value first and change it in a follow-up PR to prevent breaking production.
When adding configuration files that must be present in a Docker image, add a corresponding container structure test to verify the file exists and update the Dockerfile COPY instructions.
Place new configuration keys in the same section as related existing settings rather than adding them in an unrelated location in the config file.
Do not expose internal retry/backoff handler callbacks to callers; instead add logging inside the polling function itself, so the retry library can be swapped without breaking the public API.
Use existing shared test helper functions (e.g., wait_for_status) instead of reimplementing polling or status-checking logic inline in individual tests.
Add debug logging inside retry/polling loops to capture the current state on each iteration, aiding troubleshooting of intermittent failures.
In React components, reset all related state variables (flags, content) in error handlers to prevent stale state from a previous successful response leaking into the error state.
Add explanatory comments for non-obvious or tricky logic so future readers understand the intent without reverse-engineering the code.
Create styled icon components instead of using nested CSS class selectors to style child elements, keeping styles co-located and explicit.

Place data classification or detection logic (e.g., whether a file is truncated) on the backend where the information is already known, rather than reimplementing complex detection heuristics on the frontend.
Define static constants outside the React component function scope to avoid unnecessary re-creation on every render.
Be aware that checking loading=false and data=undefined together can cause a brief flash of incorrect UI state before data arrives; guard against this race condition in data-fetching patterns.
Use white-label-aware UI components (e.g., WaitSpinner) instead of brand-specific ones (e.g., spinning domino logo) to avoid additional white-labelling logic.
Absolute imports must appear before relative imports; rely on ESLint/Prettier auto-fix to enforce consistent import ordering.
Do not mark a function as async if its body contains no await expressions.
Add an explanatory comment when a function has a non-obvious or early return to clarify the intent for future readers.
Never use explicit `any` as a TypeScript type; use a specific type, `unknown`, or a generic instead.
Define styled components outside the React component function, never inline within JSX.
Remove commented-out code before merging; use version control history to recover old code if needed.
Define static constants outside of React component functions to avoid unnecessary re-creation on every render.
Use styled components instead of inline styles, especially when the same style is repeated across multiple elements.
When modifying code near ESLint disable comments, verify the disable directive still covers its originally intended suppression.
Remove CI pipeline parameters that are only needed for integration testing before merging to the main branch.
When deleting files, also remove or update all references to those files in CI configs, tests, and other dependent code.
Before writing custom utility code, check if existing internal libraries (e.g., domino-pytk) already provide the needed functionality.
When querying or operating on resources that can have multiple instances (e.g., pods, jobs), target by a specific identifier (e.g., execution ID) rather than a broad scope like project name.
Do not leave commented-out code in the codebase; remove it entirely and rely on version control for history.
When linking monorepo packages, verify that assumed build output directories (e.g., /dist) will exist at resolution time and document the dependency on the build step.
When injecting scripts or helpers via modules at runtime, evaluate whether race conditions can occur between module loading and script availability.
Never import mock modules or test fixtures in production source files; this causes build issues and increases bundle size. Extract shared logic into a utility instead.
Wrap callback functions defined inside React components with React.useCallback() to prevent unnecessary re-creation on every render.
Use TypeScript interface with extends instead of redefining identical types; share type definitions from a common package rather than duplicating across app and package boundaries.
Lambda functions that integrate with CI/CD systems should be thin routing layers; push business logic and config processing closer to where the code is checked out.
In Scala, distinguish between empty collections (Seq.empty) and null; they are not the same and mock setups must match the actual return types to avoid false test behavior.
Aria-label values should be human-readable names with spaces (suitable for screen readers), not id-like strings with dashes.
Domain interfaces should use domain IDs (e.g., DominoId) and keep database-specific details (e.g., MongoDB ObjectId) in the persister implementation layer.
Use CSF3 (Component Story Format 3) for Storybook stories instead of the deprecated MDX format.
Function names should accurately describe all behaviors; if a function both applies and removes tags, split it into separate single-concern functions rather than overloading one name.
In polymorphic data models, place commonly-used fields for filtering and sorting at the top level and nest type-specific definitions in dedicated fields to enable efficient querying and pagination.
Do not ship half-implemented architectural patterns (e.g., HATEOAS URI fields); either implement them properly or remove them entirely and add them back when there is a real need.
Service layer interfaces should not return REST API DTOs; this couples all service consumers (including internal callers) to API definition changes.
Place dependency injection bindings in the injection module that owns the related functionality, not in a neighboring module's injector.
When an operation can partially succeed, return what was included and what failed to include with reasons, rather than failing the entire request or silently dropping items.
Include logging statements in methods that perform significant operations (e.g., project forking, template creation) so that failures and execution flow can be traced.

When a file contains methods serving multiple subsystems, prefix method names with the subsystem or domain they belong to (e.g., makeFlyteCommand instead of makeCommand) so readers can identify purpose without inspecting signatures or return types.
Remove all console.log statements from production code before submitting a PR; use proper logging infrastructure instead of console output.
Review and remove TODO comments that are no longer valid before merging; stale TODOs create confusion about whether work is still pending.
Delete files that become empty after refactoring rather than leaving them in the codebase.
Helper functions should live in the module that owns their concern; do not add unrelated helpers to a generic helper file when a more specific test module or utility file is appropriate.

Variable names should precisely describe what they hold; rename variables when their meaning drifts from the actual content (e.g., use env_image instead of env_uri when the value is a container image reference).
Design functions to return their computed results rather than accepting a mutable container as an argument to be filled; this makes the data flow explicit and the function easier to reason about.

When multiple tests perform similar verification, extract the shared logic into a common assertion function and have each test call it with its specific parameters, reducing duplication and improving maintainability.
When a line of code (especially string formatting or assertions) is too long to read at a glance, break it into multiple lines rather than leaving it as one long horizontal scroll.
Do not create empty containers that are immediately passed to a function for population; instead, capture the function's return value directly in the variable.
Do not use `object` as a type annotation in TypeScript; use a specific interface, type alias, or Record<string, unknown> instead.
Include all dependencies in the useEffect dependency array rather than using eslint-disable; if a dependency causes infinite loops, refactor the callback to be stable.
Do not use @ts-ignore to suppress type errors; instead define correct TypeScript types or type declarations for the component or module.
Separate unrelated changes (e.g., client generation updates vs. feature logic) into distinct PRs to make review easier and git history clearer.
Name configuration variables to clarify which specific resource they refer to rather than using generic names like s3Bucket; prefer names like dominoBlobsS3Bucket.
Choose variable names that communicate the object's lifecycle state, e.g., use persistedDeployment for an entity that has been saved to the database.
Never catch all Throwables or Exceptions broadly; use scala.util.control.NonFatal to avoid swallowing fatal errors like OutOfMemoryError.
When deleting a resource that has both a database record and external dependencies (e.g., Kubernetes resources), delete the external resources first so the record is preserved if cleanup fails.
Do not call .get on Option types; use .getOrElse with a meaningful exception, pattern matching, or .fold to handle the missing-value case explicitly.
When a hardcoded value or workaround exists because upstream functionality is not yet available, add a TODO comment referencing what needs to change and ideally the tracking ticket.
Follow the Databricks Scala style guide and add curly braces around multi-line expressions for clarity and consistency.
Simplify Option handling using combinators like .filter and .getOrElse rather than verbose if/else chains; e.g., use s3DefaultBucket.filter(_.nonEmpty).getOrElse(throw ...).
Catch only NonFatal exceptions in Scala; never use a bare catch block that could swallow fatal errors like OutOfMemoryError.
Add trace-level logging to all client/service boundary methods, including the method name and all parameters.
Custom exceptions must accept and pass a cause Throwable to the Exception constructor so the full stack trace is preserved, not just the message.
When defining custom exception case classes, name the throwable parameter 'cause' and pass it to the Exception superclass constructor for proper exception chaining.

Log messages should describe the action and context (e.g. 'error creating model deployment secret, rolling back') rather than just echoing the exception message; pass the full exception object as a separate parameter for the stack trace.
If a method performs create-or-update logic, name it accordingly (e.g. 'createOrUpdateModelDeployment') so callers understand the full behavior from the name alone.
Name variables and configuration keys to reflect their actual domain purpose rather than generic infrastructure names (e.g. 'mlflowS3ArtifactBucket' instead of a generic blob bucket name).
Use getOrElse with an explicit error thrown when a required value is missing, rather than silently proceeding with a default or None.
Do not hardcode behavior or settings for a specific compute provider type; design abstractions that are provider-agnostic to avoid adding new settings or branches for every new provider.
Use Scala's idiomatic collection methods (e.g. isEmpty, nonEmpty) instead of manual equivalents for cleaner, more readable code.
When matching string input to an enum, use enumeratum's withNameInsensitiveOption instead of manually converting to uppercase before matching.
Keep create and update persistence logic at the same abstraction level; if one path persists inside a service method, the other should too.
Use separate try-catch blocks for independent operations that require different rollback logic, rather than grouping them in a single block.
Service methods should encapsulate their own persistence calls and side effects rather than requiring the controller to orchestrate them.
Ensure finally blocks do not perform persistence or state-changing operations that should only run on success; fatal errors will still execute the finally block.
Do not place success-only logic in a finally block; put it in the try block after the operation that must succeed first.
Use idiomatic Option combinators like getOrElse instead of verbose pattern matching when the logic is a simple value-or-throw.
When refactoring or restructuring code, ensure existing tests are preserved or re-added to maintain coverage.
When a lookup method returns Option/None, decide explicitly whether to throw an exception or handle the missing case rather than silently ignoring it.
When a combined create-or-update method has mostly different logic for each path, split it into separate create and update methods for clarity.
Apply retry policies consistently across similar operations; if update uses a retry policy, create should too.
When adding new components that make API calls, ensure a test suite exists and mock external dependencies to prevent real API calls in tests.
When moving or refactoring files, verify that associated unit tests exist and are migrated along with the code.
Never use fixed sleeps in test code; poll on the expected status or condition instead.
Use existing utility functions (e.g., getAppName) instead of hardcoding values or reimplementing logic that is already available.
When implementing entity copy or fork operations, ensure all attributes from the source are carried over; review for missing fields.
Use existing helper methods (e.g., getTemplateType) to avoid manually unwrapping optional types when a convenience accessor already exists.
Do not ship functionality that will not be tested by QE; if a feature is supported, it must work and have test coverage.
Fields should only be optional in REST API models for backward compatibility; in domain models, required fields must not be optional.
Validation logic (e.g., billing tags) should be applied consistently across all creation or update code paths, not just some.
Ensure exceptions map to the correct HTTP status code; IllegalArgumentException should produce a 400 Bad Request, not a 500 Internal Server Error.
Services should return Optional values and let callers decide how to handle absence, rather than failing internally with a fixed error strategy.
When multiple independent async calls exist, combine and run them in parallel rather than awaiting them sequentially.
When adding a new feature path (e.g., customer template creation), add corresponding observability counters to track usage.
When adding a new dependency to a BUILD file, be prepared to explain why it is needed; unexplained dependencies raise review flags.
When using a Provider instead of direct injection to break a circular dependency, document the reason and file a tech debt ticket for the proper fix.
When modifying shared utility code (e.g., error handling utilities), run the full end-to-end test suite to catch unintended regressions across features that depend on the utility.
Include the test scenario ID in dynamically created resource names (e.g., workspace names) to ensure uniqueness and traceability when debugging test failures.

In tests that depend on infrastructure-specific values (e.g., memory sizes, CPU counts), assert relative conditions or unit-based checks rather than exact absolute values, since those values vary by platform and node configuration.
When removing a code step or block, also remove any associated comments that reference the removed code to avoid stale or misleading documentation.

Domain entities should never depend on API DTOs; the API is one presentation of the domain, not the only one, so coupling the domain to a specific API layer creates a dependency in the wrong direction.
When a file contains a single class or object, name the file after that class to make it easy to locate and reduce cognitive overhead.

Domain entities and business logic should not depend on web framework constructs (e.g., Play Forms, HTTP-specific libraries); isolate framework dependencies to the presentation or validation layer to avoid coupling the domain to a specific web technology.

Domain entities should not include JSON serialization mappings; coupling the domain to a specific serialized format constrains its evolution and creates backwards-compatibility concerns that belong in a separate mapping layer.

When different entity variants have distinct sets of fields, use type polymorphism (subclasses or sealed traits) rather than making all variant-specific fields optional on a single type, to enforce invariants at the type level.

Validation helpers and required-field definitions are not domain entities; extract them into a dedicated file (e.g., ValidationHelpers) scoped to the package that uses them, keeping domain entity files focused on business concepts.
Database persistence records should not reuse API DTOs for field types; define dedicated persistence types so that changes to the API layer do not break database serialization and vice versa.

When a type hierarchy has variants with different field sets, do not carry fields from one variant into another as always-empty optionals; remove fields that do not apply to a given subtype to keep the data model honest.

Remove or restrict interfaces from shared modules when direct injection should be prevented; keeping implementation classes in the implementation module (rather than exposing them via an interface module) ensures consumers use the intended service facade.
When sending data between services, use a dedicated DTO rather than the internal domain representation, to decouple the internal model from the external contract and support independent evolution.
In Scala, use pattern matching on Option[Boolean] instead of nested conditionals to make the intent of each branch explicit and exhaustive.

Place DTOs in packages that reflect their boundary context (e.g., `api.models` for REST API DTOs, `remotehub.models` for external service mapping DTOs) so reviewers can quickly identify which compatibility constraints apply.
Do not add classes, DTOs, or other code that has no current usages; introduce them in the PR where they are actually needed.
Domain services should depend on domain entities, not on API-layer DTOs; coupling services to API models causes unrelated API changes to ripple into core business logic.
When adding permission checks, follow the existing authorization patterns in similar methods rather than inventing new approaches; consistency reduces review burden and prevents subtle security gaps.
When fixing a bug caused by a specific code pattern, scan the same file and nearby files for identical patterns that may have the same issue.
Prefer `aria-label` over `data-testid` for identifying interactive elements in tests; this serves the dual purpose of improving accessibility and simplifying test selectors.
Write `aria-label` values as full human-readable phrases (not terse IDs or abbreviations) since they are read aloud by screen readers for visually impaired users.
Use `dict.get(key, default)` with an appropriate default value instead of separate null-checking logic to simplify data access in Python.
Use `@dataclass` (or `NamedTuple`) with auto-generated constructors instead of custom wrapper classes with manual builder methods; prefer explicit construction over hidden convenience methods.
Avoid using singleton/global state patterns unless there is a clear need and thread-safety implications have been considered for the test runner or runtime environment.
Use list comprehensions instead of imperative for-loops when transforming a collection of items into a new list in Python.
When a helper function is almost always called alongside a logging statement, encapsulate the logging inside the helper to reduce repetition and prevent missed log calls.
Before removing environment variables, scripts, or shared resources, search for usages across all consuming repositories and CI pipelines to avoid breaking downstream dependencies.
Use existing environment variables for version strings and configuration values rather than hardcoding them, especially in CI scripts where values are already exported.
Write BDD feature file scenarios as user stories (As a [role], I'd like to [action], So that [benefit]) to clearly communicate the intent and value of each test.
Domain mapping code (e.g., converting template models to project models) should live in the service that owns the source domain, not in the consuming service, to avoid cross-service coupling.
Name test fixtures to describe the resource they provide (e.g., `git_credentials`) rather than using generic or action-oriented names.
Define named constants for magic numbers and reuse them across related logic to improve readability and maintainability.
Always await async functions inside try-catch blocks; a try-catch cannot catch errors from unawaited async calls, leading to silently unhandled rejections.
Ensure test `it` descriptions accurately reflect what is actually being asserted; a test that only validates initial state should not claim to validate state changes.
Use framework-provided codecs like AutoRecordMapperCodec instead of manually writing serialization/deserialization code.
When processing events that trigger repeated database lookups, introduce a low-complexity cache to avoid redundant queries.
In e2e tests, prefer API steps over UI interactions for test data setup to improve speed and reliability.
Move utility and helper functions outside of React component bodies to improve readability and separation of concerns.
Define related string or numeric constants as enums or typed mappings rather than loose constants to enforce type safety and discoverability.
Instead of useEffect to sync state with prop changes, use the React pattern of tracking previous props in state and updating during render to avoid missing dependency warnings and extra re-renders.

When spreading rest props containing aria attributes onto compound components (e.g., a button wrapping a link), verify that screen readers correctly interpret the duplicated attributes across nested elements.
When a component depends on asynchronously loaded configuration (e.g., locale data), consider delaying the component render until the data is available to avoid flash of incorrect content.
Domain/interface modules must not reference API/web module types; mapping between domain and API models should flow in the correct dependency direction as enforced by the build system.
In API responses, provide explicit fields (e.g., provider name) rather than expecting consumers to parse or derive values from other fields (e.g., extracting provider from a URI).
Use imperative functions (e.g., extracted fetchData callbacks) for data refresh instead of chaining boolean state flags through multiple useEffects, which causes unnecessary re-renders.
Within the React SPA, use route links (React Router Link components) instead of plain anchor tags or window.location for internal navigation.
Prefer existing shared test helpers (e.g., `@domino/test-utils` layoutTestUtils.checkBreadcrumbs) over reimplementing common assertions in individual test files.
Do not use deprecated or legacy product terminology (e.g., 'detwirl') in new code, variable names, or route definitions — use current domain language instead.

Each component should be in its own file; do not export multiple components with similar interfaces but different implementations from the same file, and avoid vendor-prefixing component names (e.g., 'Ant').
Use kebab-case for data-testid attribute values to maintain consistency across the codebase.
Do not spread-clone an entire props object just to add one property; instead pass the original props and add the extra prop individually at the call site.
If a prop is optional and has no default value, do not assume it will exist — add a null/undefined check or provide a default.
Prefer setting default values inline at the destructuring site rather than using separate conditional logic or fallback assignments later in the function body.
When code contains non-obvious defensive logic (e.g., forcefully killing a process on repeated stop requests), add a comment explaining why it is necessary.
When building generalized endpoints or services, ensure they support all planned use cases (e.g., multiple backing store types) from the start, not just the immediate one.
Never hardcode the product name (e.g., 'Domino') in UI strings; always use the app name getter to support white-labelling.
Before creating a new constant, check if an existing one already serves the purpose; if a default constant's value is insufficient, increase it rather than introducing a confusingly-named duplicate.
When multiple match/case branches repeat the same structure with different parameters, extract the varying parts into a tuple and handle the common logic once to improve readability.
Use domain-level ID constructors (e.g., DominoId.newInstance) instead of database-specific types (e.g., ObjectId) outside of persistence code to maintain abstraction boundaries.
Module-scoped or broadly-shared test fixtures should be defined in dedicated fixture files, not inline in specific test files, to clarify ownership and reusability.
Before writing inline test setup code, check for existing helper functions (e.g., create_user()) that already encapsulate the operation.
Tests that create resources (projects, users, etc.) should archive or clean them up after execution to avoid polluting shared environments.
When a variable is an alias for another concept (e.g., selectedRevision aliasing activeRevision), add a clarifying comment to prevent confusion for future readers.
Define static configuration objects like table column definitions as constants outside the component to avoid recreating them on every re-render.

When calling formatting utilities (e.g., date formatters) with potentially undefined values, verify the function handles undefined gracefully or add a guard to prevent displaying 'Invalid date' or similar artifacts.
Write BDD test step definitions that read as natural English sentences, rewording awkward phrasing for clarity.
Use enums generated from API specs (e.g., OpenAPI YAML) instead of suppressing type errors with ts-ignore comments.
Use the project's design-system components (e.g., the white-label-aware wait spinner) instead of hardcoded or third-party alternatives to ensure consistent branding.
Remove commented-out code and commented-out tests before merging; use version control history to recover deleted code if needed.
Avoid unnecessary type casting; if you find yourself casting API response types, verify the API typing is correct and use proper types directly.
Extract complex inline logic into named functions for readability and reuse.
Use rem units instead of px for margin and padding to support responsive scaling and accessibility.
Don't add conversion or mapping methods when the service layer already returns the correct type; use the returned type directly.
Place internal service models in the service interface package, not in the API models package; the api.model package should only contain API model entities.
Separate pagination, sorting, and filtering into distinct parameters in service interfaces rather than bundling them into a single object.
Model query parameters according to their actual semantics: use sort order for ranking/ordering concerns and filters for inclusion/exclusion criteria; don't conflate the two.
Use `q` as the query parameter name for search/filter endpoints across all APIs to maintain consistency, and use a more descriptive name like `searchQuery` only internally.
API parameter types should match their actual usage (e.g., single-select UI means a singular parameter, not a sequence); expanding from singular to multi-valued later is a non-breaking change.
API filter parameter names should match the corresponding domain model attribute names so the mapping is intuitive (e.g., use `owner` not `author` if the model property is `owner`).
Name binders, helpers, and utility methods after what they actually do, not what they were copied from or vaguely related to.
Use `getOrElse` with a descriptive `IllegalStateException` message instead of bare `.get` on Options; even for impossible states, clear error messages make debugging easier.
Pagination responses must compute totalCount from the database query reflecting all filtered results, not just the size of the current page.
Test fixture creators and builders should live in the shared test utilities library (e.g., `testslib`) so they can be reused across test suites, not duplicated in individual spec files.
Use the proper domain type (e.g., `DominoId`) directly instead of relying on implicit conversions from primitive types like String.
Do not use `Provider` for dependency injection unless there is an actual circular dependency; import the dependency directly for simpler, more readable code.
Avoid heavy IO on every request for data that changes infrequently; use caching or lazy refresh strategies to reduce database load.
Replace multiple individual database queries with a single bulk query using operators like $in, then diff results in memory.
When comparing or filtering datasets against a database, fetch all relevant records in one query and perform filtering in memory rather than issuing per-record lookups.
Name functions and fields after the domain concept they represent, not a loosely related concept; mismatched naming creates confusion when the API exposes those names.
Use strict equality operators (=== instead of ==) to avoid type coercion bugs in JavaScript and Scala templates.
When commenting out or disabling code due to a known issue, include a reference to the tracking ticket so the workaround can be cleaned up when the issue is resolved.
Define named constants for magic numbers like page sizes and reuse them across components to ensure consistency and ease of change.
Fix typos in type and interface names promptly; a typo that resembles another type's name creates confusion and blocks future corrections due to name collisions.
Avoid wildcard imports (`import * as`) and duplicate imports from the same module; use named imports instead.
Always use strict equality operators (=== and !==) instead of loose equality (== and !=) in JavaScript/TypeScript.
Extract utility functions and non-UI logic out of React component files into separate utility modules.
Separate styled component definitions with an empty line between each component for readability.
Always provide default values for environment variables in shell scripts using the `${VAR:-default}` syntax to prevent unbound variable errors.
Ensure API errors are surfaced to the user rather than silently swallowed, even when a hook or wrapper handles fetching.
Use descriptive, meaningful variable names that convey purpose; avoid generic or abbreviated names.
Align and format complex expressions and chained calls for readability, especially in functional pipelines and multi-line expressions.
Use modern default imports (`import React from 'react'`) instead of namespace imports for React.
Move reusable utility and helper functions into dedicated utils files rather than co-locating them in route or component files.
Use the React fragment shorthand syntax (`<>...</>`) instead of `<React.Fragment>` when no key prop is needed.
When a function's behavior changes (e.g., event handlers removed), rename the function to accurately reflect its new purpose and avoid confusion.

Avoid hardcoding dimension constants (e.g., pixel widths) in child components that the parent knows nothing about; instead derive state from a boolean or enum at the parent level to prevent fragile implicit coupling.

When an optional API field being absent causes destructive behavior (e.g., removing an existing value), document this explicitly in the API and add unit tests, because callers may assume absent fields are simply skipped.
Remove unused variables, imports, and code rather than leaving them in the codebase.
Permission denial messages should be specific about what the user cannot do and include actionable resolution steps such as contacting an administrator.
When making changes for a cloud fix or hotfix, prefer creating a wrapper/composition component rather than modifying a shared base component to minimize risk to other consumers.
Do not commit empty test files; either write meaningful tests or remove the file until tests are ready.
Use trace-level logging for high-frequency or verbose operational messages so they are off by default but can be enabled when needed for debugging.
Use a structured retry policy with backoff and max attempts instead of infinite loops for error recovery and reconnection logic.
In Scala, place constants and static-like members in a companion object rather than inline in the class body.
Name variables and types using terminology from the current domain context, not carried over from unrelated services or modules.
Add unit tests that serve as executable examples showing how a component works, especially for non-trivial stream processing or data transformation logic.
Status enums should include an explicit error state and propagate error details so consumers can handle failures gracefully.
Use explicit boolean props rather than optional function props to control whether UI elements are shown, as optional functions are less verbose and harder to reason about.
Use the same term for the same concept across all API endpoints to avoid confusion; do not introduce synonyms (e.g., 'authors' vs 'owners') for the same entity.
When an endpoint is not paginated, set the internal query limit high enough that it's effectively unreachable to prevent data from being silently truncated in the UI.
Extract hardcoded limits (like max result size) into parameters with default values so tests can set low limits to verify boundary behavior without needing large datasets.
Validation methods should receive the full objects they need to validate rather than relying on callers to extract specific fields; callers should not assume which fields will be checked.
Use configuration variables or environment-level constants for values like Docker image tags in tests rather than hardcoding them, to reduce maintenance burden when they change.
In a monorepo, import shared package components from their dist (built) files rather than directly from source to ensure consistent module resolution and avoid compilation issues.
Remove console.log and other console statements before merging; do not use console output as a workaround for unused variable warnings.
Define table column configurations in a file co-located with the table component rather than in a parent view; pass data, not column definitions, as props.
When a store (e.g., useStore) is globally accessible, consume it directly in the component that needs it rather than prop-drilling it from a router or parent.
Import UI components from the Domino design system wrapper library instead of directly from Ant Design; if the wrapper doesn't support a needed variant, flag it for extension.
When a column renderer or inline JSX block grows complex, extract it into its own component rather than leaving it as a function in a utility file.
Define a TypeScript union type for sets of related string literal keys instead of using raw strings, so new values are easy to add and type-checked.
When logging service operations, include the request filters and parameters in the log message so that issues can be diagnosed from logs alone.
When adding or modifying fields in a public API contract, check whether existing system or integration tests need to be updated to cover the new field.
Use fake implementations (in-memory, lightweight stand-ins) instead of mocks in integration and unit tests for more realistic behavior and easier maintenance.
When adding log statements to a method, include all relevant state values (e.g., flags, IDs, configuration) rather than logging only a subset.
Use the correct semantic component for the interaction — do not use a Link component as a button or vice versa.
Do not commit empty files to the repository; remove them before merging.
Add TODO labels to mark known issues or temporary workarounds in test code so they can be tracked and addressed later.
Use framework-provided components (e.g., react-router's Redirect) instead of building custom equivalents unless there is a clear justification.
When testing multiple expected failures, assert each one individually rather than relying on a loop that stops at the first failure.
Use specific selectors in e2e tests (e.g., 'I wait to see the checkbox "Label"') rather than generic text checks, to ensure the correct element type and content are verified.
Avoid using `any` type in TypeScript; use proper types or generics instead.
Do not mark test functions as async unless they contain awaited expressions.
Maintain consistent import ordering with absolute imports before relative imports, and ensure Prettier/linting enforces it automatically.
Keep React state as local as possible to the component that uses it, rather than lifting it to a container that doesn't directly need it.
Use a CodeEditor component (in read-only mode) to display code snippets rather than plain text elements, for proper syntax highlighting and formatting.
Do not mark test cases as async unless they contain an await expression; unnecessary async obscures which tests are truly asynchronous.
When migrating between SDK versions behind a feature flag, keep the v1 code path unchanged so it can serve as a reliable rollback; only modify the v2 path.
When creating wrapper abstractions over third-party SDKs, use generic names (e.g., listObjects) rather than leaking vendor-specific version details (e.g., listObjectsV2) into public APIs.

Do not store an InputStream as a class attribute because it can only be read once; instead hold a factory lambda that produces a fresh stream on each call, or use a stream-plus-metadata pattern like AWS ResponseInputStream.
In Scala, use case classes instead of regular classes with private fields and @unused annotations; case classes provide public getters automatically and eliminate boilerplate.
In Scala, use case class val accessors (obj.key) instead of Java-style getter methods (obj.getKey); follow Scala naming conventions for accessors and mutators.
Keep related abstractions (interfaces, wrappers, models) in the same package/directory even when build-system module boundaries differ from language package boundaries.
When renaming an attribute or method, update all call sites directly rather than adding a compatibility alias that mismatches the return type or semantics.
Do not add or maintain production wrapper methods that are only used in tests; instead, use the underlying client directly in test code to keep the production API surface minimal.
Only include fields in wrapper/DTO types that are actually consumed by callers; remove fields carried over from the underlying SDK that have no current use.
When injecting configuration, unpack the settings object and pass only the specific settings a component needs rather than passing the entire settings object.

New configuration settings should be added to the most relevant existing settings class rather than a generic top-level Settings file, using optional fields with sensible defaults to avoid touching application.conf.
When a flag represents more than a simple on/off state, use an enumeration with descriptive values instead of a boolean to make the intent and transitions clear.
When modifying or replacing API endpoints, keep the old endpoint available for backward compatibility, especially when external clients or agents may depend on it.
When testing state-dependent operations, include negative test cases that verify the operation does NOT trigger in states where it should be inactive.
Mutable state accessed from multiple threads must be properly synchronized using thread-safe constructs like AtomicReference, not plain mutable variables.
Do not route simple operations through complex machinery like state machines when a direct implementation would suffice; choose the simplest approach that meets actual requirements.
When documenting configurable values or behaviors in comments, ensure the comment describes the full behavior including edge cases, not just the common case.
When introducing configurable timeouts that affect user-facing behavior, ensure public documentation exists so users know about the limits and how to adjust them.
When introducing new build tools or changing the build pipeline, update the corresponding architecture documentation to reflect the changes.
Import path aliases that reference a /dist directory should resolve to compiled output, not source code; mixing these causes broken React contexts and confusing build behavior.
Values that can be computed from other stored data should not be persisted as separate database fields; instead, compute them at read time from their source data to avoid synchronization issues.
When exposing configuration values through API endpoints, assess whether sensitive secrets could leak and document the security boundary (e.g., superuser-only access).
Add a blank line between logically distinct blocks (e.g., before an if-statement that follows unrelated code) to improve readability.
Place conversion or mapping logic on the domain class or its companion object rather than in an unrelated service, so the logic is co-located with the type it transforms.
When moving code into shared/common modules, extract a clean trait or interface that is free of external library dependencies so consumers are not forced to pull in transitive dependencies.
Within a frontend module's own components, use relative imports instead of absolute/aliased imports to keep the module self-contained and avoid circular dependency risks.
Use existing centralized mock data (e.g., from api/__mocks__) instead of creating duplicate mock files in individual component directories.
Do not mark fields as optional without a clear reason; unnecessary optionals introduce null-checking burden and weaken type safety.
When adding or changing a field mapping or business rule, add or update a corresponding test to verify the new behavior is exercised.
After a state-restoring mutation (e.g., restore, unarchive), refetch the current entity's data and stay on the detail page rather than navigating the user back to the list view.
When modifying data-fetching or transformation expressions, verify the return type matches what consumers expect (e.g., avoid accidentally returning an array of arrays instead of a flat array).
Prefer a single map operation over separate map and zip calls when transforming collections, to reduce intermediate allocations and improve readability.
Create purpose-specific authorization roles for distinct use cases rather than repurposing existing roles with similar-but-different semantics, to avoid coupling and accidental permission drift.
When a callback passed to useEffect becomes stable (e.g., wrapped in useCallback with empty deps), add it to the useEffect dependency array rather than omitting it with an eslint-disable.
When a function parameter has a sensible default value, make it optional with that default rather than requiring callers to always provide it.
When the same literal string or value appears multiple times in a test scenario, extract it into a variable defined in the Background section to reduce duplication and ease maintenance.
Minimize assertions on specific UI copy in E2E tests; assert on key functional outcomes rather than exact text strings to make tests resilient to copy changes.
Make data-testid attributes and accessibility labels dynamic to reflect component state (e.g., expanded vs collapsed) so tests and assistive tools can distinguish between states.
When tests modify shared configuration (feature flags, system settings), verify that the changes are scoped so they do not interfere with other tests running in parallel.
Every new domain class or service should have a corresponding test spec file; do not merge new logic without test coverage.
Remove all debug print statements (println, console.log, etc.) before merging; use structured logging instead if runtime output is needed.
When an API endpoint can return a large number of items, use a configurable page size (with a sensible default) and document known cases where the default may be insufficient.
When code impersonates a user or uses elevated privileges, add an inline comment explaining why it is safe to do so and what authorization mechanism protects the call.
Service implementations in Guice injection modules should be bound as eager singletons using .asEagerSingleton() to ensure only one instance is created.

API controller methods should log success at info level and errors at error level, including structured context (userId, entity IDs, request parameters) in both cases; extract a reusable executeOperation helper to reduce verbosity.
Add trace-level log statements at service method entry points to aid debugging without polluting info-level logs.
When a method delegates to a dependency, write a unit test that verifies the dependency is called with the correct arguments, not just that the method succeeds.
When logging request or response data (headers, payloads), verify that authentication tokens, secrets, or credentials are not included in the log output.
In Scala, catch NonFatal(ex) instead of RuntimeException to avoid swallowing fatal errors like OutOfMemoryError while still handling all recoverable failures.
Error log messages must include the operation that failed and relevant entity identifiers (e.g., modelId, userId) to enable effective debugging.
Calling .get on an Option is a code smell; refactor the return type or use pattern matching, getOrElse, or fold to handle the None case safely.
API response type names must make sense for every endpoint that uses them; if a response is shared across endpoints, choose a generic name rather than one tied to a specific operation.
When logging exceptions, pass the exception object as a parameter to the logger method so the full stack trace is captured, rather than only logging the message string.
In user-facing text, capitalize Domino-specific entity names (Workspace, Model API, Artifacts, Datasets) but use lowercase for generic terms (published apps, docs).
Capitalize product feature names (e.g., 'Exports', 'Feature Store') but use lowercase for generic actions (e.g., 'export files', 'import data').
Use a consistent format for contextual help links across the product, such as 'Learn more about {feature}' without trailing punctuation.
Every TODO comment in code must have an associated Jira ticket to ensure it gets tracked and eventually addressed.
System tests and integration tests should automatically collect debug artifacts (e.g., support bundles, logs) when jobs or operations fail, to aid post-failure investigation.
API tests should explicitly assert HTTP status codes rather than only checking response bodies, to catch unexpected error responses early.
Magic numbers (e.g., sleep durations, timeouts) that are used in multiple places should be extracted into named constants or shared configuration to keep them in sync.
Do not place conflicting version constraints for the same dependency in a single requirements file; use a single combined constraint (e.g., `>=1.0,<2.3`) or separate requirements files per context.
When adding behavior gated by a feature flag, add unit tests that validate the code path under the new flag to prevent regressions from untested branches.
Do not skip or comment out tests without documenting the reason and creating a Jira ticket to track re-enabling them.
In React components, prefer useRef over useState for values that do not need to trigger re-renders, reserving useState for values that affect the rendered output.
Components should inject their own data into callbacks rather than receiving pre-curried functions from parent levels; keep data binding close to the source.
Do not add new eslint-disable exceptions for useEffect dependency arrays; include all dependencies, especially simple values like booleans that are unlikely to cause issues.
Wrap React Context provider values in useMemo to prevent unnecessary re-renders of all child components consuming the context.
Files named styles.tsx should only contain styled components; standalone React components with their own logic belong in separate files.
The `useX` naming convention is reserved for React hooks; do not use it for component props or callback names.
Name components after what they actually are; do not include 'Button' in a component name if the component is not a button.
In Scala, use `NonFatal` in catch blocks to avoid catching fatal errors (like OutOfMemoryError) that should cause the service to fail.
When a default string value is used in multiple places, extract it into a shared constant to ensure consistency and ease of maintenance.
Return the correct HTTP status code for the error condition; use 403 for forbidden access, not arbitrary 4xx codes.
Do not create container/wrapper components when there is no heavy logic or backend calls to justify the separation; keep the component tree flat when possible.
In BDD feature files, use `Then` to begin verification/assertion steps, not `And` or other keywords, to clearly distinguish actions from verifications.
Add explanatory comments for test steps whose purpose is not immediately clear, especially workarounds for previous bugs or non-deterministic behavior.
Use Option/Maybe types (e.g., Option[String]) to represent optional values instead of empty strings or other sentinel values, even when it requires additional validation work.
When test steps need entity IDs, extract them from API response bodies rather than parsing them out of URLs, which is fragile and couples tests to URL structure.
When introducing new environment variables or config that changes routing or behavior, default to the safe/no-op value initially and change it in a follow-up PR to prevent breaking production.
When adding new files that must be present in Docker images (e.g., .env files, config), add a corresponding container-structure-test entry to verify the file exists in the built image.
When adding new configuration keys, place them in the existing section for that feature or domain rather than adding them in an unrelated location in the config file.

Keep retry/polling implementation details (e.g., backoff handlers) internal to helper functions rather than exposing them to callers, so the underlying retry library can be swapped without breaking consumers.
Before writing custom polling or wait logic in tests, check if an existing helper method already handles the use case (e.g., wait_for_status) to avoid duplicating logic and keep tests concise.

When fetching data that sets multiple state variables, ensure error handlers also reset dependent state (e.g., flags and content) to prevent stale values from a previous successful fetch from persisting.
When styling icons or child elements within a React component, create a dedicated styled component rather than using nested class selectors, which are harder to maintain and less explicit.
