Place data detection and validation logic on the backend where the authoritative information exists, rather than implementing complex client-side heuristics to infer server-side state.
Static constants (arrays, objects, config values) that don't depend on props or state should be defined outside the component function to avoid unnecessary re-creation on each render.
Use white-label-aware UI components (e.g., WaitSpinner) instead of brand-specific alternatives to avoid additional white-labelling logic.
Do not mark functions as async if they contain no await expressions.
Add a comment explaining non-obvious return statements so reviewers and future readers understand the intent.
Never use explicit `any` as a TypeScript type; use proper types, generics, or `unknown` instead.
Define styled components outside the JSX render tree, not inline within component markup.
Remove commented-out code before merging; use version control history to retrieve old code if needed.
Do not commit empty files; either add content or remove the file entirely.
Define static constants outside of React component functions to avoid unnecessary re-creation on each render.
When moving or modifying code near ESLint disable comments, verify the disable directive still applies to the intended line.
When deleting files, verify that no CI configs, scripts, or other files still reference the deleted path.
Place reusable test utilities in the shared tests-lib module rather than duplicating them in individual adapter or service test directories.
When an enum value's name no longer matches its semantic meaning due to backward compatibility, add a comment to the enum explaining the mapping (e.g., 'bitbucketServer' actually means 'datacenter').
When the same transformation or sanitization logic appears in multiple places, move it onto the relevant domain object so consumers get the behavior for free.
When an operation can fail for multiple reasons, write error messages that describe the observed symptom generically rather than asserting a specific cause that may be wrong.
Do not mark a value as lazy if it is referenced on the very next line; the deferred initialization adds complexity with no benefit.
When filtering one collection by membership in another, convert the lookup collection to a Set first to achieve O(n) instead of O(n²) runtime.
Use Scala's collect with a partial function instead of chaining filter and map when both selecting and transforming collection elements.
Place methods specific to a single external provider (e.g., Bitbucket, GitHub) on that provider's implementation class rather than on the shared base client or trait.
When using string replacement or pattern matching on URLs or paths, consider edge cases where the target substring may appear multiple times and ensure the logic handles all occurrences correctly.
When a single integration has fundamentally different implementations (e.g., cloud vs. datacenter), use separate feature flags for each so they can be enabled, disabled, and debugged independently.
Use map/filter/reduce instead of manually creating arrays and pushing elements into them.
Define styled components outside the component body to avoid re-creating them on every re-render.
Show a loading indicator while asynchronous data (e.g., dropdown options, form defaults) is being fetched.
Import shared UI components (tooltips, modals, etc.) from internal wrappers rather than directly from third-party libraries like antd.
Use the project's shared FormItem components for form fields to maintain consistent styling across the application.
Configuration key names must exactly match the names referenced in the code that reads them; mismatches cause silent failures.
Define configuration defaults in one canonical location (e.g., application.conf) and remove duplicate defaults from code to avoid divergence.
Remove developer names, nicknames, and personal debug markers from code before merging.
Name feature flags and configuration parameters descriptively so their purpose is immediately clear (e.g., uploadFileBatchCount instead of a vague abbreviation).
All user-facing pages and components must support white-labeling; never hard-code product-specific branding.
Ensure every element in a React list has a unique key; duplicated keys cause rendering bugs and reconciliation issues.
Scope CSS and styled-component overrides to the specific element or variant that needs them; avoid broad selectors that unintentionally affect unrelated components.
Remove all debugging print statements and temporary logging before submitting a PR for review.
Related API endpoints (e.g., list and get-by-id) should use the same DTO models to avoid divergent representations of the same resource.
Design domain models and DTOs so that invalid combinations of fields cannot be constructed; validate at creation time rather than relying on downstream checks.
Error messages, validation messages, and other user-facing strings should start with a capitalized word.
When the same logic appears in multiple files, extract it into a shared utility function to reduce duplication and ensure consistency.

When a component has multiple conditional branches based on feature flags, consider splitting it into separate components — one per flag state — to reduce complexity and make cleanup easier when the flag is removed.
Test mock data should live in the shared mocks package rather than co-located with individual test files, so mocks can be reused across test suites.
Always use color variables or design tokens from the design system instead of hardcoded hex values in component styles.
Never use .get() on Scala Option values; use .map/.getOrElse or pattern matching to safely handle the None case with a meaningful fallback.
When introducing new log markers or event names, choose names that will not collide with planned features to avoid confusion when both coexist in production logs.
End-to-end test steps that verify backend or API behavior should not depend on being on a specific UI page; use API-based approaches or parameterized steps to avoid brittle coupling.
Use the project's shared/common modal component instead of creating custom one-off modals, to ensure visual consistency and make future restyling feasible.
When passing a boolean prop with value true in JSX, use the shorthand form (e.g., `<Route exact>` instead of `<Route exact={true}>`).
When adding retry/wait logic for tests, extend the existing helper function rather than creating a new one, so all retry behavior lives in one place.
When a test needs to wait for an asynchronous operation, identify the root cause of the delay and use a targeted wait (e.g., wait_for_status) rather than a generous blanket timeout.
When implementing exponential backoff or retry delay calculations, validate that the computed delay cannot overflow numeric limits at high attempt counts.
Use Option types to represent optional updates instead of pairing a value field with a boolean flag; this eliminates invalid states and reduces the API surface.
When changing API method signatures or adding new parameters, update the corresponding scaladoc/swagger documentation in the same PR to describe the new behavior.
When adding or changing fields in a backend API, verify whether frontend mocks, TypeScript types, or swagger docs also need updating to stay in sync.
When conditionally applying an update based on an Option value in Scala, use `option.foreach` instead of if/else checks for more idiomatic and concise code.
Every bug fix must include a test that reproduces the bug and verifies the fix.
When gating a feature behind a feature flag, ensure all related UI elements (buttons, checkboxes, table columns) are hidden, not just the primary component.
Use established libraries (e.g., python-box) for data access and transformation instead of writing manual attribute-mapping code.
When polling for status completion, short-circuit on all terminal states (error, stopped, failed) not just the success state, to avoid infinite loops or wasted resources.
Drive hardcoded values (URLs, endpoints, magic strings) from constants or a config file rather than embedding them inline.
Keep CI pipeline steps minimal by combining related commands into a single step, and always enable shell debug flags (set -exuo pipefail) to aid troubleshooting.
When calling functions with positional boolean arguments, use named variables or code comments to document what each value means.
Model authorization using the existing roles and permissions system rather than ad-hoc conditional checks; create tech debt tickets if refactoring is needed to unblock a release.
Split authorization checks to match the scope of each query — don't require broad permissions when the user only needs access to a narrow subset of data.
Add explicit type annotations to variables holding complex expressions (tuples, mapped collections) to improve readability.
Use case-insensitive comparison (e.g., .lower) for user-facing search and filtering to avoid missed matches.
Follow established project naming conventions (e.g., directory-per-hook) even when the resulting names are long, to maintain codebase consistency.
Use the project's standard shared UI components (e.g., Domino modal) rather than creating custom one-off implementations, to maintain consistency and reduce duplication.
Do not update local state or flags until the corresponding remote/async operation completes successfully, to avoid inconsistent state on failure.
Critical business logic (e.g., cache refresh, data synchronization) must have dedicated tests even when mocking is difficult; find a way to test it rather than skipping coverage.
When adding or changing operational configuration (feature flags, cache TTLs, service parameters), document them in the relevant runbook so on-call engineers can find and adjust them.
Test helper functions that handle failures should call record_failure (or equivalent) so that support bundles and diagnostic artifacts are captured for debugging.
When implementing retry logic for opaque exceptions, match on specific error messages rather than broad exception types to avoid retrying non-transient failures.
Log messages and status labels must use terminology that accurately reflects the operation being performed (e.g., 'Stopped' not 'Delete' when stopping a run).

Example manifests and sample configurations that use permissive security settings (e.g., wide-open NetworkPolicies) should include comments warning that production usage should be more targeted and restricted.
In TypeScript, prefer `unknown` or generic type parameters over `any` to preserve type safety and enable type checking at call sites.
When two methods return the same result, consolidate them into a single method to reduce maintenance burden and confusion.
Do not overload a DTO with fields that conceptually belong to a different entity; place each field on the DTO that owns that concept.
Error messages thrown from lookups should name the entity type and ID so operators can quickly identify what was not found.
When a find-entity-or-throw pattern appears multiple times, extract it into a dedicated `findOrThrow` method on the relevant manager/repository.
When a refactor empties or nearly empties a file, delete it entirely rather than leaving a skeleton behind.
When a chained or nested expression is hard to parse at a glance, assign it to a descriptively-named local variable to improve readability.
When the same few lines of logic appear in multiple places within a service, factor them out into a dedicated method to reduce duplication.
When an Ant Design table column simply renders the field value as-is, rely on `dataIndex` alone and omit the `render` prop.
Styled components should live in dedicated component or styles files, not in utility modules.
Route path constants and link builders should be co-located in the central routes definition, not scattered across utility files.
Function and test step names should include the domain or system they interact with (e.g., 'keycloak') so readers can understand them without surrounding context.
BDD/Cucumber step definitions should be descriptive enough to understand when listed outside their specific test scenario, such as in step catalogs or documentation.
If a function is called only once and there is no reason to expect reuse, inline it at the call site rather than extracting it into a separate function.
Magic values and literals embedded in the middle of functions should be moved to configuration files and referenced from there.
Debug log messages should include the relevant data being operated on (e.g., the config object) so developers can trace behavior without stepping through code.
When mapping a fixed set of values to corresponding outputs (e.g., icons, components), use a dictionary/map rather than if-else chains or switch statements.
Boolean fields and variables should use the 'is' prefix (e.g., isWorkloadServiceExecution) to make their boolean nature immediately clear at the call site.

When iterating over items that require remediation or processing, extract the loop body into its own function with independent error handling so a failure on one item does not prevent processing the rest.
Route components should use lazy loading so they are only fetched when the user navigates to that route, reducing the initial bundle size.
New routes for a feature area should be added to the existing module router rather than creating a parallel navigation entry, to avoid fragmenting routing logic.
In tests, use DOM API methods like getAttribute('href') to read element attributes instead of casting the element to a specific type to access properties directly.
If a function returns the same result on every call and takes no arguments, define its output as a module-level constant to avoid re-creating it on every render.
When a class only needs a subset of a configuration object, accept that narrow type directly instead of depending on the full configuration, to reduce coupling and simplify construction.
Pin third-party dependency versions in package.json to avoid unexpected breaking changes from upstream updates.
Reference color values from the shared colors.ts (or equivalent design token file) instead of hardcoding hex values in components.
Use the project's existing shared components (modals, buttons, etc.) rather than creating ad-hoc replacements, to maintain visual and behavioral consistency across the app.
Extract magic numbers into named constants with meaningful names that explain the value's purpose.
When changing a variable's semantics (e.g., removing patch from a version string), update the variable name to reflect its new meaning.
When a test checks for an error condition, use explicit assertion mechanisms (e.g., pytest.raises) so the test actually fails when the error is absent, rather than silently passing.
Use the project's structured audit event system instead of ad-hoc logger.info calls for actions that need to be auditable, ensuring consistent format and queryability.
Do not add logging to intermediate steps (e.g., per-chunk upload) when the concluding step already logs the complete action; log at the boundary that represents the completed operation.
When renaming a parameter in one file, update the corresponding parameter names in all related interfaces and adapters to maintain consistency across the codebase.
Before adding a new audit event, consider how frequently the triggering action occurs (e.g., every file preview click) and assess whether the logging volume is acceptable for the system.
Use the correct HTTP status code for each operation: 201 for resource creation, 200 for responses with a body, and 204 for delete operations with no response body.
Use 'preview' instead of 'beta' in API path prefixes to align with the organization's established terminology for pre-GA endpoints.
Use PATCH instead of PUT for update operations to support partial updates and avoid requiring clients to send the full resource representation.
Name creation request DTOs using the pattern 'New[EntityName]' (e.g., NewResourceConfiguration) to clearly distinguish them from read models and update DTOs.
Name update request DTOs using the pattern 'Updated[EntityName]' (e.g., UpdatedResourceConfiguration) to clearly distinguish them from creation and read models.
Write OpenAPI operation descriptions in third person (e.g., 'Gets all Deployment Target Types') rather than imperative form (e.g., 'Get all Deployment Target Types').
Reference related entities by their primary key (ID) rather than by name or other mutable fields to avoid synchronization complexity if renaming is ever supported.

When a UI wizard creates a parent entity with its children (e.g., a deployment target with resource configurations), use a single creation endpoint rather than multiple sequential calls to avoid partial success states.
When removing entities that are referenced by other records (e.g., resource configurations referenced by model deployments), archive them instead of deleting to preserve referential integrity.
When modifying an API or data model, take the opportunity to fix misleading names in the same area rather than perpetuating them further.
Maintain separate admin and user-facing API endpoints when they require different field visibility or permission filtering, even if they access the same underlying data.

Ensure OpenAPI spec status codes match the actual runtime behavior of the API framework; if the framework always returns 200, document 200 rather than a theoretically correct but unimplemented status code.
Model child entities as subresources (e.g., /deploymentTargets/{id}/resourceConfigurations) when they need independent CRUD operations, rather than embedding them only in the parent.
Use consistent phrasing and structure across similar API endpoint descriptions in OpenAPI specs.
Update/PATCH endpoints should not mark all fields as required; an update should allow modifying only a subset of fields.
When introducing a new API spec alongside an old one, create a ticket to track the removal of the old spec to prevent indefinite accumulation.
Add debug-level logging to status polling and readiness checks that includes the resource identifier, current count, and expected count.
When a design involves non-obvious resource allocation tradeoffs (e.g., volume sizing, memory splitting), document the behavior clearly for users.
In pattern matching, let redundant or default-behavior cases be covered by the wildcard (_) case instead of listing them explicitly.
Do not hardcode navigation paths inside UI components; define routes in a centralized location and import them.
Before reimplementing behavior manually, check if the component library already provides a prop for it (e.g., destroyInactiveTabPane for tab cleanup).
Before writing new state management logic, check if a shared hook or utility already exists for the pattern (e.g., useSearchParamTabController for URL-synced tabs).
Prefer idiomatic Scala collection operations like `.map(...).toMap` over imperative loop-and-accumulate patterns when building maps from collections.
When filtering and transforming a collection simultaneously, use `.collect` with pattern matching instead of chaining `.filter` and `.map` separately.
Initialize data structures with their intended values at declaration rather than creating empty structures and mutating them afterward.
New external provider integrations (e.g., BitBucket, GitHub) must include system tests that exercise the real API endpoints, not just unit tests with mocks.
Scenario and test names should precisely describe what behavior is under test, not make broader claims the test doesn't actually verify.
Use ES module imports (import/export) instead of CommonJS require; use .mts file extensions when needed to enable ESM syntax in TypeScript.
Before adding a new dependency injection binding, check whether the same binding already exists in another injection module to prevent duplicate registrations.
Methods should live in the service that owns the relevant domain concept — don't add launcher-related logic to a launcher-job service or vice versa.
Class names should clearly communicate their purpose and domain concept; avoid vague, cryptic, or 'weird' names that force readers to inspect the implementation to understand intent.
Add a description comment to entity classes and data structures whose purpose is not immediately obvious from the name, especially integration-specific DTOs.
Order import statements with absolute (third-party/package) imports first, followed by relative (local) imports.
Prefer separate styled components for each element instead of nesting style selectors within a parent styled component.
Use the shorthand <> fragment syntax instead of React.Fragment when no key prop is needed.
Side effects like changing user selection or UI state after an async operation must be placed in the success handler, not unconditionally after the operation completes.
Place components in subdirectories that reflect their domain hierarchy rather than leaving them at the feature root level.
Use JSX expression interpolation instead of the + operator for string concatenation inside JSX templates.
Keep test fixture JSON files trimmed to the minimum data needed for the test scenario rather than using full production-sized responses.
When a match on Option returns None for None and transforms Some, use .map() instead of an explicit pattern match.
E2E test scenarios that create resources (apps, workspaces, etc.) should include cleanup steps to stop or remove them after the test completes.
Before implementing inline utility logic, check for and use existing global helper functions (e.g., getAppName) already available in the codebase.
When updating configuration values for a new version, ensure the same change is applied consistently to all relevant version entries and their defaults.
Always validate YAML files for structural errors like duplicate keys using an IDE or linter before submitting for review.
Do not resolve PR review comments until the underlying issue has actually been fixed in a commit.
Ensure PR descriptions and summaries accurately reflect the actual code changes, including correct names for resources referenced.
Use flexible version constraints (>=) in requirements files rather than compatible release (~=) to avoid breakage when other dependencies or vulnerability fixes require newer versions.
Compute derived boolean values in the component that uses them rather than passing them as props from a parent, unless multiple children need the same value.
Use established package import aliases (e.g., @domino/core-ui/Link) instead of relative paths when importing shared components.
When introducing new environment variables or setup requirements for running tests, document them in the README so developers know what to configure.
When configuration values fail to resolve, make downstream error messages actionable so developers can trace failures back to the missing configuration.
Do not maintain multiple configuration keys that hold the same value; consolidate to a single canonical key used everywhere.
Add descriptive comment sections to test scenarios (e.g., linking to test case IDs) to improve readability and make failure triage easier.
Async data fetches in UI components should handle errors gracefully and show loading feedback (spinner or disabled state) while the request is in flight.
Prefer explicit, typed data contracts (defined case classes/interfaces) over storing arbitrary JSON blobs, as defined contracts are easier to work with in code, tests, and database inspection.
When designing metadata structures for external integrations, capture all available identifying fields even if currently redundant, to avoid breaking changes when future features need them.
Avoid unnecessary intermediate nesting levels in data model field names; prefer flatter, clearer naming when the context is already established by the parent structure.
When a React component's render method grows complex, extract logically distinct sections into dedicated sub-components for better readability and maintainability.
Before writing a new utility or computation, search the codebase for existing utilities that already compute the same value to avoid duplication.
Always use version specifiers (e.g., `>=`) when declaring Python dependencies in requirements files to avoid unexpected breakage from upstream changes.
When modifying a shared test data file or fixture, verify that all other tests depending on that file still pass and are unaffected.
When validating values that cross service boundaries (e.g., Kubernetes quantities), prefer existing SDK/library parsing utilities over custom validation to ensure correctness.
When fixing a bug in a class that lacks tests, create a test file and add tests covering the fix rather than leaving the class untested.
Prefer function-level test fixture scope over session scope unless sharing state across tests is explicitly required; place shared fixtures in a common fixtures file so other tests can discover them.
Route and utility function names should clearly describe their destination or purpose (e.g., `getProjectLauncherPath` instead of a generic name) so callers understand the navigation target.
Use `2>/dev/null` to suppress stderr output silently; do not use `2>&1` when the intent is to discard errors rather than merge them with stdout.
Generate and include a correlation ID (e.g., UUID) in error responses so operators can correlate client-facing errors with server-side log entries for debugging.
When modifying or renaming a shared configuration file, audit and update every service that includes or depends on that file to avoid runtime failures.
Before renaming shared configuration files or resources, get explicit alignment from the domain owners to ensure the new name is semantically correct and agreed upon.
Correct typos in type names, interface names, and other identifiers immediately when discovered to prevent propagation through the codebase.

Do not nest configuration providers inside feature components; instead, use prop composition (e.g., passing a ReactNode to a prop like emptyMessage) to achieve the same behavior without tight coupling.
Use JSDoc-style comments (/** ... */) for component and function documentation so that IDEs can display them in hover hints and autocomplete.
When removing or renaming fields from serialized models, assess whether the change could break serialization compatibility and create a separate ticket to track the migration if so.
Use template literals instead of string concatenation to build strings, and guard against potentially undefined values that would produce 'undefinedsome-str' when concatenated.
Define named constants for magic numbers (e.g., max lengths, limits) so the value can be reused consistently and the intent is self-documenting.
Maintain consistent import ordering: absolute (third-party/package) imports must come before relative imports.
Keep all styled components in a single location per feature — either all in a dedicated styles file or all co-located in the component file, not split across both.
Do not pass empty no-op functions as event handlers when the framework or parent component already handles the behavior (e.g., form item onChange).
When the same logic appears in multiple components, extract it into a single shared function rather than duplicating it.
Repeated string literals (e.g., field names, keys) used in query building or data access should be defined as named constants within the class.
When writing tests for serialization or type conversion logic, include edge case tests that verify correct behavior across type boundaries (e.g., retrieving an integer value as a Double).
When adding boolean props to shared UI components, make them optional with a default value of false so callers only need to pass them when opting in.
When adding logging inside nested or helper functions, verify the logger is within scope and actually producing output, as loggers invoked inside nested functions can silently fail.
Remove all commented-out code before submitting a PR; do not leave dead code in the codebase as it creates noise and confusion about intent.
Do not wrap child components in a render function unless the parent is actually injecting props through it; use the component directly as a child instead.
Never suppress or skip required dependencies in useEffect; if a function is used inside the effect, define it inside the effect body or properly declare it as a dependency.
Every async operation that can fail (API calls, form submissions) must have user-visible error feedback; never silently swallow failures.
When using Ant Design Form components, let the Form manage field state rather than using it purely as a layout/styling wrapper with separate state management.
Do not wrap components in React fragments when there is only a single child element; fragments are only needed to group multiple sibling elements without adding a DOM node.
If a function does not reference any variables computed inside a React component (props, state, hooks), define it outside the component to avoid unnecessary re-creation on each render.
In tests, prefer using waitFor or similar async utilities to assert state changes rather than hardcoded setTimeout delays, which are flaky and slow.
Use React hooks (e.g., useHistory) to access framework-provided values instead of passing them through component props.
Before building new UI elements, check if an existing shared component (e.g., HelpLink) already provides the needed functionality.
New admin UI entries and user-facing features must be gated behind a feature flag before merging.
Implement UI and styling in React components rather than server-side Scala templates to keep rendering logic consistent and maintainable.
When using well-known specifications (e.g., JSON Schema meta-schemas), reference the official spec rather than creating a custom copy.
When adding advanced OpenAPI schema features (e.g., additionalProperties with complex types), verify they are supported by the project's code generation tooling.
API schema fields with a known set of valid values should use an enum rather than a freeform string type.
API schema descriptions should be detailed and include examples so that complex structures are easy to understand without reading the implementation.
When removing a property from an API schema, also remove it from the required array to avoid invalid specs.
In meta-schemas (schemas that define schemas), avoid field names like 'description' or 'type' that conflict with the actual data fields they constrain, as this creates ambiguity.
API specifications should define the minimal contract; put validation logic and known-constant enforcement in application code rather than embedding it in the spec.
When an API spec represents a collection of schemas, model it as an array with typed items rather than nesting objects with redundant wrapper properties.
When a type field in an API schema has a known fixed value, constrain it with an enum declaration to prevent invalid values.

When handling process termination or state transitions, branch on the specific signal that caused the transition (e.g., stop properties being set) rather than the current broad state, to avoid catching unrelated exit reasons.
If a React component prop is always passed by every caller, declare it as required rather than optional to accurately represent the component's contract.
Test step definitions should be explicit about what they do and named intuitively so other engineers will discover and reuse them; avoid combining unrelated behaviors into a single opaque step.

Prefer explicit constructor parameters over Scala implicits because implicit constructors create hidden nullary constructors that cause surprising instantiation errors and obscure the dependency graph.
Follow the project's established import ordering convention to keep files consistent and diffs clean.
When multiple authorization paths perform nearly identical permission checks, combine them into a single code path to reduce duplication and prevent drift.
When modifying role-based access controls, explicitly verify with the product manager which user tiers (e.g., lite users) should or should not have access to the affected endpoints.
Use React's useId() hook to generate stable, unique IDs for DOM elements instead of manual ID generation or random strings.
In end-to-end tests, ensure the user performing an action and the entity being tested are not the same or co-located, to avoid false positives from implicit permissions.

When adding configuration values to a service, verify whether existing config-fetching code could pull and override those values from a remote source like Nucleus, and ensure the override precedence is correct.
Use the framework's default timeout values in tests unless there is a specific, documented reason to override them; arbitrary custom timeouts add unnecessary divergence.
Use useMemo to create a debounced handler and useCallback for the onChange, rather than syncing debounced values through useEffect.
Maintain consistent import ordering with absolute imports grouped before relative imports.
When a conditional structure checks against multiple discrete values and is likely to grow, use an array with .includes() instead of if/else or switch chains.
Do not add explicit cleanup steps in tests when the test setup or fixture already handles that cleanup automatically.
API endpoint implementations should live in the shared @domino/api package rather than being defined inline in feature modules.
Index files should only re-export from other modules; do not define function implementations directly in index files.
When a type is used across module boundaries, export it from the shared package rather than re-defining or importing it from an internal path.
When a user action triggers multiple sequential API calls, the UI must indicate that work is in progress so the user knows something is happening.
Modals should call generic callbacks (onClose, onSuccess) provided by the parent, not directly trigger side effects like data refetching that are the parent's concern.
Never use explicit `any` as a type annotation when TypeScript can infer the type automatically; rely on type inference instead.
When modifying a handler or function used by multiple callers, check existing integration/e2e tests that cover all call sites before merging.

Move generic utility code (e.g., AWS client wrappers) into common shared modules rather than domain-specific packages; remove domain-specific dependencies (like config objects) in favor of primitive parameters to keep the code reusable.
Name wrapper types using consistent Request/Response pairs — if input types are named 'Request', output types should be named 'Response' (not 'Result') to match SDK v2 conventions.
When wrapping exceptions across SDK versions, define proper custom exceptions that set both message and cause on the parent Throwable so stack traces remain useful for debugging.
Do not include code in a PR that cannot be validated without the context of a future PR; defer it so reviewers can assess correctness with full context.
API and wrapper methods should return typed data structures rather than pre-serialized strings; let callers decide how to format or log the data. If the return value is not needed, return Unit.
When the same conversion or transformation logic (e.g., safe number parsing that prevents NaN) is repeated multiple times, extract it into a shared utility function.
When adding new event types to an existing set, follow the established naming convention of the existing events (e.g., if existing events use 'Change' suffix, don't switch to 'Updated' or 'Edit').

Before adding authentication redirects or route guards to a React component, verify the component's mounting lifecycle — if it is always mounted while the app is active, the guard may unintentionally affect unrelated routes.
Always roll back temporary or unstable package versions and use a stable develop version before merging a PR.
Use static Object methods like Object.keys().forEach() instead of calling prototype methods directly on objects.
Only include dependencies that are actually used inside the useEffect callback; extra dependencies cause unnecessary re-runs and potential bugs.
Order import statements so that absolute imports come before relative imports.
Use themeHelper tokens (e.g., themeHelper('margins.tiny')) for margins and spacing instead of hardcoded values.
Ensure all useEffect dependency arrays are complete and correct; missing deps should produce linting errors that must be resolved, not suppressed.
Let API utility functions throw exceptions and handle errors with try/catch or .catch() at the call site rather than passing onSuccess/onError callback parameters.
API request functions should not handle error notification (e.g., console.error or toast); error display is the responsibility of the calling component or hook.
When catching errors from API calls, consider whether the user needs to be informed through UI feedback (e.g., a toast or inline message), not just a console.error.
Replace nested ternary expressions with if/else blocks, early returns, or helper functions for better readability.
Move constant values outside of component functions and name them using UPPER_SNAKE_CASE (e.g., MAX_RETRY_COUNT).
Give meaningful names to numeric literals so their purpose is clear without additional context.
Add documentation to functions that use domain-specific numbers or non-obvious logic, explaining what the values represent and what the function returns.
When a function's return type is explicitly declared, avoid redundant type casts on the returned value — the type annotation already provides type safety.
When a React component has nothing to render, return `null` instead of an empty or meaningless DOM element like `<span />`.
When using styled-components, define all styles in the styled component definition rather than using inline `style` props — this keeps styling centralized and consistent.
In JSX, pass string literal props without curly braces — use `prop="value"` instead of `prop={"value"}`.
Prefer built-in array methods like `.some()` over manual loops or reduce patterns when checking a condition across array elements.
Keep utility and helper functions in dedicated utility files rather than defining them inside component files.
Move constant values and static derived data outside the component body to avoid recomputing them on every render.
Define colors in the project's centralized color constants file (e.g., `colors.ts`) rather than hardcoding hex values in components.

When using styled-components, do not use `className` for styling — use styled-component composition instead. Reserve `className` as a last resort for deeply nested third-party components you cannot reach otherwise.
When the same style override appears in multiple views, extract it into the shared component that owns the styling rather than duplicating it across consumers.
When a piece of UI (like a modal or dialog) is defined inline within another component, extract it into its own component file and render it from the parent.

When adding ScalaDoc to a method, document all parameters and include a summary of what the method does — partial documentation is worse than none because it implies the undocumented parts are unimportant.

If a method mutates a builder or performs side effects and callers discard the return value, the return type should be Unit — a non-Unit return type implies a pure transformation the caller should use.
Write test names as human-readable behavioral descriptions (e.g., 'should return empty list when no items match') rather than mirroring method names (e.g., 'should testGetItems').
Data wrapper or result types should not extend Exception or RuntimeException unless they are genuinely intended to be thrown as exceptions.
When a function may not handle all input cases, return a functional error type like Try or Either instead of throwing exceptions for unmatched cases.
Cross-cutting dependencies like ExecutionContext that are already available in the dependency graph should be taken as implicit constructor parameters rather than passed explicitly at each call site.
When a method returns a generic type (like String) that represents domain-specific content, add documentation explaining what the returned value represents.
When consolidating similar exception handling branches during refactoring, verify with the original author or through analysis that the differences between branches were not intentional.
Integration tests should use real implementations (e.g., real persisters and repositories), not mocks; mocking in integration tests defeats their purpose and reduces coverage of real interactions.
Capitalize domain-specific terms (e.g., Dataset, ID) and proper abbreviations in client-facing error messages and API route descriptions for consistency and professionalism.
API controllers should include a catch-all exception handler that returns a 500 status code to prevent unhandled exceptions from leaking implementation details.
When migrating between SDK versions, verify that string representations and serialized outputs match between old and new versions, especially if those outputs are consumed downstream.
Include a `DeletedAt` (or equivalent) timestamp field in entities that support soft deletion, so deletions are auditable and reversible.
All CRUD service methods should include telemetry events for observability; plan telemetry from the start even if it ships in a follow-up PR.
CRUD integration tests should include explicit authorization test cases to verify that unauthorized users cannot access or modify protected resources.
Add a comment explaining why a value is intentionally discarded (e.g., `val _ = ...` in Scala) so future developers do not accidentally remove the side-effecting call.
A field's name must accurately reflect the type of value it holds — e.g., if the value is a resource name, the field should be called `pvName`, not `pvId`.
When a field's context already implies its type, use the shorter name to reduce redundancy (e.g., `pvcName` instead of `persistentVolumeClaimName` when inside a PVC-related object).
Always capture the return value of `setTimeout` (the timeout ID) and use it with `clearTimeout` for proper cleanup; cancel the timeout when the async operation completes.
Define static constants (values that never change between renders) outside the React component body to avoid unnecessary re-creation on every re-render.
Replace magic numbers with named constants that convey the value's meaning and make future changes easier.
Configuration files of the same kind (e.g., ESLint configs) should use a consistent file format and extension across all packages in a monorepo.
Magic numbers and arbitrary constants must include a comment explaining how the value was chosen and what it relates to.
Every TODO comment in the codebase must reference a Jira ticket so the work is tracked and not forgotten.
PRs should only contain changes related to the ticket or feature described in the title; unrelated modifications should be split into separate PRs.
Debug print statements (println, console.log, etc.) must be removed or replaced with proper logging before a PR is merged.
Enum values in Scala should follow camelCase naming convention to stay consistent with the codebase style.
When changing serialization formats, markup patterns, or data structures, verify that existing persisted data will still be correctly interpreted after the change.
When setting multiple fields on the same form or state object, combine them into a single call rather than making multiple sequential calls.
When renaming or reformatting identifiers, apply the same convention uniformly across all related files to avoid inconsistency.
When catching exceptions, always preserve the original exception as the cause; never silently swallow or discard exception information.
Within a single test scenario, use a consistent interaction approach (UI or API) rather than mixing both without justification.
When introducing new console methods or APIs, verify they pass the project's ESLint rules before submitting the PR.
When a new UI pattern (like a status tag) is introduced in a feature module, extract it as a reusable component in the shared packages/ui library.
When a test library target (e.g., tests-lib) already bundles common dependencies like the core library and test frameworks, do not re-declare those dependencies in consuming BUILD targets.
When filtering, sorting, and pagination logic is duplicated across modules, extract it into a shared commons library and have modules extend it rather than re-implementing it.
Do not use unexplained magic numbers in code; when a reviewer has to ask 'why this number?', the value should be a named constant or accompanied by a comment explaining its derivation.
API route parameter definitions should include $ref or enum constraints to enable automatic API request validation rather than accepting unvalidated strings.
API method signatures should use typed enums (e.g., SortableColumn) instead of raw String parameters, and the enum should be referenced in API docs for validation.
Scala imports should follow a consistent ordering convention (java/javax first, then scala standard library, then project imports) and group related imports together.
Test specs should not contain unused test fixtures, setup data, or helper objects; clean up any test data that is no longer exercised by assertions.
Write Storybook stories in CSF3 format, not MDX, which is no longer supported in Storybook 8.
Extract complex sub-renders and inline logic into separate components or pure functions to improve testability and readability.
Avoid nested ternary expressions in JSX; use early returns, variables, or helper functions for complex conditionals.
Use styled-component props for dynamic styling instead of toggling CSS class names.
Use semantic color tokens from the theme palette (e.g., colorPalette.textPrimary, colorPalette.bg) instead of raw color values or hardcoded hex codes.
Import colors from the canonical colors.ts file, not from alternative color files, to ensure theme switching works correctly.
Components should reference the dynamic color palette rather than implementing hardcoded dark/light theme branches.
Use Ant Design's data-driven items array pattern instead of deprecated JSX children for Menu and similar components.
Leverage the base component library's built-in props and APIs (e.g., icon prop on Ant Design SubMenu) rather than re-implementing equivalent functionality.
Provide sensible default values for component props (e.g., orientation defaults to horizontal) while allowing callers to override via props.
Component prop types should extend or pick from the base library (Ant Design) types rather than defining loose, unconstrained prop interfaces.
Style customizations should go through the Ant Design Theme Provider rather than manually overriding component styles in styled-components or CSS.
Use the themeHelper utility for accessing theme values in styled-components instead of hardcoding values or accessing the theme object directly.
Always define theme keys for both light and dark modes explicitly, even when the values are identical, to support future design divergence.
Always reference colors from the theme palette rather than hardcoding hex values or using colors that may not exist in the current theme.
Import UI components from Domino's design system wrappers, not directly from the antd package.
Do not use useMemo or React.memo unless wrapping an expensive computation; unnecessary memoization adds complexity without performance benefit.
Use spacing and dimension tokens from the theme instead of raw CSS units (rem, px) so that layout values stay consistent with the design system.
Prefer async/await with try/catch over .then/.catch promise chains for consistency across the codebase.
Shared route definitions should live in a centralized package location (e.g., packages/ui/src/core/routes) rather than within individual app modules.
Use proper TypeScript generics on utility methods like reduce() to eliminate the need for type assertions/casting downstream.
Mock data should use realistic example values that reflect the actual shape and format of real data (e.g., realistic IDs) to make tests and development more representative.
Use themeHelper utilities for font-size, margin, and other styling values instead of hardcoding them directly in styled components.
Remove commented-out code before merging; do not leave dead code in the codebase.
Replace switch statements or if-else chains that map values to strings with a dictionary/map object for cleaner, more maintainable code.
Avoid inline styles in React components; use styled components or CSS classes instead.
Favor template literal string interpolation over string concatenation for readability and consistency.
Use union types of string literals with the UnionToMap utility instead of TypeScript enums.
Do not commit secrets, tokens, or credentials to version control; use a secrets management solution instead.
Import from canonical module paths rather than deprecated aliases to avoid breakage when aliases are removed.
Maintain consistent casing conventions for identifiers across a codebase (e.g., if existing code uses PascalCase like CustomSVG, new related identifiers should follow the same convention).
Components that consume global store via hooks like useStore should be memoized to prevent unnecessary re-renders.
Use a dictionary or mapping for aria-label values so the same icon always gets the same label, ensuring consistency for QA automation selectors.
API parameter documentation should enumerate the allowed or expected values, not just describe the parameter abstractly.
When adding fields to API response DTOs, verify that all downstream clients are tolerant of extra fields before merging.
Never import from a package's own dist/build output folder; use relative paths within the same package.
Use color values from the design system palette (e.g., colors.palette.danger) instead of hardcoded hex values in styled components.
When multiple async requests are independent of each other, use Promise.all to execute them in parallel instead of sequentially.

Use the same domain terminology at all layers of the application (ubiquitous language from DDD); don't invent new names for concepts that already have established terms in documentation and user-facing surfaces.
Configuration settings should be grouped with their related domain module, not added to a monolithic Settings class.
Use the structured logging framework (logger) instead of raw println statements, even for temporary debugging output.
When a file format or technology is no longer supported (e.g., MDX in Storybook), remove the files rather than refactoring them into the deprecated format.
Don't repeat parent context in nested configuration key names; if a setting is already scoped under 'workflows.storage', don't prefix its children with 'flows' or 'storage' again.
Avoid inline styles in React components; use styled-components, CSS modules, or Ant Design's component props for styling.
Before creating custom UI elements, check if an existing component in the shared library (e.g., ActionDropdown.Button) already provides the needed functionality.
Do not render a separate modal instance for every table row; use a single shared modal at the table level and pass the selected row's data to it.
When a function name collides with or closely resembles an existing function in the codebase, use a more specific name that conveys scope, version, or return type to avoid confusion.
When accessing properties on array elements or objects that may be undefined, use optional chaining (?.) with nullish coalescing (?? '') instead of assuming the value exists.
Use Array.reduce() or other functional methods instead of declaring mutable variables with let and imperatively building up values.
Do not import from your own package's published name (e.g., @domino/ui) within that same package; use relative imports instead.
Do not copy data from a custom hook (e.g., useRemote) into local state via useEffect; use the hook's returned data directly to avoid unnecessary re-renders and state synchronization bugs.
When a function argument is unused, remove it from the function signature rather than adding an eslint-disable comment to suppress the warning.
When a component receives data from a single object, pass the object as one prop rather than spreading its fields, to keep the component's prop interface simple and explicit.

Component props should be typed so that downstream consumers do not need to cast values; if a prop is expected to have a specific type, define it in the component's prop interface rather than casting at the call site.
Place TypeScript type and interface definitions in a dedicated types file (e.g., types.ts), not in a utils file which should contain runtime logic.
Avoid adding eslint-disable comments when the code can be written in a way that satisfies the lint rule naturally; fix the code rather than silencing the linter.
If code must be left commented out, add a TODO comment explaining why it is retained and under what conditions it should be restored or removed.
Never mutate backend-fetched data when applying client-side filters; store the original data as an immutable source of truth and compute filtered views from it using separate filter state.
When adding try/catch blocks to prevent crashes, also provide user-visible feedback (e.g., a toast or error message) so the user knows something went wrong rather than silently swallowing the error.
Always use strict equality (===) instead of loose equality (==) to avoid type coercion bugs, unless there is an explicit reason for loose comparison.
If a component is no longer used and there are no planned additions, delete it rather than leaving dead code in the codebase.
Use the nullish coalescing operator (??) instead of logical OR (||) for default values when only null/undefined should trigger the fallback.
Pure API call functions should throw errors and let the caller handle error messaging; API layers should have no concern about user-facing error messages.
Components that can be used globally should be moved to shared packages (e.g., packages/ui) with proper Storybook documentation rather than kept in feature-specific modules.
When the same placeholder or sentinel value appears in multiple places, extract it into a named constant (e.g., EMPTY_PLACEHOLDER) and reuse it across related utility functions.
Always use themeHelper or theme token values for colors and fonts in styled components instead of hardcoded hex values or raw numbers.
Style definitions should reference design system theme values rather than hardcoded numbers, even when maintaining existing code.
Keep import statements sorted in a consistent order within each file.
Avoid using negative margins in CSS; prefer restructuring layout with proper spacing, padding, or flexbox/grid alignment instead.
When using batched MongoDB queries with $in, document the chosen batch size, the reasoning behind it, and the implicit BSON document size limits that constrain it.
When an API schema field has implicit constraints (e.g., nesting depth limits), document those constraints explicitly in the schema description everywhere the assumption applies.
API schema definitions should include concrete examples to clarify expected structure and values for consumers.
Use `unknown` instead of `any` in TypeScript to preserve type safety and force explicit type narrowing before use.
Remove code that is no longer needed rather than leaving it in place; do not keep dead code on the assumption it might be useful later.
When a field accepts multiple value types (e.g., not just strings), add test cases covering non-string and non-default value types to ensure correct handling.

A class name must accurately reflect its actual behavior; if a class named 'Logger' also enforces authorization or throws exceptions, rename it to reflect its broader responsibility (e.g., 'Checker' or 'Enforcer').
Log messages should include contextual identifiers (e.g., controller name, operation name) and use trace-level logging before and after a block to aid debugging.
When making API fields optional, write at least two tests: one that modifies all optional fields and one that modifies none, to verify correct behavior at both boundaries.
Maintain consistent formatting conventions (e.g., punctuation, capitalization) across method descriptions and documentation comments within a codebase.
Include example values (e.g., example hostnames, URLs) in API documentation and configuration descriptions to help consumers understand expected formats.
Use theme helper functions for styling values like font sizes and colors instead of hardcoding raw pixel values in component files.
Do not use explicit `return` statements in Scala; rely on expression-based control flow as the language intends.
When a component file contains multiple distinct components or variants (e.g., controlled and uncontrolled versions), split them into separate files.
Define default callback functions and static helper functions outside the React component body to prevent unnecessary re-creation on each render.
Use `unknown` instead of `any` for TypeScript type parameters when the values are not actually used, to preserve type safety.
Extract repeated test setup logic (e.g., creating test fixtures like unauthorized principals) into reusable helper methods to reduce duplication in test files.
When caller already has the needed data (e.g., a Principal), pass it directly to downstream services rather than forcing a redundant database or API lookup.

Prefer `find -exec rm -rf {} +` over piping `find -print0 | xargs -0 rm -rf` for batch file deletion — it's simpler and faster, and always use `-mindepth 1` to protect the parent directory from deletion.
Order import statements so that absolute (package/library) imports appear before relative imports.
Reuse existing route/path generator functions from shared utility modules instead of hardcoding URL paths inline; export them if not already exported.
When constructing URLs from variables, validate that all interpolated values are defined before building the URL to avoid generating broken paths like `/u/undefined/undefined/browse-git`.
Name methods to precisely reflect their behavior — a method that updates a resource should not be named `create`, and a method that creates or updates should be named `createOrUpdate`.

When soft-deleting entities that have associated secrets or credentials, explicitly decide whether those credentials should be purged or retained, since users may expect their stored credentials are fully removed when they delete a resource.
When suppressing compiler or linter warnings, target the specific warning being suppressed rather than using broad suppression annotations.
Extract interactions with external services (e.g., Vault, secret stores) into separate classes rather than mixing them into the main service implementation, for better organization and testability.
Mock data used in tests or storybook should be typed using the types generated from the API schema, not left untyped or manually typed.
Remove all debug identifiers, test strings, and temporary values (e.g., hardcoded test IDs) from code before submitting a PR.

When a value can be computed from existing state or props, calculate it directly (or use `useMemo`) instead of synchronizing it via `useEffect` + `useState`, which adds unnecessary complexity and re-renders.

When a form renderer uses a switch/case or conditional to render different input types, extract each form control type into its own component to keep the renderer manageable as new input types are added.
Never reference color variables directly in component styles; always consume colors through the theme helper to ensure consistency and support theming.
When a manual conversion pattern is repeated (e.g., enum to constants), create a utility function to automate it rather than doing it one by one.
Use common code comments or markers to flag configuration lines that must be updated when cutting a release branch, so they are easy to find and not missed.
Name parameters explicitly to convey their meaning — e.g., use `pageNumber` instead of a vague abbreviation like `page` when the context could be ambiguous.
When an operation requires a record to exist and its absence would be a logic error, use a get-or-create pattern instead of a plain get that can fail.
When adding a lifecycle state transition method like `pause`, always provide its complement (e.g., `resume`) rather than overloading an existing method like `start`.
When an operation can fail for multiple similar reasons, the error message should specify which state caused the failure rather than being vague.
If a feature (like a lease holder check) was designed for a use case that doesn't exist in the current PR, remove it to reduce complexity.

Do not enable background services (watchers, schedulers, periodic jobs) in all pod types when they are only needed in a specific one (e.g., dispatcher) — this avoids unnecessary k8s API load, heap/CPU waste, and future side-effect multiplication bugs.
When watching Kubernetes resources, use field selectors to limit the watch to only the specific resources you need, reducing memory footprint and API load.
When state can be accessed by multiple threads, add explicit synchronization — protect both reads and writes using a dedicated lock object or volatile + synchronized pattern.
When protecting shared mutable state with a lock, synchronize on both read and write paths — a write lock alone does not prevent stale reads from other threads.
When creating a resource that must be cross-referenced in another system, create the primary record first to obtain its ID before creating the secondary record that references it.
When constructing or logging domain objects, place the entity ID as the first parameter (or immediately after the creator) so the most important identifier is immediately visible.
Prefix class names with the specific domain or subsystem they belong to (e.g., IdpUserReconciler instead of UserReconciler) to disambiguate purpose and avoid collisions.

When parsing strings into structured types (e.g., UUIDs, URIs, enums), validate the format at the point of parsing and handle failures explicitly rather than letting them propagate as unexpected runtime errors.
Delete code that is not actively used instead of leaving it in the codebase undocumented; unused code adds maintenance burden and confusion without providing value.

When introducing polling or periodic checks against external services, expose the interval as a configurable parameter (e.g., via Central Config) rather than hardcoding it, enabling tuning without redeployment.
When adding periodic or synchronous calls to external services, consider whether requests can queue up and block other operations; apply timeouts to bound the impact.
When exposing a new endpoint or service method, explicitly verify that appropriate authentication and authorization checks are in place, even when adapting code from existing patterns.
When suppressing deprecation or compiler warnings in build files, document which specific deprecations are being silenced and why, so reviewers and future maintainers understand the trade-off.
Do not merge pull requests that point package dependencies at custom or feature branches; all dependencies must reference stable, published versions before landing.
Use configurable white-label product names instead of hardcoding the product name in UI components.
Before adding a dependency, verify it is not deprecated and follow the recommended migration path if it is.
Before adding a dependency, verify it supports the project's framework version (e.g., React 18) and do not add packages that lack support.
In Vite apps, use import.meta.env.MODE to check the application mode instead of process.env.NODE_ENV, and compare with strict equality.
Use dynamic import() instead of require() for conditional module loading in ES module codebases.
When adding new ESLint rules, start them as warnings rather than errors to avoid forcing developers to use --no-verify or add excessive exceptions.
Component prop types must accurately reflect which props are actually used; either Pick the forwarded props or use the rest operator to pass through unhandled ones.
Use styled-components instead of inline styles for component styling.
Extract sub-components (e.g., tab contents) into their own files early, even if they are small, to prevent a single file from growing unwieldy.
Do not declare the same utility, constant, or type in multiple locations; consolidate into a single shared definition.
Define styled-components in a dedicated styled.ts file rather than co-locating them in the component file.
Use NavTabs for full-page tab navigation and Segmented controls only for toggling between views within a section.
Use Ant Design's Flex component for layout instead of creating custom divs with flexbox styles.
When a feature flag controls visibility of a component in one view, ensure it is consistently applied in all other views that render the same component.
Use optional chaining (?.) combined with nullish coalescing (??) to safely access nested properties and provide fallback values instead of verbose null checks.
TypeScript utility and helper files should follow camelCase naming conventions (e.g., getTheme.ts, not get-theme.ts) to match the project's frontend naming standards.
Color tokens should be used according to their semantic meaning — do not repurpose a token like 'color-link' to style non-link elements; use the semantically correct token (e.g., 'primary') instead.
When a React component contains self-contained logic (e.g., side effects, state management), extract it into a separate custom hook for better separation of concerns and reusability.
Remove commented-out code before merging — version control preserves history, so dead code should not live in the active codebase.
Do not include files that are not imported or referenced anywhere in the codebase; remove dead files to keep the project clean.
Remove outdated version numbers from file and component names when they no longer reflect the current product version, to avoid confusion.
Instead of using 'any', leverage TypeScript utility types like ReturnType<typeof fn> to derive proper types and maintain type safety.
Package.json dependencies must not reference custom or feature branches when merging to develop/main — always point to published versions or the main branch.
API specification fields should include example values to clarify expected formats and usage, especially for non-obvious types like stringified values or comma-delimited arrays.
API schemas should not define the same concept (e.g., 'type') in multiple places; consolidate to a single source of truth to avoid ambiguity.
Do not wrap JSX in React.Fragment (or <Fragment>) when a shorthand fragment (<>) or no wrapper at all suffices.
Default values and initial state objects should be extracted to named constants or a shared constants file rather than inlined in components.
When a result is unexpectedly absent or unknown (e.g., a missing exit code), default to failure rather than assuming success to avoid silently passing broken states.
Use modern React Router hooks instead of deprecated higher-order components like withRouter.
When creating similar page variants, explicitly evaluate whether duplicated components or a single component with conditionals is the better trade-off before proceeding.
Functions with multiple parameters should accept a single options object instead of positional arguments to avoid ordering errors and improve readability.
All form fields should have explicit name attributes to ensure proper form state management, validation, and accessibility.
Use a single Ant Design form instance to manage related form state and validation rather than creating multiple form instances or tracking values in component state.
Before creating a new custom hook, check whether an existing hook in the codebase already handles the same concern (e.g., URL-synced tab state).
Before defining new enums or type constants, check if an equivalent type already exists in the codebase and reuse it.
API endpoint summaries and descriptions should accurately describe what the endpoint does, not use vague or misleading language.
Boolean-returning methods should use names that clearly convey both the condition being checked and its specificity (e.g., `areMultipleStoragesRegistered` instead of ambiguous alternatives).
When you only need to know the count of records, use a COUNT query at the persistence layer instead of fetching full objects into memory.
Do not leave commented-out code in the codebase; remove it before merging since version control preserves history.
Always consume colors from the theme (or palette) rather than importing them directly from a color constants file, so that theming and rebranding propagate automatically.
Use a <button> element (not an <a> or link) when the element triggers an action rather than navigating to a URL; if the link-styled button pattern recurs, create a dedicated button variant.
When a component is a specialization of another, place it in its own file rather than co-locating it inside the base component's file.
Do not leave commented-out code in the codebase; delete it and rely on version control to recover it if needed.
When a JSX conditional involves multiple chained expressions, extract them into a descriptively named boolean variable defined before the return statement to improve readability.
When a function's behavior is extended (e.g., adding a filter parameter), update its doc comment to reflect the new functionality.

Use consistent casing and spacing in API route descriptions and doc comments — match the variable name's word boundaries (e.g., 'data plane' not 'dataplane') and capitalize acronyms like 'ID' consistently.
When adding a new filtering or query parameter to an existing endpoint, add integration or unit tests that verify the filtering behavior.
When a component is used across multiple sections or modules, move it to a shared package (e.g., a common UI library) rather than importing across section boundaries.
When a parent already manages a form field's state, use the `value` prop instead of `defaultValue` to make the component fully controlled and avoid needing to sync state manually.
Components consumed by multiple modules should be placed in packages/ui so that no single module owns a shared dependency.
Extract repeated expressions into utility functions and avoid nested ternary expressions for readability.
Group related data-fetching and transformation logic in one place rather than scattering it across the function, to improve readability.
Do not wrap lightweight operations in useMemo or useCallback; inline them in JSX and use a simple variable if the expression is reused.
When adding log-and-rethrow error handling, investigate whether a higher-level caller is swallowing exceptions and fix the root cause rather than only improving logging.
Replace unused variables with underscore (_) to signal intent and avoid confusion about whether the value is needed.
Avoid nesting CSS class selectors inside styled components; apply styles directly to the styled component definition instead.
Use styled components instead of inline styles for consistency and to keep styling concerns in one place.
Extract non-trivial filter or transformation functions into separate utility modules for reuse and testability.
When theme values are available through the theme provider or feature flags, use them directly instead of reading from localStorage.
When declaring @font-face rules, set font-weight to match the actual weight of the font file so the browser selects the correct face.
When a magic value (e.g., version string) is used across multiple files, extract it into a single shared constant rather than duplicating literals.
Name functions to match how their return value is used at call sites; if callers assign the result to `currentVersion`, the function should be named `getCurrentThemeVersion`, not something ambiguous.
When creating theme or layout components scoped to a specific release version, include the version identifier in the component name (e.g., `AppPanelThemeRelease5`) for clarity.
All touched React files should include an explicit `import React from 'react'` for consistency across the codebase.
Extract complex inline JSX expressions or conditional rendering blocks into named variables (e.g., `mainContent`) to improve readability.
Avoid creating multiple similar DTOs (e.g., StorageDto, StorageInfoDto, StorageViewDto) when a single DTO with an additional field would be simpler and clearer.
When exposing data that may vary by user permissions, explicitly verify whether unauthenticated or non-user callers should have access, and flag any security concerns in review.
Name map/dictionary variables to indicate what the key represents (e.g., `dataPlaneHwTierMap` when the key is a data plane ID) so readers understand the lookup semantics at a glance.
When a collection is filtered by the requesting user's permissions, encode that in the variable or field name (e.g., `accessibleHardwareTiers` instead of just `hardwareTiers`).
Order imports so that absolute (library/package) imports appear before relative imports for consistent readability.
Consume colors from the theme provider rather than hardcoding hex values, especially once the design system's color palette is available.
Move non-trivial inline calculations in components to a utility function to keep component code focused on rendering.
Avoid using `let` with subsequent value overwriting; instead, extract the logic into a function that returns the final value to promote immutability and clarity.
When consuming a stream of events that each triggers a database write, consider batching writes (e.g., update after N events or within a time window) to reduce write churn.

Name database collections, queues, and other shared resources with specific prefixes that identify the subsystem (e.g., 'mongoevents_' instead of generic 'events_') to avoid ambiguity when multiple subsystems use similar concepts.

Add comments explaining why retry or reconnection loops exist, including the failure scenarios they handle (e.g., cursor closed due to node failover), so future developers don't accidentally remove them.
Use theme helper functions and design tokens for spacing, colors, and typography instead of hardcoded pixel values or raw color references, even when the exact value isn't available in the token set.
React components that render JSX should live in their own component files, not inside utility modules — even if they started small.
Route paths like '/admin' should not be hardcoded in component files; use constants or a route configuration to keep navigation paths centralized and maintainable.
All values used inside a useMemo (or useCallback) computation must appear in its dependency array; do not force re-renders by adding unrelated dependencies that the memo body does not consume.

React hooks must only be called from React function components or custom hooks — never from plain utility functions, as this violates the Rules of Hooks and can cause crashes when hook call order changes at runtime.

If a file exports a React component, name it as a component (PascalCase, no 'Render' prefix or 'Util' suffix); file names should reflect what the module actually is, not how it was originally conceived.

When a theme function already receives colors or tokens as parameters, use those parameters instead of importing and referencing the color constants directly, to ensure theme overrides and dark mode work correctly.
CSS for React components should be co-located with the component (CSS modules, styled-components, or CSS-in-JS), not placed in global stylesheets, unless it genuinely applies globally.

Call React hooks at the top level of a component and assign results to variables before using them in arrays, objects, or other data structures — never invoke hooks inline within data structure definitions.

When an error occurs in the UI, don't just display an error message — design a recovery path such as redirecting to an error page or offering a retry action, and coordinate with UX on the appropriate user experience.
Business logic such as validation and URI construction belongs in the domain layer, not the infrastructure layer.
Remove println and other debug print statements before merging; use proper logging instead.
Choose the exception type that most accurately describes the underlying error condition rather than using a generic or loosely related exception.
Before implementing ad-hoc solutions for cross-cutting concerns like obfuscation or redaction, check for and reuse existing platform utilities and patterns.
Do not explicitly assign a property to its default value; it adds noise without changing behavior.
Place entity classes in packages that reflect their source or ownership — entities retrieved from an external API belong in the upstream package, not alongside internal domain entities.
Use named parameters consistently within a file — either always include them or always omit them for the same function calls.
When computing derived values from optional fields, handle the case where the field is not set to avoid inconsistent or misleading results.
Before creating a new class, check if an identical or equivalent class already exists in another module and reuse or consolidate it to avoid duplication.
Consider caching responses from service calls that return data that changes infrequently, even if call volume is currently low, to protect against future scaling.
Keep CSS class selectors flat rather than nesting them; nested classes increase specificity and make styles harder to override and maintain.
When working on migration tasks, verify that files already migrated are not accidentally reverted to their pre-migration state.
When a component supports props like color and fontSize directly, pass values through props rather than wrapping the component in a Styled component.
Order import statements with absolute (library/package) imports first, followed by relative imports.
Express sizes in px rather than em unless there is a specific reason for relative sizing; the codebase convention is px.
Use the project's spacing utilities (e.g., getMargins, themeHelper) for margin and padding values instead of hardcoding inline styles.
Name styled components and wrappers to accurately describe what they render; a component named 'Span' should not render an icon.
Reference semantic theme tokens (e.g., palette.borderLight) instead of hardcoding hex values or raw CSS color strings.
When assigning colors to components, verify that the color matches the intended semantic meaning in the current theme (e.g., primary color is blue, not another color).
Do not cast a value to a type it has already been cast to; check if an earlier cast already covers the needed type assertion.
Instead of passing null for required parameters, use a proper placeholder or sentinel value (e.g., an 'uncorrelated' context) to maintain type safety and avoid null pointer issues.
Only audit events that represent user-initiated actions; system-driven status changes (e.g., job status transitions not triggered by a user) should be excluded from audit trails.
