Avoid making additional database queries solely for auditing purposes; capture audit data from context already available at the call site rather than fetching it after the fact.
Parameter names should be aligned with their corresponding type names for clarity and consistency.

When adding new dependency injection bindings, follow the same pattern used by existing modules (e.g., @provides with named annotations) rather than introducing a different abstraction for the same purpose.
Use direct imports (e.g., import package.ClassName) instead of qualified references through intermediate objects when the direct import is available.
When moving a class to a different module, move its corresponding test spec to the same module to keep tests co-located with the code they exercise.
When implementing rollback or cleanup stacks, write tests that verify execution order and behavior when individual rollback callbacks throw errors.
Use the appropriate log level for the situation: expected recovery operations (like rollback after an exception) should log at info, not warn; actual failures should log at error level.

Each class should use its own logger instance rather than accepting another class's logger as a parameter; log correlation should be done via shared context (e.g., DominoContext), not by sharing logger instances.
Critical operational failures (such as rollback failures) should trigger admin-facing alerts via supportAlerter, not just log messages, because logs alone are likely to go unnoticed.
API endpoints that advertise feature support should check that all prerequisites (e.g., PAT configuration) are actually met before reporting the feature as available.
In React components, derive computed values with useMemo instead of storing them in state via setState; use early-return if-statements for clearer conditional logic.
Wrap event handler functions in React components with useCallback to prevent unnecessary re-renders of child components.
When multiple objects or parameters share the same shape, define a TypeScript interface to avoid repetition and improve type safety.
Event handler functions defined inside React components should be wrapped with useCallback to prevent unnecessary re-renders of child components.
Modal components should extend the base modal's prop interface (e.g., open, onClose) rather than redeclaring those props, reducing duplication and ensuring consistency.
Use simple parameter names (e.g., datasetIds) and only add qualifiers like 'toInclude' or 'toExclude' when disambiguation is actually needed.
When a method's return value is not immediately obvious from the signature, add a comment above it explaining what is being returned.
Generic, reusable UI components (e.g., cards, modals) should be placed in the shared packages/ui library, not in app-specific module directories.
Do not add extra wrapper div elements in React components unless they serve a structural or styling purpose; unnecessary nesting hurts readability and can affect performance.
User-facing strings should not hardcode the product name (e.g., 'Domino'); use generic phrasing like 'A user' instead of 'A Domino user' to support whitelabel deployments.
In TypeScript, prefer 'interface' over 'type' for defining component prop shapes and object structures, as interfaces are more extensible and produce clearer error messages.
When the same logic appears in multiple React components, extract it into a shared custom hook to eliminate duplication and centralize maintenance.
Before modifying interactive UI behavior (e.g., hover states, disabled styling), confirm the intended behavior with the UX team to avoid unintended regressions.
When storing a value that does not need to trigger a re-render, use useRef instead of useState to avoid unnecessary component updates.
When using spacing utility functions like getMargin, verify the multiplier logic (e.g., getMargin(16) may compute 16 * BASE, not 16px) to avoid unintended values.
Use the same CSS unit system (px or rem) consistently throughout the application; do not mix units unless there is an established convention for when each is appropriate.
When computing layout values based on viewport or container size, use a ResizeObserver to recalculate on resize rather than only computing once on mount.
New DynamicField field types must check whether the field is editable and only render interactive controls when editable; return null or a read-only view otherwise.

Components added to reusable systems like DynamicField should be generic and simple; if a component is highly complex or domain-specific, extract it into its own module rather than embedding it in the reusable system.
Always use the IconResolver abstraction for rendering icons rather than importing icon components directly, to ensure consistent icon handling across the application.
Remove commented-out code before merging; if the code is needed later it can be retrieved from version control.
When adding conditional behavior based on an enum or provider type, ensure all variants are handled including unknown or unrecognized values.
When a service makes repeated lookups for the same data, apply a caching pattern to avoid redundant calls and improve performance.
Non-function constants (such as table column definitions or configuration arrays) should be named using UPPER_SNAKE_CASE to distinguish them from functions and variables.
Do not use explicit `return` statements in Scala; instead, restructure the control flow (e.g., invert the if condition) to rely on expression-based returns.

Ensure that type annotations match the actual data representation; avoid using narrower types (e.g., Int) when the source data uses a wider type (e.g., BigDecimal) to prevent silent data loss from implicit conversions.
When modeling JSON Schema enum fields, support JsValue (or equivalent generic JSON type) rather than only String, since enum values may be numbers or other non-string types.
Move hardcoded constant values into companion objects, and reference existing shared constants from other modules rather than redefining them locally.
When checking for duplicate or existing versions of a resource, compare the actual schemas or content for equality rather than relying solely on version identifiers.
Define pure utility functions (like sorting or data transformation) outside the component body and have them fully encapsulate their logic, including any copying or spreading of data.
Avoid rendering empty or unnecessary DOM elements (e.g., empty spans) when the underlying data is null or empty; conditionally render only when there is content to display.
Move reusable helper and utility functions out of component files and into dedicated utility files to improve discoverability and reuse.
Replace hardcoded numeric literals with named constants that convey the value's purpose and meaning.
Use the project's design system spacing functions (e.g., getMargin, getSpacing) instead of hardcoded CSS spacing values to maintain consistency.
In Scala, use `val` instead of `var` when the reference itself is never reassigned, even if the underlying collection is mutated.

When mapping between two enums, use a validated bidirectional mapping builder (e.g., StrictEnumBiMapBuilder) with explicit overrides rather than manual match/map expressions, so that missing mappings are caught at class-load time.
Wrap API calls in error handling (try/catch or .catch()) to prevent unhandled exceptions from propagating and crashing the UI.
Use the nullish coalescing operator (??) or optional chaining (?.) to simplify conditional expressions that handle null or undefined values.
Access colors through themeHelper or theme tokens instead of importing color variables directly.
Use theme spacing utilities like getMargin and getPadding instead of hardcoded margin/padding values in styled-components.
Use Ant Design Form, Form.List, and shouldUpdate instead of building custom form state management with nested conditionals.
Use AntD Form.List for dynamic array fields instead of implementing custom add/remove logic.
Use native JavaScript methods (Array.find, Array.filter, etc.) instead of utility library equivalents like Ramda when the native API provides the same functionality.
Do not create a named type or interface for values that are only used inline with hardcoded literals — pass the object directly.
Do not check the same condition twice in contiguous lines — consolidate the logic into a single conditional block.
Use word-break: break-word instead of break-all to avoid cutting words in the middle when wrapping text.
Use the AntD Form onFinish/submit handler and Form change events instead of manually collecting and submitting form values.
Pass boolean props using JSX shorthand (e.g., <Collapse accordion />) instead of explicitly setting the value to true.
When a function has four or more parameters, convert to an options object to avoid relying on positional argument order.
Sort React component props alphabetically for consistent readability and easier diffing.
Do not leave commented-out code in PRs; use TODOs with ticket references instead if future work is planned.
Remove redundant null/undefined checks when the subsequent condition already handles the falsy case.
Prefer explicit if-statement null checks over TypeScript non-null assertion operators (!) to make safety guarantees visible in the code.
Wrap expensive computations in lambdas or lazy values so they are only evaluated when actually needed.
When introducing new policies or behaviors, use no-op or passthrough implementations for unrelated components to avoid unintended side effects.
Ensure all resources (RevWalk, TreeWalk, streams) are properly disposed even within Using blocks, since close() and dispose() may have different semantics.
Add doc comments explaining the rationale behind magic numbers and non-obvious default values.
Use existing well-tested libraries for common operations (e.g., unit conversions) rather than writing custom utility code that must be maintained and tested.
When pattern matching on enums or change types, explicitly consider all variants including edge cases like deletes, and add tests to verify behavior for each.
Place private methods at the bottom of the class so the public API and core logic are visible first.
Before implementing new logic, check for existing utility methods that already provide the same functionality to avoid code duplication.
Move validation checks out of API service classes into dedicated validator services (e.g., ModelDeploymentValidator) to separate concerns.
Methods that don't depend on instance state should be static or moved to the companion object.
Regex patterns and other compiled constants should be defined as static fields on companion objects so they are only compiled once, not on every method invocation.
Avoid accessing tuple elements via positional indices (_1, _2); use case classes or named fields instead for readability.
When the same logic appears in multiple places within a class, extract it into a dedicated helper function to reduce duplication.
Each custom React hook should live in its own file rather than being co-located in a shared hooks utility file.
Ensure functions passed as props or used in dependency arrays are memoized (useCallback/useMemo) to prevent unnecessary re-renders.
Replace switch/if-else chains that map status values to icons or components with a dictionary/object lookup for cleaner, reusable code.
Controllers should delegate to domain services for business logic like lookups and data assembly rather than implementing it directly.
Service methods should return domain-specific objects (case classes) rather than raw tuples or maps so callers know what each field represents.
When the same UI component appears in multiple places, extract it into a shared general-purpose component rather than duplicating it.
When adding documentation to one function in a group of related functions, add docstrings to all of them for consistency.
Memoize sorting and other computationally expensive operations in React components using useMemo to avoid re-computing on every render.
Use theme helper tokens (e.g., themeHelper('palette.textPrimary')) instead of hardcoded color values to ensure consistency with the design system.
Before building a new UI element, check if an equivalent shared component already exists in the codebase (e.g., TooltipRenderer, empty state components).
Maintain correct import ordering with absolute paths before relative paths, and ensure linting is properly configured to enforce import sorting.
Avoid explicit type casting when using autogenerated API clients; rely on the generated types unless the API definition is known to be incorrect.
Replace magic numbers with named constants (e.g., MAX_TEMPLATES_TO_SHOW) and reuse the constant wherever that value is needed.
Challenge every @ts-ignore directive; remove it if the underlying type issue can be fixed, or document why it is necessary and track removal in a tech debt task.
Do not add more logic to already-large components; encapsulate new functionality within its own component or a dedicated hook to keep parent components manageable.
Use styled-components for styling React components instead of inline styles to maintain consistency with the codebase's styling approach.
Never define a React component inside another component; move it to its own declaration outside, or assign the JSX directly to a variable without wrapping in an arrow function.
Do not store derived values in useState with a useEffect to sync them; compute the value directly from its source during render.
Never mutate arrays received as props or stored in state; clone them before filtering or modifying to avoid subtle React rendering bugs.
When a permission is not found or undefined, default to hiding the item rather than showing it; fail closed on unknown authorization state.
When filtering child items from a parent menu or group, also hide the parent if all children are filtered out to avoid empty submenus.
File names should accurately reflect the scope and specificity of their contents; a file containing admin-specific logic should be prefixed with Admin, not given a generic name.
Never merge a PR to develop or main while package.json dependencies point to a custom or feature branch; resolve all branch-based references first.
Isolate dependency or API client changes into a separate PR and merge it promptly to minimize merge conflicts with concurrent work.
Every API call in the UI must have user-visible error feedback; never silently swallow fetch failures.
When two utility functions have identical logic, combine them into a single shared function with a configuration flag rather than maintaining duplicates.
Permission-gated UI must account for the loading and error states of the permissions fetch; do not render all items as visible while permissions are still loading or if the request fails.
Use the project's existing spacing utility functions (e.g., getMargin) consistently instead of hardcoding spacing values, even when setting padding.
Do not explicitly set CSS properties to their default values (e.g., flex-direction: row) as it adds noise without changing behavior.
Use existing custom hooks (e.g., useInterval) instead of manually wiring up setInterval with useEffect cleanup for common recurring patterns.
When migrating functionality to a new implementation, remove the old files if they are no longer referenced; verify by deploying after removal.
Move styled component definitions out of the main component file and into a separate file to keep component files focused on logic and markup.
Reference traits or interfaces rather than concrete service implementations to maintain loose coupling and testability.
Before building a new UI component, check if an equivalent component already exists in the codebase and consult UX if the existing one can be adapted.
When importing modules within the same package, use relative import paths instead of absolute paths.
Add comments specifying the expected units (e.g., milliseconds, bytes, seconds) for numeric fields that represent time or measurements.
Add try/catch blocks around async operations and display meaningful error feedback to the user when failures occur.
Access colors through the themeHelper utility rather than hardcoding hex values or referencing theme props directly, ensuring consistency with the theming system.
Use the theme context to resolve colors instead of importing from static color constants, so that colors respond correctly to theme changes.
Declare fixed configuration values (timeouts, limits, thresholds) as constants rather than variables to communicate immutability.
When adding environment variables or metadata to Kubernetes workloads, also add corresponding Kubernetes labels so admins and tooling can filter and identify workloads.
Do not assume optional or dynamically-provided values are always defined; add explicit checks or fallbacks for cases where the value may be absent.
Do not wrap a field in Option when the unwrapped type already has a valid empty/default value (e.g., empty string for String); prefer the simpler type unless None carries distinct semantic meaning.
When adding feature-specific behavior (logs, files, UI elements), gate it on the relevant configuration flag so it does not appear when the feature is disabled.
When a user's permission or role state is unknown (e.g., request pending or failed), default to the most restrictive access level rather than granting elevated privileges.
When calling a service that can fail, provide user-visible feedback (error message, toast, fallback UI) rather than silently swallowing the error.
Replace magic HTTP status code numbers with named constants from the framework (e.g., play.api.http.Status.CONFLICT instead of 409) for readability and maintainability.

Name method parameters to describe their purpose or behavior (e.g., filterByUserAndOrgIds instead of userAndOrganizationIds) so callers understand how the argument is used without reading the implementation.
Extract inline event handler functions into memoized callbacks using useCallback to avoid unnecessary re-renders of child components.
Before creating custom styled components or layout utilities, check for existing shared components in the UI package (e.g., FlexLayout) and reuse them.

Do not hardcode shared values (URLs, support links, config strings) in feature components; place them in the appropriate shared utility module (e.g., supportUtil.ts) so they can be maintained in one place.
Code should be understandable to other engineers without requiring the author to explain it; if a reviewer cannot follow the logic, refactor for clarity.
Do not explicitly pass `true` to boolean JSX props; use the shorthand attribute name alone (e.g., `<Comp disabled />` instead of `<Comp disabled={true} />`).
Hide pagination controls when there is insufficient data to paginate rather than displaying an empty or single-page paginator.
When modifying a shared or base component, validate the change across all consumers to ensure no regressions, since it can affect many downstream components.
Never import from a package's own dist output within that same package; use relative imports to the source files instead.
Prefer built-in component props (e.g., Ant Design's `noStyle`) over custom CSS overrides when the framework already provides the desired behavior.
Do not hardcode URLs that vary across deployments; use configuration hooks like `helpLink` or its associated hook to resolve environment-specific URLs.
Use the simpler `get` method for required configuration values instead of manual error handling; it throws an exception with the key name if the value is missing.
Remove commented-out code before merging; if it must stay, add a comment explaining why and link the tracking ticket.
Revert exploratory or debugging changes to shared config files (e.g., package.json) that did not resolve the issue before merging.
Files containing only styled-components should be named `styled-components.tsx` rather than generic names like `styles.tsx`.
Always wrap JSON.parse in a try-catch even when form validation exists, because optional or defaulted fields may still produce invalid JSON at runtime.
When a periodic task has multiple independent stages, catch NonFatal exceptions in each stage independently so that a failure in one does not prevent the others from executing.
When fixing a bug, add a regression test that reproduces the original bug scenario to prevent future regressions.
Test names should use positive statements that match what the test actually asserts (e.g., 'these providers are supported') rather than negative exclusions (e.g., 'all except X are supported').
Bug fixes in serialization or mapping logic should include a unit test verifying the correct behavior, even if it requires adjusting method visibility.

Do not modify shared utility methods (e.g., adding case-insensitive matching) to fix a single call site; instead, create a separate method with a comment explaining why the relaxed behavior is needed.
Do not allow invalid string representations (like the string "None" from an Option's toString) to propagate through the system; validate and throw at the boundary instead.
Test data should match realistic API input values (e.g., use the actual casing the API sends) to catch case-sensitivity bugs.
Types and interfaces should be moved to a dedicated `types` file rather than defined inline in utility or component files.
Use idiomatic collection operations like drop(offset).take(limit) for pagination instead of manual slice arithmetic.
Method names should accurately reflect their behavior; if a method performs pagination, include that in the name (e.g., getPaginatedModelDeployments).
Extract repeated parsing and transformation logic into a single helper function to avoid duplication and reduce error-prone inline parsing.
Do not retrieve all records from the database on every request; apply server-side filtering or pagination to avoid fetching excessive data.
Keep import statements organized and properly ordered in source files.
Place modal instances at the table level rather than inside each table row; row actions should only trigger the modal, not own their own instance.
Add an explicit guard condition when logic only applies to a specific subtype, making the precondition obvious to readers.
Add DEBUG or TRACE level log statements before key decision points so the decision-making process is observable in logs.
New service classes with non-trivial logic should have a corresponding unit test Spec class.
Function names must accurately describe the scope of their behavior; if a function stops an entire workflow rather than a single task, the name and documentation must make that clear.
Choose field names that are clearly distinct from existing fields on the same type; when a name is ambiguous, add a comment explaining its purpose.
Do not pass arguments to a function that are not declared in its parameter list; relying on closure scope while passing a value positionally is misleading.
In JSX, omit the `={true}` for boolean props; use the shorthand attribute form instead.
Use a component's built-in loading mechanism rather than wrapping it in an additional loading spinner, unless the loading state is for data outside the component's responsibility.
When rendering lists in React, use a unique identifier (e.g., entity ID) as the key prop instead of the array index to ensure correct reconciliation and avoid subtle rendering bugs.
When designing APIs that support multiple providers or backends, accept generalized parameter names from the user and map them internally to provider-specific fields, reducing user-facing complexity.
Instead of commenting out code to disable functionality, create a new variable or configuration that achieves the desired state cleanly, keeping the codebase free of dead commented-out blocks.
When an RPC or service call already receives an entity ID, assemble the full request payload within the RPC context rather than passing redundant data from the caller.

When adjusting visual layout such as spacing, indentation, or dividers, prefer CSS rules on the appropriate container or group elements rather than restructuring component hierarchy or adding JavaScript logic.

When modifying permission or role definitions, explicitly verify that each role (especially restricted roles like limitedAdmin) has the expected access level and cannot view or modify resources beyond its intended scope.
Review conditional branches and guard clauses to identify code that can never be reached due to earlier conditions, and remove any debugging or test code that was accidentally left in.

When overriding a pattern match to customize handling for specific cases, delegate all other cases to the superclass or default implementation rather than duplicating or restricting the existing logic.

When modifying authorization, configuration, or behavior for one entity type (e.g., templates, projects), verify whether the same changes need to be applied to related entity types (e.g., apps, models) to avoid inconsistent behavior.

In tests that do not depend on real-world values (e.g., real time, real network), use simulated or mocked dependencies instead of real ones to clearly signal that the test is not relying on those external factors.
Prefer structured or typed data access over manual string parsing; string parsing is fragile and should be a last resort with a clear comment explaining why.
Explicitly consider and handle cases where expected data may be absent, and decide whether that is a valid state or an error.
When using undocumented or hard-to-find API endpoints, add a comment explaining where the endpoint was discovered and link to the closest available documentation.
Do not add dependencies that are no longer actively maintained; prefer actively maintained alternatives to reduce risk from unpatched bugs and vulnerabilities.
Access colors through the theme helper abstraction rather than importing directly from the colors map, ensuring consistent theming and easier future changes.
Use existing library functions (e.g., Ramda, Lodash) for common operations like unique/set instead of writing custom implementations.
Do not extract single-use expressions into named functions; place the logic inline at the call site to reduce indirection.
When logic is reusable across components or modules, extract it into a shared utility function rather than keeping it inline in a single component.
Before creating a new utility, check for existing implementations and extend them rather than duplicating constants or logic.
Use reduce instead of forEach when building or accumulating a result, as it makes the data transformation intent explicit and avoids side-effect-driven mutation.
Replace hard-coded numeric literals with named constants to improve readability and maintainability.
When domain terms are overloaded, introduce method names that make the specific use case explicit rather than relying on generic names with parameters.
Test names should unambiguously describe the scenario under test; when a reviewer has to ask 'is this X or all X?', the name is too vague.
Never hardcode font-family values in component styles; remove inline font-family declarations or reference the value from the theme.
Avoid introducing custom color values in theme definitions or components; map to existing palette tokens (e.g., palette.danger) to keep the design system consistent.
When replacing icons from one library (e.g., AntD) with another (e.g., FontAwesome), the aria-label must match the original to avoid breaking existing tests.
When migrating components or icons, verify that conditional rendering logic and visual states remain identical to the original implementation.
Theme object entries for color attributes should reference existing semantic palette tokens, not non-standard hex values, to maintain consistency across the application.
Do not explicitly set style properties that already match the theme defaults; remove redundant declarations like fontSizes.medium or fontWeights.normal when they duplicate the default values.
Before defining custom color values in theme files, check if the default palette already provides the needed token to avoid duplication and inconsistency.
When implementing paginated API responses, use the existing PaginatedResponse traits from the shared utilities rather than creating ad-hoc pagination wrappers.

Scaladoc and code comments must accurately describe the class or method semantics; do not add documentation that attributes behavior or constraints that are actually imposed by callers rather than the type itself.

Be cautious when adding feature-specific traits or changes to shared infrastructure files (e.g., telemetry events); ensure the additions are generic enough to be reused by other consumers of that module.
When introducing a v2 implementation alongside a legacy version, create a separate test spec file for the new version rather than adding v2 tests to the existing legacy spec file.
Use the standard DominoLoggable trait or lazy val logger pattern instead of manually creating loggers, unless there is a specific cross-service requirement for a named logger.
Use concise entity type names (e.g., 'user' instead of 'userAccount') in APIs and domain models unless the longer name disambiguates between genuinely different concepts.
When migrating to new icon components, apply color via a StyledComponent wrapper rather than passing color as a direct prop, as the new icon components may not accept color props the same way.
Move complex serialization logic to the model's companion object or a dedicated serializer in the same namespace, and add serialization tests to verify the output format.
Remove print statements and debug logging added during development before merging; use proper logger calls at appropriate log levels instead.
Review logging statements to ensure they use appropriate log levels; remove debug/trace logging or bump it down from info level before merging to production.
Add tests for all significant code paths and features, not just the primary one; if a module handles multiple cases (e.g., both 'target' and 'location'), each should have dedicated test coverage.
Centralize repeated encoding/transformation logic (such as double URL encoding) into a single shared utility function so it can be updated in one place when the underlying framework changes.
When adding encoding logic to shared/common components, verify that already-encoded values are not encoded again to prevent double-encoding bugs across the application.

In property-based tests, use the most specific generator that matches the domain constraint (e.g., use relativeFilePathGen for values that must always be relative paths) rather than a more general generator.
When registering scheduled tasks or injection modules, verify which service instances they will run in and ensure they only execute where needed to avoid unnecessary resource consumption.
When adding new customer-facing features or modifying visibility/access logic, update the corresponding runbook documentation so operations teams can troubleshoot issues.
Do not set both `data-test` and `testId` props on the same component; use only the one that your test framework requires to keep test selectors consistent and avoid confusion.
Use the shared `getErrorMessage` utility from `@domino/ui/dist/utils/errorUtil` to extract error messages instead of writing custom error parsing logic.
Absolute imports must appear before relative imports; enforce consistent import ordering even when the linter misses it.
Extract utility functions, constants, and sub-components into separate files; keep the main component file focused on its primary responsibility.
Use styled-components instead of inline styles for consistent, maintainable styling.
Use the shared mock utility pattern (e.g., `createMockComponent`) for mocking components in tests so you can access props and call callbacks on the mock.
Create reusable mock data generator functions in `frontend/packages/mocks/generators` instead of inlining test data, so mock data can be shared across test suites.
Prefer `slice` over `splice` for array operations to avoid mutating the original array, even when working with a copy.
When intentionally omitting dependencies from a React useEffect/useCallback dependency array, leave a comment explaining why to prevent future confusion.
Avoid creating separate React state for values that can be derived or computed from existing state to reduce synchronization bugs.
Before creating custom styled elements, check if an existing shared component (e.g., Link components) already provides the needed functionality.
Import style modules using namespace imports (`import * as Styles from './styles'`) so styled components are clearly identifiable as components in JSX.
Use theme or palette colors instead of hardcoded color values, and avoid `!important` overrides in styled-components.
When encountering an error, investigate and fix the root cause rather than disabling or bypassing the failing code path, especially when the workaround has unintended side effects on other features.
When refactoring code that moves responsibility from one class to another, ensure equivalent unit tests are created for the new class covering the same behavior.

When implementing policies that enforce uniqueness or singleton constraints, account for race conditions where multiple concurrent requests could bypass the check, especially in distributed systems with multiple service instances.
Query methods that filter, scope, and sort results should have tests verifying each dimension: correct entity type, correct scope (e.g., project), correct status filtering, and correct sort order.
Do not wrap values in useMemo unless the computation is expensive or the reference identity matters for downstream effects; unnecessary memoization adds complexity without benefit.
Use theme helper constants (e.g., margins.tiny, margins.small) instead of hardcoded pixel values in styled components to maintain consistency with the design system.
When a value can be computed directly from existing state or props, calculate it inline rather than synchronizing it through a separate useState and useEffect pair.

Documentation and help links should use centralized support utilities (e.g., HelpLink component or docs base path hook) rather than hardcoded URLs, so links update automatically when docs locations change.
When the same conditional logic is duplicated across multiple components in the same file or module, extract it into a shared utility function to avoid repetition and ensure consistency.
When multiple styled components share the same styles (e.g., icon wrappers with identical styling), consolidate them into a single reusable styled component.
When a component is already a styled-component, set all its styles (including fontSize) in the styled definition rather than passing them as inline style props.
If a function is fully replaced by another (e.g., update replaced by patch), delete the unused function rather than keeping both to avoid confusion about which to use.
Configuration flags that control independent behaviors should remain independent; disabling one feature flag should not silently disable an unrelated feature, even if the code paths are nearby.
When implementing a feature that operates on project files, verify it works across all supported project types (e.g., DFS and GBP) rather than only the default type.
Name constants to clearly indicate their domain or purpose, not just their structural role.
Do not add DI framework dependencies (e.g., Guice) directly in library classes; initialize bindings in injection modules to prevent transitive dependency leakage into downstream services.
When logic is extracted into a new class, tests must follow the logic to the new class rather than relying on indirect coverage from the original caller.
New code paths and branches should have corresponding test cases, even if added in a follow-up PR.
Domain models should only contain fields that are actively used; do not add fields speculatively for future use.
Service implementations that make HTTP calls or depend on external infrastructure belong in the infrastructure layer, not in the domain or server layer.
Entity type identifiers should be specific enough to distinguish between the entity and its parent or container (e.g., `centralConfigSetting` vs `centralConfig`).
Reference defined constants or enum values (e.g., `EventTarget.Project`) instead of inline string literals for type identifiers.
Field names like `Id` should contain actual identifiers, not display names; rename fields whose values don't match the semantic meaning of the name.
Telemetry and audit trail events should include all available identifying fields (e.g., both ID and name) to make events useful for debugging and auditing.
In React/TypeScript files, leave a blank line between type/interface declarations and the component function definition for readability.
Order imports so that absolute (package) imports come before relative imports.
Use styled components instead of inline styles, reference colors from the theme rather than hardcoding hex values, and use named constants for magic numbers in calculations.
When a common/shared component exists (e.g., a shared link component), prefer reusing it over creating a local duplicate.
Use router links instead of anchor tags when navigating to React pages to avoid full page reloads.
Use semantic color tokens (textPrimary, textSecondary) from the design system instead of hardcoded or arbitrary color values in styled components.
Use design system palette border colors for dividers and borders instead of hardcoded color values.
Use the getMargin spacing utility instead of deprecated paddings.* keys for calculating spacing values.
Use the themeHelper utility for accessing theme values in styled components instead of raw theme property access.
Use existing route helper functions to build navigation URLs instead of constructing URL strings manually.
Simplify conditional logic by using early returns instead of if/else chains when the if-branch sets a value and returns.
Before writing custom state management or effects, check if an existing hook (e.g., useOnChange, useRemoteData.refetch) already provides the needed behavior.
Use the refetch method from data fetching hooks (e.g., useRemoteData) instead of copying remote data into local state to trigger re-renders after mutations.
Define event handler functions with useCallback to prevent unnecessary re-creation on every re-render.
Apply the same post-mutation side effects (e.g., page reload, refetch) consistently across all similar async operations in a component.
If a method's return type includes values it can never actually produce (e.g., always returns true), refactor the return type to accurately reflect actual behavior (e.g., Unit).
Before modifying or removing props from shared UI components, verify that no other consumers depend on the changes, especially for long-lived branches.
When replacing legacy Ant Design icons with custom icons, preserve the same aria-label values to avoid breaking existing tests.
Verify that new UI layouts render correctly at the minimum supported viewport dimensions (1366x768) before merging.
Leverage existing internal utility libraries (e.g., domino-pytk) for common operations like resource traversal instead of writing custom implementations.
When operating on resources that can have multiple instances (e.g., jobs in a project), target by a unique identifier (e.g., execution ID) rather than a broad parent identifier (e.g., project name).
Do not leave commented-out code in the codebase; either remove it entirely or explain why it must remain.
When linking to internal packages, do not assume build output directories (e.g., /dist) will always exist; validate or document the dependency on prior build steps.
When injecting helpers or scripts via modules at runtime, evaluate whether race conditions can occur due to load ordering or timing.
Never import mock modules or test utilities in production source files; this causes build issues and increases bundle size. Duplicate the needed functionality as a utility instead.
Wrap callback functions passed as props or used in effects with React.useCallback() to prevent unnecessary re-creation on every render.
Use TypeScript interface with extends to compose types rather than defining duplicate type aliases; if a type already exists in a shared package, reuse it.
When a function already computes or retrieves a value that callers need, return it directly rather than forcing callers to re-derive it separately.
Lambda handlers should be lightweight routers that validate and forward requests, pushing business logic and config processing to downstream services.
In Scala, distinguish between empty collections (Seq.empty) and null — they are not the same and mock argument matchers must account for this difference.
When storing polymorphic domain entities, put commonly-used fields for filtering and sorting at the top level, and nest type-specific definitions in dedicated sub-fields.
Domain interfaces should use domain IDs (e.g., DominoId) and keep persistence-specific details (e.g., MongoDB ObjectId) confined to the persister implementation layer.
Use Storybook CSF3 format for stories instead of the deprecated MDX format.
Import from lodash-es instead of lodash to enable proper tree-shaking and reduce bundle size.
Function names should accurately describe all behaviors — a function named onApplyTag that also removes tags should be split into separate functions with single concerns.
Remove poorly-implemented HATEOAS uri fields rather than keeping them — if nobody currently needs the feature, delete it and implement it properly when needed.
When a create operation involves copying multiple sub-resources, do not fail the entire operation if some items cannot be copied — instead track what succeeded and report failures with reasons.

Service layer interfaces should not return REST API DTOs — this couples all service consumers (including internal ones) to the API definition and forces changes in the API layer to propagate to unrelated consumers.
Dependency injection bindings should be placed in the module that owns the feature (e.g., TemplatesInjectionModule), not in a parent or sibling module (e.g., ProjectsInjectionModule).
Add logging to new service operations and API endpoints so that behavior can be traced in production.
When a file contains functions for multiple subsystems, prefix function names with the subsystem name to make their scope clear from the call site.
Remove all console.log statements and other debug logging artifacts before submitting a PR for review.
Review and remove TODO comments that are no longer valid when modifying or moving code.
Do not commit empty or unused files; delete them as part of the PR cleanup.
Place new tests in a dedicated test module rather than appending them to an existing unrelated test file; group related tests together by concern.
Functions should return computed results rather than requiring callers to pass in empty containers to be filled.
When multiple tests share the same assertion logic, extract a common assertion function and have each test call it with its specific parameters.
Use descriptive, domain-specific variable names (e.g., env_image instead of env_uri) that accurately reflect the value's meaning.
Do not use `object` as a type annotation in TypeScript; use a specific interface, type alias, or `Record<string, unknown>` instead.
Include all dependencies in React useEffect dependency arrays rather than using eslint-disable; refactor the effect or extract stable references to avoid infinite loops.
Use proper TypeScript types for test props instead of @ts-ignore; convert JSX components to TSX or provide type declarations so tests are type-safe.
Disentangle unrelated concerns (e.g., client generation, dependency updates, feature logic) into separate PRs so each change is easier to review and reason about in history.
When a variable refers to one of several possible resources of the same kind (e.g., S3 buckets), include a qualifier in the name to clarify which resource it refers to.
Name variables to reflect their lifecycle state (e.g., `persistedDeployment` instead of a generic name) so readers immediately know whether the value is in-memory, persisted, or pending.
Never catch all Throwables; use `scala.util.control.NonFatal` to avoid swallowing fatal JVM errors like OutOfMemoryError.

When deleting a resource that has dependent external resources (e.g., Kubernetes objects), delete the database record only after the external cleanup succeeds so the record is preserved for retry if cleanup fails.
Do not call `.get` on Option values; use `.getOrElse`, `.fold`, pattern matching, or throw a descriptive exception to handle the None case explicitly.
Use curly braces for multi-expression blocks and follow the Databricks Scala style guide conventions for brace placement.
Add trace-level logging to all client/infrastructure methods, including the method name and all parameters.
Catch only NonFatal exceptions in Scala; never catch all exceptions with a bare catch block, as this can swallow fatal errors like OutOfMemoryError.
Custom exceptions must accept and pass the underlying cause Throwable to the Exception constructor so stack traces and root causes are preserved in logs.
Name the exception parameter 'cause' (not 'ex') and pass it to the parent Exception constructor so the JVM exception chain is properly linked.

Log messages should describe the operation context (e.g. 'error creating model deployment secret, rolling back'), not just echo exception.getMessage, since the full exception is already logged via the second parameter.
If a method performs create-or-update logic, its name should reflect both operations (e.g. 'createOrUpdateModelDeployment') rather than implying only one.
Name variables after their domain purpose (e.g. 'mlflowS3ArtifactBucket') rather than the config key they're read from, so the code is self-documenting about intent.
Use enumeratum's withNameInsensitiveOption for parsing enum values from external input instead of manually converting to uppercase, to handle mixed-case input safely.
Use getOrElse with an explicit error thrown for required values rather than silently returning None or a default, so missing data fails fast with a clear message.
Service layers should use generic, provider-agnostic abstractions rather than hardcoding behavior for a specific compute provider; design for dynamic discovery and configuration.
Use idiomatic Scala collection methods like isEmpty instead of manual length or size checks for cleaner, more readable code.
Keep persistence logic (e.g., MongoDB saves) in the same architectural location for both create and update paths of the same entity to avoid confusion and inconsistency.
Use separate try-catch blocks for independent operations that require different rollback logic, rather than grouping them into a single block.
Controllers should delegate to a single service method that encapsulates both persistence and downstream calls, rather than orchestrating persistence and business logic separately.
Ensure finally blocks do not execute unintended side effects on fatal errors; guard finally logic with appropriate flags that account for both fatal and non-fatal failure paths.
Do not place success-only logic in finally blocks; code that should only run when no exception occurred belongs in the try block after the operation that may fail.
Follow the Databricks Scala style guide for curly brace usage in conditionals and control structures.
Use idiomatic Scala Option methods like getOrElse instead of verbose if/else or match expressions when extracting a value or throwing on None.
When refactoring or rewriting code, verify that all existing tests are preserved or re-added; do not silently drop test coverage.
When a method returns Option/None, explicitly handle the absent case — decide whether to throw an exception, return a default, or propagate the Option.
When create and update paths share a method but have mostly different logic, split them into separate methods for clarity and maintainability.
Apply retry policies consistently across analogous operations (e.g., if update uses a retry policy, create should too) unless there is a deliberate reason for the difference.
When adding new components that make API calls, ensure a test suite exists and mock external calls to prevent unintended network requests in tests.
When moving or refactoring files, check whether the original file had unit tests and ensure equivalent coverage exists for the new location.
Use polling on status instead of fixed sleep/wait calls in tests and automation, as sleeps are fragile and waste time.
Use existing utility functions (e.g., getAppName, getTemplateType) instead of manually handling raw values or reimplementing logic that a utility already encapsulates.
When copying or forking an entity, ensure all attributes are included in the copy — review the source model field by field to avoid silently dropping properties.
When adding a new dependency to a BUILD or build file, be prepared to explain why it is needed; unnecessary dependencies increase build times and coupling.
Fields that are optional in REST API DTOs for backward compatibility should be required in the domain model; keep the distinction clear and plan to version the API to tighten optional fields.
Do not allow a code path that enables functionality which has not been tested by QE; if a feature is not supported in MVP, the code should not permit it.
Ensure that exception types map to the correct HTTP status codes — user input errors (e.g., IllegalArgumentException) should return 400, not 500.

Services should return Optional values and let the caller decide how to handle absence, rather than throwing exceptions internally — the correct failure mode (IllegalArgument vs IllegalState) depends on the caller's context.

Validation logic (e.g., billing tags, permissions) should be applied consistently across all code paths that create or modify the same entity, not duplicated in different places with divergent behavior.
When adding counters or metrics for one variant of a feature, ensure equivalent metrics exist for all variants to maintain observability parity.
Use existing accessor methods on domain objects (e.g., d.getTemplateType) instead of manually unwrapping optional fields, to reduce boilerplate and avoid null-handling bugs.
When modifying shared utility code that is used across multiple sections, run the full relevant test suite to catch unintended regressions.
Include scenario IDs or other unique identifiers in test resource names (e.g., workspace names) to avoid collisions and improve traceability.

In tests that validate environment-dependent values (e.g., memory, CPU), assert on relative properties (unit suffix, greater-than threshold) rather than exact values to avoid brittleness across different node types or platforms.
When removing a code step or block, also remove any associated comments that reference the deleted code to avoid stale documentation.
Domain entities should never reference API model classes; the API is one presentation of the domain and coupling the domain to it creates a dependency in the wrong direction.
When a file contains only one class or object, name the file after that class to make it easy to locate.

Domain entities and validation logic should not depend on web framework constructs (e.g., Play Forms); if the framework changes or the domain is reused in a non-web context, the domain should remain unaffected.
Domain entities should not include JSON or other serialization mappings because it couples the domain with a specific serialized format and constrains future evolution of the entity.
When different subtypes of an entity have distinct fields, use polymorphism (subclasses) rather than making all fields optional on a single type, to enforce correctness at the type level.
Validation helpers and required-field definitions should live in their own file, separate from the domain entity definitions, and be scoped (e.g., package-private) to the consuming code.

Database record definitions should clearly show what is stored without requiring developers to trace through codecs and external DTOs; do not reuse API DTOs as persistence record fields since changes in the API layer should not affect the persistence layer.
When a data type represents a specific variant, remove fields that never apply to that variant rather than leaving them as unused optional fields.
Remove public interfaces from shared modules when a class should only be consumed through a higher-level service, preventing other services from bypassing the intended access layer.
Place REST API DTOs and remote/internal mapping DTOs in separate packages so reviewers can immediately identify what backward-compatibility guarantees apply to each.
Do not include code (classes, files, endpoints) that has no current usages; add it in the PR where it is actually needed.
Domain services should depend on domain entities, not on API-layer DTOs; coupling services to API models causes unrelated API changes to ripple into business logic.
When adding permission checks, follow the existing pattern used by similar methods in the codebase rather than inventing a new approach.
When fixing a bug in one code path, check whether the same problematic pattern exists in nearby or analogous code and fix those instances too.
Prefer aria-label over data-testid for identifying interactive elements in tests; this simultaneously improves accessibility and simplifies test selectors.
Write aria-label values as full, human-readable words (not abbreviations or IDs) because they are read aloud by screen readers for visually impaired users.
Use built-in default-value mechanisms (e.g., dict.get with a default) to eliminate unnecessary conditional branches.
Use @dataclass with its auto-generated __init__ for simple data holders instead of writing custom wrapper classes that hide straightforward construction behind unnecessary abstraction.
Do not use module-level singletons or global-like variables unless there is a clear, justified need; prefer passing dependencies explicitly.
Use list comprehensions instead of imperative for-loops with append when transforming a collection into a new list in Python.
When a function is almost always invoked alongside a logging call, move the logging inside the function to avoid repetitive boilerplate and missed log statements.
Before removing shared configuration like environment variables or build scripts, check for dependencies in other repositories that may consume them.
When an environment variable already exists for a value, reference the env var instead of hardcoding the value in other scripts or config files.
Phrase BDD feature file scenarios as user stories (As a [role], I'd like to [action], So that I can [benefit]) to clarify intent and audience.
Cross-domain mapping logic (e.g., template-to-project) should live in the originating service, not the consuming service, to avoid coupling unrelated services.
Name test fixtures to describe the data or resource they provide (e.g., `git_credentials`) rather than using generic or action-oriented names.
Move complex inline expressions into named utility functions or variables to improve readability and testability.
Replace magic numbers with named constants (e.g., `MAX_GOALS_SHOWN`) so the intent is clear and the value can be reused consistently.
Ensure the test's `it` description accurately reflects what is being asserted; if it claims to test a behavior change, the test must actually trigger and verify that change.
Use existing utility classes like AutoRecordMapperCodec for serialization instead of writing manual mapping code, unless there is a specific technical reason not to.
When processing a stream of events that each trigger a database or service lookup, introduce a low-complexity cache to avoid redundant lookups that scale linearly with event volume.
Prefer API-based setup steps over UI-based steps in end-to-end tests to reduce flakiness and speed up test execution, unless the test specifically validates UI-only behavior.
When a function or hook accepts several parameters, group them into a single options/params object to improve readability and reduce confusion at call sites.
Move pure utility functions and mappings outside of React component bodies into separate utils files or module-level declarations to improve readability and testability.
When defining a set of related string or numeric constants, use an enum (or a const object with an extracted type) rather than loose constants, and tie the type to the enum keys for type safety.

Instead of using useEffect to react to prop changes (which can cause extra renders and missing-dependency warnings), use the 'previous value comparison during render' pattern recommended by the React docs.

When using rest/spread props that include accessibility attributes like aria-label, verify the attribute is interpreted correctly on each target element, especially when both a link and a button receive the same spread props.
When a component depends on asynchronously loaded configuration or locale data, consider delaying child rendering until that data is available to prevent render inconsistencies.

Domain/interface modules must not reference API/web module types (e.g., DTOs). Mapping between domain and API models should live in the appropriate layer, and build tools like Bazel should enforce this boundary.

When the frontend needs a derived value (e.g., provider name from a URI), include it as an explicit field in the API response rather than requiring consumers to parse or compute it, especially when the mapping already exists on the backend.
Use imperative functions (e.g., a callable `fetchData`) for data refreshes instead of chaining multiple useEffect hooks with boolean flags, which causes unnecessary re-renders.
Before writing custom test assertions, check for existing shared test helpers (e.g., `@domino/test-utils`) that already cover common verification patterns like breadcrumbs, layout, and routing.
Do not use deprecated or legacy product terminology (e.g., 'Detwirl') in new code, variable names, route names, or comments — even when refactoring code that originally used those terms.
Use React Router route links instead of raw anchor tags or `window.location` for in-app navigation within the React SPA to preserve client-side routing and avoid full page reloads.
Do not clone or spread an entire props object just to inject one additional value; pass the original props through and add the extra prop individually.
Use kebab-case for `data-testid` attributes to maintain consistency across the codebase.

Each React component should live in its own file; do not export multiple components with similar interfaces but different implementations from the same file. Avoid prefixing component names with vendor library names (e.g., 'Ant').
If a prop is optional and has no default value, do not assume it will exist at runtime — add a null/undefined guard before accessing it.
Set default values for optional parameters at the destructuring site or function signature rather than with conditional logic later in the function body.

Add explanatory comments or improved logging for non-obvious control flow, especially around retry logic, race conditions, or multi-step shutdown sequences where the 'why' is not self-evident from the code.
When removing all usages of a module during a refactor, also delete the now-unused source file to prevent dead code from accumulating.
When building a generalized endpoint, ensure it supports all intended backing types (e.g., DFS and Git), not just the immediately needed one.
Never hardcode the product name in UI strings; always use the app name getter to support white-labelling.
Before creating a new constant (especially for timeouts/thresholds), check if an existing one can be reused or adjusted instead.
When a large match/case statement repeats the same function call structure in every branch, extract the varying parts into a tuple and call the function once after the match.
Do not add explicit imports for classes that are already covered by a wildcard import in the same file.
Use domain-level ID abstractions (e.g., DominoId.newInstance) instead of database-specific types (e.g., ObjectId) outside of database layer code.
Module-scoped pytest fixtures that could be shared across tests should be defined in a separate fixtures file, not inline in a specific test file.
Use established test helper utilities (e.g., create_user() from user_helpers.py) instead of reimplementing test setup logic inline.
Archive or delete test-created resources (projects, users, etc.) after tests complete to avoid polluting the test environment.
When a variable is an alias for another concept (e.g., selectedRevision aliasing activeRevision), add a comment explaining the relationship to avoid confusion.
Define static configuration objects like table column definitions as constants outside the component to avoid re-creating them on every re-render.
When calling date formatting utilities, verify they handle undefined or invalid inputs gracefully and do not render 'Invalid date' to the user.
Use TypeScript enums generated from API/YAML specs instead of @ts-ignore or hardcoded string literals.
Use white-label-aware UI components (e.g., waitSpinner) instead of hardcoded or generic alternatives to ensure theming consistency.
Remove all commented-out code and commented-out tests before merging; do not leave dead code in the codebase.
Do not use type casting to work around API type mismatches; fix the underlying type or remove the cast.
Move complex inline expressions into named functions for readability and reusability.
Use rem units instead of px for margin and padding values to support scalable, accessible layouts.
Write BDD/Gherkin test steps in clear, natural language that reads as a straightforward English sentence.
Do not place internal service models in the API model package; keep API models and internal domain models in separate packages.
Design service interfaces with distinct parameters for pagination, sorting, and filtering instead of bundling them into a single object.
Return the most specific type directly from service methods instead of adding unnecessary conversion methods when the service already produces the correct type.
Use the same query parameter name (e.g., `q` for search) across all APIs to maintain a consistent developer experience.
Use `getOrElse` with an `IllegalStateException` containing a clear message instead of calling `.get` on Options, even in cases that should never fail, to make debugging easier.
API filter parameter names should match the corresponding domain model attribute names so the mapping is easy to understand; fix naming mismatches early because they become harder to change later.
When a simpler API shape (e.g., single-value parameter) satisfies current requirements and expanding it later (e.g., to multi-value) is not a breaking change, ship the simpler version first.
Use the proper domain type (e.g., `DominoId`) explicitly rather than relying on implicit conversions from primitive types to ensure type safety and code clarity.
Move reusable test fixture creators into shared test utility modules (e.g., `testslib`) so they can be used across multiple test suites.
The `totalCount` in a paginated API response must reflect the total number of results matching the filter in the database, not just the current page size.
Name variables and binders to accurately reflect what they bind or represent; misleading names create confusion during code review and maintenance.
When upstream data changes rarely (e.g., once every few months), avoid performing heavy IO on every endpoint call; use caching or a check-before-fetch strategy to reduce database load.
When checking existence or fetching multiple records, use a single bulk query with $in or similar operators and do in-memory diffing, rather than issuing one query per item.
When synchronizing upstream data with a local database, consolidate queries into bulk operations rather than performing per-record IO; consider strategies like remove-all-and-reinsert for simplicity.
Function and field names should match their domain-level meaning (e.g., use 'hub' when filtering by hub, not 'author') to avoid confusion between API concepts and internal filters.
Use strict equality (=== instead of ==) to avoid implicit type coercion bugs, especially in template and frontend code.
When commenting out code or disabling tests due to a known issue, include a reference to the tracking Jira ticket so future readers know why it was disabled and when it can be re-enabled.
Define named constants for magic values like page sizes, and reuse the same constant everywhere the value is referenced to ensure consistency and single-source-of-truth.
Choose clearly distinct names for types and interfaces; if a name looks like a typo of another type in the same file, rename it to reflect its actual purpose to avoid confusion.
Avoid duplicate imports from the same module and prefer named imports over wildcard (* as) imports in React files.
Always use strict equality operators (=== and !==) instead of loose equality (== and !=) for comparisons.
Prefer the 'unknown' type over 'any' in TypeScript to maintain type safety, especially in test mocks and type assertions.
Assign boolean literals to descriptively named variables before passing them as function arguments to improve readability.
Always provide a default value for shell/environment variable expansions using ${VAR:-default} to prevent unset variable errors.
Ensure API errors are surfaced to the user or handled explicitly, not silently swallowed by the calling component.
Use clear, descriptive variable names that convey the purpose and content of the variable.
Align and format complex expressions or chained operations for readability, especially in functional pipelines.
Use default imports for React (`import React from 'react'`) instead of namespace imports.
Move reusable utility functions out of component files and into dedicated utils files.
Use the short fragment syntax `<>` instead of `React.Fragment` when no key prop is needed.
When refactoring changes a function's behavior, rename the function to accurately reflect its new behavior and avoid confusion.
Avoid hardcoding magic numbers (e.g., pixel widths) in child components; instead derive UI state from parent-controlled boolean or enum props to prevent fragile coupling.
When an optional API field has a destructive implicit behavior (e.g., omitting it deletes existing data), document that behavior in the API contract and add a unit test covering it.
Remove unused variables, imports, and dead code rather than leaving them in the codebase.
Permission denial messages should specify what the user lacks permission to do and direct them to contact their administrator for resolution.
When making a targeted fix (especially for cloud/hotfix), create a wrapper component that composes the shared component instead of modifying the shared component directly.
Do not commit empty or placeholder test files; either write meaningful tests or omit the file until tests are ready.
Use trace-level logging for verbose operational messages so they are off by default but can be enabled when debugging; reserve info/debug for significant state changes.
Use a bounded retry policy with backoff instead of an infinite loop when waiting for an external resource or condition.
Add exception handling around operations that interact with external systems or can fail at runtime; do not leave them unguarded.
Place constants and static-like values in a companion object rather than inline in the class body.
Name variables and types using the language of the current domain context, not terms inherited from upstream or adjacent services.
When adding a new consumer, parser, or non-trivial logic, include a unit test that demonstrates the expected input/output to make the behavior concrete for reviewers and future maintainers.
When defining status enums, always include an explicit error state and ensure error messages from downstream systems are passed through rather than silently dropped.
Do not use optional function props as the mechanism to show or hide UI elements; use an explicit boolean prop or a more verbose pattern that makes the conditional rendering obvious.
When multiple APIs refer to the same concept, use the same term everywhere to avoid confusion between equivalent names.
For unpaginated endpoints, set the internal query limit high enough that results are never silently truncated in practice, preventing mysterious missing data in the UI.
Extract hard-coded limits (e.g., max result size) into method parameters with sensible defaults so tests can set low values to verify limit enforcement without relying on large data sets.

When a method's responsibility is to validate an entity, pass the entire entity to it rather than having the caller decide which fields will be checked — the validation method should own that decision.

Use variables or configuration references for environment-specific values (image tags, versions, URLs) in test files instead of hardcoding them, to prevent silent test failures when those values change.
Remove console.log and other console statements from production code before merging; do not use console output as a workaround for unused variables.
Define table column configurations in a separate file near the table component that renders them, not in the parent view that passes them as props.
When a store (e.g., useStore) is globally accessible, consume it directly in the component that needs it rather than threading it through router or parent props.
Import UI components from the Domino design system library rather than directly from Ant Design; document the reason if a direct Ant import is necessary due to missing design system support.
When a column renderer or inline render function grows complex, extract it into its own component rather than keeping it in a utility file.
Define a TypeScript union type for a fixed set of string keys so new values are caught at compile time rather than silently passing.
Import from the built dist output of internal packages rather than reaching into source files directly.
When logging service operations, include the filter or query parameters in the log message so issues can be traced to specific inputs.
Use fake implementations rather than mocks for test doubles; fakes exercise more realistic behavior and are easier to maintain.
When logging an operation or decision point, include all relevant contextual values (e.g., flags, IDs, status) so logs are self-contained for debugging.
Use the correct semantic HTML element or component for its purpose — do not use a Link component as a button or vice versa.
Do not commit empty files to the repository; remove them or add their intended content before merging.
When leaving temporary workarounds or placeholders in test files, annotate them with a TODO label so they are trackable and not forgotten.
Use framework-provided components (e.g., react-router's Redirect) instead of writing custom equivalents unless there is a clear, documented reason the built-in cannot work.
When testing multiple expected failures, assert each one individually rather than relying on a loop that short-circuits on the first failure.
In E2E tests, use the most specific selector available (e.g., `I wait to see the checkbox "Practitioner"`) rather than generic text matching, to make tests more resilient and intention-clear.
Verify the actual UI element type (checkbox, button, text, etc.) before writing E2E test assertions against it to avoid false passes or misleading test logic.
Avoid using `any` in TypeScript; use proper types or generics to maintain type safety.
Do not mark test functions as async unless they actually contain awaited expressions; unnecessary async adds confusion about test intent.
Maintain consistent import ordering: absolute/external imports should come before relative imports, enforced by Prettier or ESLint.
Keep React state as close as possible to the component that uses it rather than lifting it to a parent container that doesn't need it.
When displaying code snippets in the UI, use a CodeEditor component (read-only) rather than plain text elements, to provide proper syntax highlighting and visual context.
Do not mark test cases as async when they contain no await expressions.
When introducing a feature toggle to switch between old and new implementations, keep the old implementation unchanged so it remains a valid rollback target.
Public API method names should not expose underlying implementation details like SDK version numbers; use generic names that describe the operation.
When wrapping an input stream, provide a way to regenerate it (e.g., via a factory lambda) rather than holding a direct reference that can only be read once.
In Scala, prefer case classes with public vals over regular classes with private fields and explicit Java-style getter methods.
If fields in a Scala class require @unused annotations because they are private, convert to a case class to make them public getters and eliminate the suppression.
Keep related source files in the same package or directory even when module/build boundaries differ, to improve discoverability.
When renaming an attribute, update all usage sites directly instead of adding compatibility aliases that obscure the actual type and add unnecessary indirection.
Remove fields, methods, and wrapper operations that are not used in production; do not include code that has no current consumers.
Do not add production wrapper methods solely to support test code; instead, have tests use the underlying client or SDK directly.
When a method duplicates the logic of an existing method with only minor argument wrapping, consolidate callers to use the canonical method instead of maintaining both.
Feature flags and configuration concerns should not be embedded in domain or data wrapper objects; keep them at the infrastructure or injection layer.
Unpack settings objects and pass only the specific values a component needs, rather than passing the entire settings object, to minimize coupling and clarify dependencies.

New configuration settings should be added to the appropriate domain-specific settings class (e.g., ComputeGridSettings) rather than the global Settings file, using optional fields with sensible defaults.
When a value has more than two meaningful states (e.g., waiting, requested, completed), use an enum or sealed type rather than a boolean, and name it to capture the intent of each transition.
When modifying or replacing an API endpoint, keep the old endpoint functional for backward compatibility, especially when external consumers or agents may depend on it.
When testing state-dependent logic, include negative test cases that verify the system does NOT perform an action when it shouldn't, not just that it performs correctly when it should.
Mutable state accessed by concurrent callers must be properly synchronized using atomic references or other concurrency primitives; a plain mutable variable is not safe.
Don't route simple operations like writing a file through heavyweight abstractions (state machines, actor messages) when a direct call from the request handler would suffice.
Comments on configuration fields and methods must describe the complete behavior, not just part of it; if a timeout triggers both deduplication and forced termination, both must be documented.
When introducing configurable timeouts or limits that affect user-facing behavior (e.g., post-run script execution windows), ensure public documentation exists so users understand the constraints.
When adding new build tools or CLI utilities, update the relevant architecture documentation (wikis, ADRs) so the team understands how the new tool fits into the system.
Import aliases for internal packages (e.g., @domino/ui/dist) should resolve to compiled output, not source code, to avoid breaking React contexts and causing build inconsistencies.
Use scoped package names (e.g., @domino/ui, @domino/components) for import aliases to make the nature and origin of the import explicit.

When migrating to a new build tool (e.g., tsc to Vite), replace all relevant scripts (build, watch, type-check) with the new tool's equivalents rather than leaving legacy scripts alongside the new ones.

Fields whose values are derived from other data (e.g., a path computed from a container config) should not be stored in the database; compute them at read time to avoid staleness and maintenance burden.
When exposing configuration values through API endpoints, assess whether sensitive secrets could leak outside their intended security boundary.
Use relative imports for files within the same frontend module rather than absolute imports to maintain module encapsulation.
Reuse existing shared mock data from centralized mock directories (e.g., api/__mocks__) instead of creating duplicate mocks within individual component directories.
Do not mark fields or parameters as optional unless there is a concrete reason they may be absent; unnecessary optionals weaken type safety.
When adding a new field to a domain mapping or data transformation, update or add a test that verifies the field is correctly set.
After a state-changing action on a detail view (e.g., restore, archive), refetch the entity and stay on the detail page rather than navigating back to the list.
Keep core traits and interfaces clean of external library dependencies; refactor shared classes so a dependency-free trait can be extracted.
Conversion and mapping logic should live on the relevant domain class or its companion object rather than in unrelated service code.
When transforming a collection, prefer a single map/traversal that produces the final result rather than chaining separate map, zip, and toMap calls that create unnecessary intermediate collections.

When an existing role or entity has a semantically loaded name that doesn't exactly match the new use case, create a new purpose-specific one rather than repurposing it, to avoid future bugs when the original's permissions evolve independently.

When a useEffect callback dependency becomes stable (e.g., wrapped in useCallback with empty deps), add it to the dependency array rather than leaving it out, to keep the hook correct and avoid stale-closure bugs.
When a function parameter has an obvious default value used by most callers, make it optional with that default rather than requiring every caller to pass it explicitly.

When the same literal string or value appears multiple times in a test file, extract it into a shared variable (e.g., in the Background section for feature files) to reduce duplication and ease future updates.
E2E tests should not assert on excessive specific UI copy; reduce text-matching assertions to only what is essential for validating behavior, so tests are resilient to copy changes.

When a component toggles between states (e.g., expand/collapse), its data-testid and accessible label should update dynamically to reflect the current state, improving both testability and screen-reader experience.
When adding or changing feature flags, configuration, or shared resources in tests, verify that the change does not create side effects for other tests running in parallel.
