Every new service or domain class (e.g., a Resolver or Handler) should have a corresponding *Spec.scala test file; do not merge new logic without tests.
Remove all println, console.log, or other debug output statements before submitting code for review; use structured logging instead if runtime output is needed.

When introducing a default page size or limit for a list endpoint, research actual production data volumes (e.g., number of environments, EDVs, snapshots) to ensure the default serves the majority of users without requiring extra pagination.
When adding a new Bazel project/module, run the project generation script (e.g., dev/bazel/gen_bazelproject.py) so that IDE tooling like IntelliJ picks up the new target.
When impersonating a user or using elevated privileges, add a comment explaining why it is safe and what authorization context is being used.

API controller operations should use structured logging that includes all relevant context (user ID, entity IDs, operation name) on both success and failure paths, ideally via a reusable helper that captures context once.
When a method delegates to another service, write a unit test that verifies the delegate is called with the correct arguments.
Before logging variables that may contain HTTP headers or request metadata, verify that authentication secrets and tokens are not included in the output.
Catch `NonFatal` exceptions instead of broad exception types like `RuntimeException` to avoid swallowing fatal JVM errors.
Error log messages must include the operation that failed and all relevant entity identifiers to enable effective debugging.
Calling `.get` on an `Option` is a code smell; if the value is always expected to be present, refactor the return type to a non-optional type instead.
API response type names should make sense for every endpoint that uses them; avoid naming a shared response after a single operation (e.g., 'SagasStartedResponse' is wrong for a stop endpoint).
When logging errors, pass the exception object as a parameter to the logger method so the stack trace is captured, rather than only logging the message string.
Capitalize Domino-specific product entity names (Workspace, Model API, Artifacts, Datasets) but use lowercase for generic terms (published apps, docs) in UI copy.
Capitalize product feature names (e.g., 'Exports', 'Feature Store') when referring to the named feature, but use lowercase for generic actions (e.g., 'export files from a project').
Use a consistent format for help links across the UI: 'Learn more about [feature name]' without trailing punctuation.
Every TODO comment in code must have an associated JIRA ticket to ensure it gets tracked and resolved.
Tests that launch jobs or long-running operations should automatically collect debugging artifacts (e.g., support bundles) when the operation fails.
Tests that make HTTP requests should explicitly assert the response status code rather than only checking the response body.
Magic numbers used in multiple places (e.g., sleep durations, timeouts) should be extracted into named constants or a shared configuration file.
Do not specify multiple conflicting version constraints for the same dependency in a requirements file; combine them into a single constraint expression.
When adding a feature flag or configuration toggle, add unit tests that validate the correct behavior under each flag state to prevent regressions.
Do not skip or comment out tests without documenting the reason and creating a JIRA ticket to re-enable them.
Components should inject their own parameters into callbacks rather than requiring parent components to pass curried functions down through multiple levels.
Do not add eslint-disable comments for useEffect dependency arrays, especially when the dependencies are simple value types like booleans that are cheap to compare.
Wrap React context provider values in useMemo to prevent unnecessary re-renders of all child components that consume the context.
Files named styles.tsx should only contain styled-components; if a file contains a standalone component with its own logic, it should be extracted to its own file.
The `useX` naming convention is reserved for React hooks; do not use it for component props, as it misleads readers into thinking it is a hook.
Name components to accurately reflect what they render; do not include 'Button' in a component name if the component is not a button.
In Scala catch blocks, use `NonFatal` pattern matching to avoid catching fatal errors (like OutOfMemoryError) that should propagate and crash the application.
When the same string literal is used as a default value in multiple places, extract it into a shared constant to avoid inconsistency and duplication.
E2E tests should assert the semantically correct HTTP status code (e.g., 403 for forbidden access) rather than accepting any 4xx error.
Do not create a container/wrapper component when there is no significant business logic or backend calls to justify the separation from the presentational component.
In BDD/Gherkin feature files, steps that verify or assert an outcome should start with 'Then', not 'And' or other keywords.
When a test step exists for a non-obvious reason (such as verifying the absence of a previously known bug), add a comment explaining the intent.
Use Option/Optional types to represent absent values instead of defaulting to empty strings.
Obtain entity IDs from API responses rather than parsing them out of URLs.
When introducing a new config value that changes behavior, deploy with a safe no-op default first, then change the value in a follow-up PR to prevent breaking production.
When adding config files (e.g. .env.*), ensure they are COPY'd in the Dockerfile and validated with a container structure test.
Place new configuration keys in the existing section designated for that feature area rather than adding them in an arbitrary location.
Do not expose internal implementation details (such as retry library callbacks) in utility function signatures; keep them encapsulated so the underlying library can be swapped later.
Use existing helper methods (e.g. wait_for_status) instead of reimplementing polling or status-check logic inline in tests.
In error handlers for async data fetching, reset all dependent state variables (e.g. flags and content) to safe defaults, not just the error state.
Create a styled component for icons and elements instead of targeting them with nested CSS class selectors.
Place data detection and validation logic on the backend where the source of truth exists, rather than reimplementing complex matching logic on the frontend.
Define static constants outside the React component's function scope to avoid unnecessary re-creation on each render.
Absolute imports must come before relative imports; rely on eslint/prettier auto-fix to enforce consistent import ordering.
Do not mark a function as async if it contains no await expressions.
Add an explanatory comment for return statements whose purpose is not immediately clear from the surrounding code.
Styled components must be defined outside the React component body, not inline within JSX.
Remove commented-out code before merging; use version control history to recover deleted code if needed.
Define static constants (arrays, objects, config values) outside the React component function to avoid unnecessary re-creation on each render.
Prefer styled components over inline styles, especially when the same style is applied to multiple elements.
When modifying code near eslint-disable comments, verify the directive still applies to the correct line and does not break the original suppression intent.
When deleting a file, also remove all references to it (CI configs, imports, test steps) to prevent broken builds.
When changing how configuration values are parsed or validated, verify the change does not break upgrades for customers with existing configurations.
Place reusable test helpers in the shared tests-lib module rather than duplicating them in individual service test directories.
When a provider has multiple distinct implementations (e.g., cloud vs. datacenter), use separate feature flags so each can be toggled independently.
When an enum value's name does not clearly convey what it represents (e.g., a legacy key), add a comment explaining the mapping.
When the same transformation or sanitization logic appears in multiple places, move it to a shared domain object so consumers get the behavior for free.
When parsing or manipulating strings (e.g., stripping suffixes), consider edge cases where the target pattern appears multiple times in the input.
Avoid marking a value as lazy when it is used on the very next line; the deferred evaluation adds overhead with no benefit.
When an operation can fail for multiple reasons, write error messages that are general enough to be accurate for any failure mode rather than assuming a single cause.
When matching elements between two collections, convert one to a Set for O(1) lookups instead of nested iteration that produces O(n²) runtime.
Prefer idiomatic collection operations like collect with partial functions over manual filter-then-map chains for cleaner, more readable code.
Name variables to reflect their domain meaning (e.g., workspaceId instead of a generic name) so readers immediately understand the value's purpose.
Provider-specific methods should live on the provider's own implementation class, not on a shared base client used by all providers.
When adding a new provider, verify it handles the same edge cases (e.g., missing branches) as existing providers to maintain consistent behavior.
Use functional array methods like map, filter, and reduce instead of imperatively creating arrays and pushing elements into them.
Define styled components outside the React component body to prevent them from being regenerated on every re-render.
Show a loading indicator while asynchronous data (e.g., dropdowns, selectors) is being fetched to give users feedback that content is on the way.
Import shared internal component wrappers (e.g., tooltipRenderer) instead of importing directly from third-party libraries like antd to maintain consistency and allow centralized customization.
Use shared form components like FormItem for consistent styling and layout across the application rather than ad-hoc form markup.
Configuration keys must exactly match the names referenced in the code that reads them; mismatches cause silent failures.
Define default values in one canonical location (e.g., the config file) and remove duplicate defaults from code to avoid drift between them.
Remove personal identifiers, debug print statements, and temporary markers from code before merging a PR.
Name variables and config flags to precisely describe what they represent (e.g., use 'Count' suffix for counts, 'Size' for byte sizes).
Do not mark route parameters as required if the controller already handles the missing/empty case with a default value.
When a method's parameter list or behavior changes, update the method name to accurately reflect the new contract.
Scope CSS and style overrides to the specific component or element that needs them, rather than applying broad selectors that affect unrelated components.
Remove all debugging print/log statements before submitting a PR for review.
Use the same DTO model structure across related endpoints (e.g., list and get-by-id) to avoid divergence and inconsistency.
Design DTOs and domain models so that invalid combinations of fields are unrepresentable at the type level, rather than relying on runtime validation.
User-facing error and validation messages should start with a capitalized word.
When the same logic appears in multiple files, extract it into a shared utility or hook to avoid duplication.
When a component has multiple conditional branches for a feature flag, consider splitting it into separate components — one per flag state — to reduce complexity.
Test mocks that could be reused across test suites should live in a shared mocks package, not alongside individual test files.
Always use design system color variables/tokens instead of hardcoded hex or color values in component styles.
Never use .get() on Scala Option values; use .map/.getOrElse or pattern matching to handle the None case safely.
When naming log events or event types, check that the name won't conflict or cause confusion with upcoming features that will use similar naming.
E2E test steps should not depend on specific UI page state when the same data can be obtained via API, as this makes tests brittle and limits reusability.
Always use the project's shared/common UI components (e.g., Domino modal) instead of creating custom one-off implementations, to ensure visual consistency and simplify future restyling.
When an API field is conditionally present, use Option/Optional types to encode optionality rather than adding separate boolean flags that duplicate intent and force callers to provide unused values.
When adding retry/wait logic to tests, extend the existing helper function rather than creating a new wrapper; tests that don't need retries can call the underlying API directly.
In tests, wait for a specific status transition (e.g., wait_for_status(stopped)) rather than using an overly generous max timeout, to make tests faster and failure causes clearer.
When implementing exponential backoff or retry delay calculations, ensure the math cannot overflow numeric type limits as attempt counts grow large.

When modifying the semantics of API parameters (e.g., making fields optional or changing nullability), update the corresponding scaladoc/documentation to describe the new behavior (e.g., 'Ignore if None, Unassign if Some(None)').
When setting a boolean prop to true in JSX, use the shorthand form (e.g., `<Route exact />`) instead of the explicit form (`exact={true}`).
When adding or changing fields in a backend API, verify whether frontend mocks, TypeScript types, or Swagger/OpenAPI docs also need to be updated to stay in sync.
Every bug fix PR should include a test that reproduces the bug and verifies the fix.
When gating a feature behind a feature flag, ensure all related UI elements (checkboxes, buttons, columns) are also hidden, not just the primary component.
In Python test helpers, use python-box (or equivalent established library) for dict-to-attribute access instead of writing manual conversion logic.
When polling for a status change, short-circuit on all terminal statuses (error, stopped, failed, etc.), not just the success state.
Drive hardcoded values (URLs, thresholds, environment-specific strings) from constants defined in a config file rather than inlining them.
CI shell scripts should use `set -exuo pipefail` for debuggability and fail-fast behavior, and consolidate related commands into a single step to reduce complexity.
Replace or annotate magic literals (like `true true`) with named variables or code comments so their purpose is immediately clear.
Use the roles-and-permissions system to model access control rather than writing ad-hoc conditional authorization checks; create tech debt tickets if refactoring is needed to unblock a release.
Split authorization checks so each query only requires the permissions it actually needs, rather than demanding broad authorization for narrowly scoped operations.
Add explicit type annotations to vals/variables that hold complex expressions (tuples, mapped collections) to improve readability.
When implementing search or filter functionality on user-provided strings, normalize to lowercase before comparison to avoid case-sensitivity bugs.
Follow established project naming conventions (e.g., directory-per-hook with matching names) consistently, even when the resulting names are long.
Use the standard Domino modal and other shared design system components instead of building custom implementations, to maintain UI consistency.
Only update state or flags after the associated asynchronous operation (e.g., remote call, database write) completes successfully, not before.
Critical business logic such as cache refresh and invalidation must have dedicated tests, even if mocking is difficult — find a way to test it.
When adding or changing operational configuration (timeouts, feature flags, cache settings), document it in the relevant runbook so on-call engineers can find it.
Test helper functions that detect failures should call record_failure (or equivalent) so that support bundles and diagnostic artifacts are captured for debugging.
When implementing retry logic for opaque exceptions, match on specific known error messages rather than broad exception types to avoid retrying non-retryable failures.
Log messages and status values should accurately describe the operation being performed (e.g., use 'Stopped' not 'Delete' when stopping a run).
Sample or example deployment configurations should include comments warning where production configs need to be more restrictive (e.g., network policies, RBAC).
Use generic type parameters or `unknown` instead of `any` in TypeScript component APIs to preserve type safety.
When two methods return the same result, consolidate them into a single method to reduce confusion and maintenance burden.
Place fields on the DTO that conceptually owns them rather than overloading a related DTO with cross-cutting concerns.
Error messages thrown from domain-specific code should include the domain entity name so the error is immediately identifiable in logs and stack traces.
When a find-then-throw-if-missing pattern repeats across a codebase, extract it into a dedicated `findOrThrow` method on the relevant manager or repository.
When a refactoring removes most of a file's contents, delete the file entirely rather than leaving a near-empty shell.
When an inline expression grows complex (nested maps, Options, chained accessors), assign it to a descriptively named variable to improve readability.
When the same few lines of logic appear multiple times in a file, extract them into a dedicated method to reduce duplication and maintenance risk.
When an Ant Design table column simply displays the field value without custom formatting, use `dataIndex` alone and omit the `render` prop.
Styled components should live in their own component files or alongside the component that uses them, not in shared utility files.
Application route paths and link constants should be defined in the core routing module, not scattered across utility or feature files.
Function and step names should include the domain or service they interact with (e.g., 'keycloak') to avoid ambiguity, even if it feels redundant — clarity for readers outweighs brevity.
If a function is called only once and there is no clear reason to expect reuse, inline it rather than extracting it into a separate function.
Magic literals (strings, URLs, file paths) embedded in the middle of functions should be extracted to a config file and referenced from there.

When iterating over items to perform operations that can fail, extract the loop body into a function that fails gracefully so that a failure on one item does not prevent processing the remaining items.
Route components should use lazy loading to avoid loading unnecessary code upfront and improve initial page load performance.
When a component maps between a fixed set of values (e.g., type to icon), use a dictionary/map rather than conditional logic to make the mapping explicit and easy to extend.
When accessing DOM element attributes in tests, prefer getAttribute('href') over type casting to avoid unnecessary type assertions and keep test code idiomatic.
Debug log messages should include sufficient context (e.g., the object being operated on) so that logs are useful for troubleshooting without additional instrumentation.
If a function returns the same result every render and takes no arguments, define its output as a constant outside the component to avoid unnecessary recomputation.
Pin new dependency versions in package.json to avoid unexpected breaking changes from semver-range updates.
Reference color values from the shared design token file (e.g., colors.ts) instead of hardcoding hex values in component files.
When building new UI features, use the project's existing shared components (modals, buttons, etc.) to maintain visual consistency across the application.
Replace magic numbers with named constants that convey the value's purpose.
When changing the semantics of a value, update all variable and string references to reflect the new meaning — stale names mislead future readers.

When a test checks for the presence of unwanted resources or states, ensure it actually fails (e.g., raises an exception or calls pytest.fail) when the condition is detected, rather than silently passing.
Use the project's structured audit event system instead of logger.info with hand-formatted strings for actions that need to be auditable.
Do not add audit or log events at intermediate processing steps if the completing step already records the same information.
When renaming a parameter in one file, update the corresponding parameter names in all related interfaces, traits, and adapter files to keep the codebase consistent.
Before adding audit events to an operation, evaluate how frequently it fires in practice — high-frequency triggers (e.g., file preview clicks) can create excessive noise.
Use 'preview' instead of 'beta' for pre-release API path segments to maintain consistent terminology across the platform.
Use 201 for resource creation, 200 for responses with a body, and 204 for delete operations with no response body — do not mix them up.
Name creation request DTOs as `New[EntityName]` and update request DTOs as `Updated[EntityName]` to clearly distinguish their purpose in the API contract.
Use PATCH instead of PUT for update endpoints to support partial updates and avoid requiring clients to send the full resource representation.
Write OpenAPI operation descriptions in third person (e.g., 'Gets all resources') rather than imperative or first person.
Use primary key IDs rather than names when referencing other entities in API contracts to avoid sync complexity if entities are renamed.

When a parent and its children should be created as a unit (e.g., from a single UI confirmation), include child resources in the parent creation request rather than splitting across multiple endpoints to avoid partial success states.
When replacing child resources during a parent update, archive the old records instead of deleting them to preserve referential integrity from existing associations.
Verify that status codes declared in the OpenAPI spec match what the server actually returns at runtime, especially when code generators may silently override spec values.
Do not reuse the same endpoint for admin and regular user access when the response shape, visible fields, or permission model differs — create distinct endpoints to avoid leaking admin-only data.
Use consistent phrasing across similar API endpoint descriptions (e.g., if one list endpoint says 'based on the provided filters', all similar list endpoints should use the same wording).
Update/PATCH request schemas should not mark fields as required, since an update may only modify a subset of fields.
When introducing a replacement for existing code (new API spec, new module, etc.), create a ticket to track removal of the old version so it doesn't get forgotten.
Add debug logs at key checkpoints (e.g., readiness checks, polling loops) that include identifying context such as resource name, current count, and expected count.
Internal infrastructure errors and retry failures should not bubble up to user-facing output; keep them in system logs only.
Avoid embedding route paths directly inside UI components; centralize route definitions so they can be maintained in one place.
Before implementing custom behavior (e.g., manually destroying inactive tabs), check if the component library already provides a prop or built-in feature for it.
Before writing new state management logic for common UI patterns (tabs, search params, filters), check if an internal hook or utility already exists in the codebase.
In pattern matches or switch statements, let removed or legacy cases fall through to the default/wildcard handler rather than listing them explicitly.
Use idiomatic Scala collection methods like `.map(...).toMap` and `.collect` with pattern matching instead of imperative loop-and-accumulate patterns.
Initialize collections with their intended values at declaration rather than creating an empty collection and mutating it afterward.
New or modified integration endpoints (especially third-party provider integrations) must have corresponding system tests that exercise the real endpoint.
Test scenario titles should precisely describe the behavior being validated, not a vague or inaccurate summary of the feature area.
Use ES module syntax (import/export) and appropriate file extensions (.mts) instead of CommonJS require() when the toolchain supports it.
Before adding a new dependency injection binding, check whether the same binding already exists in another injection module to avoid duplicates.

Methods should be placed in the service that owns the domain concept, not in a related but semantically incorrect service (e.g., launcher config belongs in the launcher service, not the launcher job service).
Class names should clearly communicate the entity's purpose; avoid vague, cryptic, or 'weird' names that force readers to inspect the implementation to understand intent.
Add a description or doc comment to entity classes and data structures whose purpose is not immediately obvious from the name alone.
Always use strict equality (===) instead of loose equality (==) for comparisons in JavaScript and TypeScript code.
Order import statements so that absolute (library/package) imports come before relative (local module) imports.
Prefer separate, flat styled components over nesting styles within a single styled component when you control the child elements.
Use the short syntax <> instead of React.Fragment when no key or attributes are needed.
Side effects like changing user selection or UI state after an async operation must be gated on the success path, not executed unconditionally after the operation completes.
Place feature components in descriptive subfolders (e.g., guardrails/project/models/) rather than dumping them at the module root.
Use JSX interpolation with template expressions instead of the + operator for string concatenation inside JSX.
Trim JSON test fixtures to the minimum data needed to exercise the test case; avoid committing large, unredacted API responses.
When pattern matching an Option only to return None for None and transform Some, use .map() instead of an explicit match expression.
E2E test scenarios that create resources (apps, workspaces, etc.) should include cleanup steps to stop or remove them after the scenario completes.
Before writing inline utility logic, check for existing global or shared helpers (e.g., getAppName) and use them instead of reimplementing.
When renaming or removing a configuration flag or central config key, evaluate whether a database migration is needed for existing persisted values.
Code review comments should not be marked as resolved until the underlying question or concern has been actually addressed or answered.
When updating configuration values for a new version, ensure the same change is applied consistently across all version entries that should share the value.
Always validate YAML files in an IDE or linter before submitting to catch duplicate keys that silently override values.
Never resolve PR review comments until the underlying issue has actually been fixed in code.
Ensure the PR summary accurately reflects entity names and references used in the actual code diff.
Use >= rather than ~= for Python dependency pinning in requirements files to avoid breakage when transitive dependencies shift, unless a known incompatibility requires a tighter bound.
If a boolean or derived value is only used by one child component, compute it inside that child rather than passing it down as a prop from the parent.
Use the canonical package alias paths (e.g., @domino/core-ui/Link) instead of deep relative imports to keep imports consistent and refactor-friendly.
When adding new environment variable dependencies to tests, document them in the project README so other developers know to configure them.
Do not maintain duplicate configuration keys that hold the same value; consolidate to a single canonical key used everywhere.
Group test scenarios with descriptive comment headers to improve readability and make triage faster when tests fail.
When calling an async API in a React component, catch errors gracefully and show a loading indicator (spinner or disabled state) while the request is in flight.
Define explicit typed data structures for API contracts rather than storing arbitrary JSON blobs, to keep code readable, testable, and self-documenting.
When designing metadata schemas, capture all available identifiers even if currently redundant, to avoid breaking changes when future features require them.
When a React component's render method grows complex, extract logical sections into dedicated sub-components for readability and reusability.
Before writing a new helper, search the codebase for existing utilities that compute the same value to avoid duplication and inconsistency.
Always use version constraints (e.g., `>=`) when specifying Python dependencies in requirements files to prevent unexpected breakage from new releases.
When modifying shared test data files, verify all other tests that depend on the same file still pass before merging.
When changing shared configuration (e.g., environment images, tags), notify downstream test owners who may be affected by the change.
When working with external system values (e.g., Kubernetes resource quantities), use the official SDK's parsing and validation utilities rather than hand-rolling string conversions.
When fixing a bug in a class that lacks tests, add a test class and cover at least the fixed scenario rather than leaving it untested.
Use the narrowest fixture scope possible (function over session) to avoid unnecessary environment setup when running subsets of tests.
Name route-building or utility functions to reflect their specific purpose (e.g., `getProjectLauncherPath` instead of a generic name) so callers understand intent without reading the implementation.
Use `2>/dev/null` to suppress stderr output; do not use `2>&1` which merges stderr into stdout instead of discarding it.
Include a correlation ID in error responses so operators can match the user-facing error to the corresponding entry in server logs.
When renaming or modifying a shared configuration file, search for all services that include or reference it and update them in the same PR.
Before renaming shared configuration files, align with the original authors or domain owners on the new name to ensure it accurately reflects the file's purpose.
If a function's name implies a return value (e.g., `isSomethingEnabled` suggesting a boolean), its actual behavior must match — rename it if it throws exceptions or has side effects instead.
When reviewing tests, verify that both positive (valid input producing expected output) and negative (invalid input returning undefined/error) cases are covered.
Do not nest configuration providers (e.g., ConfigProvider) inside feature components; instead, use existing component props to pass customization.
Use JSDoc block comment format (/** ... */) for component and function documentation so IDEs display it in hover hints.
When removing or renaming fields in serialized models, explicitly verify serialization compatibility and create a separate ticket to track the breaking change.
Boolean props or parameters that modify default behavior should be optional with a sensible default value (typically false), rather than required.
Use template literals for string concatenation and guard against undefined values, since undefined + 'string' produces 'undefinedstring'.
Extract magic numbers (e.g., max lengths, limits) into named constants so they can be reused consistently across related logic.
Maintain consistent import ordering: absolute/library imports must come before relative imports.
Keep all styled components in a single location per module — either all in a dedicated styles file or all co-located in the component file, never mixed.
Do not pass empty no-op callback functions (e.g., onChange={() => {}}) when the framework or parent component already handles the behavior.
When identical logic appears in multiple components, extract it into a single shared utility function rather than duplicating it.
Extract repeated string literals (especially query field names and filter keys) into named constants at the class or module level.
Add test cases for implicit type coercion boundaries (e.g., integer-to-double conversion) in serialization and deserialization logic.
When using loggers inside nested or helper functions, verify they actually produce output, as loggers can silently fail when invoked outside their configured context.
Review hardcoded timeout and wait values critically; suspiciously long durations (e.g., one hour) should be justified or reduced, even if inherited from existing code.
Remove all commented-out code before submitting a PR; version control preserves history, so dead code should be deleted, not commented.
Do not wrap child components in render functions when the parent is not injecting anything through the render prop; use the component directly as a child instead.

Never skip required dependencies in useEffect; if a function references external variables, either declare them as dependencies or define the function inside the useEffect to keep the dependency array honest.
Every async operation that can fail must surface feedback to the user; silent failures erode trust and make debugging impossible for end users.
When using Ant Design Form components, let the Form manage field state rather than using it only as a styling/layout wrapper with separate state management.
Always set the name property on Ant Design Form.Item components so the form can properly track and manage field values.
Do not wrap JSX in a React Fragment when only a single child element is returned; fragments should only be used to group multiple siblings without adding a DOM node.

Functions that do not reference component state, props, or hooks should be defined outside the component body to avoid unnecessary re-creation on each render and to clarify that they are pure utilities.
In UI tests, prefer async waitFor assertions over hardcoded setTimeout delays; fixed delays are flaky and slow, while waitFor polls until the condition is met.
Review PRs for leftover debug statements, temporary scaffolding, and test artifacts that were not intended to be committed.
Do not encode known/fixed values (e.g., type: object) in API specs when they can be enforced programmatically; keep specs declarative and lean.
When designing schemas that may eventually be user-defined, minimize surface area now to reduce future validation burden.
When a field in an API spec has a fixed set of allowed values, always declare an enum constraint to prevent invalid input.
Use explicit state flags (e.g., stopProps being set) to determine intent rather than inferring meaning from a status enum, which may conflate different scenarios.
If a component prop is always passed by every caller, declare it as required; optional props should only be used when callers genuinely omit the value.
Write test step definitions that clearly describe their action; avoid 'clever' steps that bundle multiple behaviors in non-obvious ways, as future authors won't reuse them.
Review test scenarios for steps that duplicate assertions or setup already performed by prior steps, and remove them to keep scenarios concise.
Prefer explicit constructor parameters over Scala implicits, as implicit params create hidden nullary constructors that produce surprising instantiation behavior.
Follow the project's established import ordering convention; keep imports grouped and sorted consistently across files.
When multiple permission check paths (e.g., All vs. ByName) have nearly identical logic, consolidate them into a single check to reduce duplication and maintenance burden.
When assigning or restricting permissions for user roles (especially lite/restricted roles), explicitly verify the expected access level with the product manager before merging.
Use React's useId() hook to generate stable, unique IDs for DOM elements instead of manual ID generation to avoid collisions and hydration mismatches.

In e2e tests, ensure test entities (orgs, projects) are created by the appropriate user role so that the entity owner is not part of the group being tested, avoiding false positives from implicit owner permissions.
When adding new configuration values, verify whether existing code pulls and overrides these values from a remote source (e.g., nucleus), and ensure the override precedence is correct.
Use default wait timeouts in e2e tests unless there is a specific, documented reason to override them; custom timeouts add unnecessary complexity and divergence.
Use useMemo to create a debounced handler and useCallback for the change handler instead of wiring debounce logic through useEffect.
Absolute imports must appear before relative imports in the import block.
Replace growing if/else or switch chains with a list and Array.includes() to keep conditional structures from expanding unboundedly.
Do not add explicit cleanup steps in tests when the test framework or setup already handles that cleanup automatically.
Index files should only re-export; do not define function implementations directly in index.ts files.
API endpoint wrappers should be added to the shared @domino/api package rather than defined inline in feature modules.
When a type is useful beyond the component that defines it, export it from the shared package so consumers don't have to re-declare it.
Modals should be agnostic of parent-level side effects like data refetching; pass an onClose or onSuccess callback from the parent instead.
When a mutation affects multiple entities, expose an onSuccess callback so parent components can coordinate follow-up actions like refetching related data.
When a user action triggers multiple sequential API calls, display a loading indicator so the user knows the operation is in progress.
Before creating a new utility function, check whether adding a parameter to an existing function achieves the same result with less surface area.
In authorization-related classes and methods, place the principal or identity parameter at the top of the parameter list for consistency.
Do not use explicit `any` as a type annotation when TypeScript can infer the type automatically; rely on type inference instead.
When modifying a handler or function used by multiple callers, verify that existing integration/e2e tests covering all call sites still pass before merging.

Generic infrastructure code (e.g., AWS SDK wrappers) should live in shared modules like common-core, not in domain-specific packages; replace domain-specific config objects with primitive parameters to remove coupling.
When wrapping SDK types, adopt the naming conventions of the target SDK version (e.g., use 'Response' instead of 'Result' when migrating to AWS SDK v2).
When wrapping exceptions across SDK versions, define proper custom exceptions that set message and cause on the parent Throwable; never throw wrapper objects that lose the original stack trace.
Do not include code in a PR that cannot be exercised or validated until a future PR; defer it to keep the PR reviewable and testable.
Generic trait methods should return structured types or Unit, not serialized strings; let callers decide how to serialize if needed.
When the same conversion or transformation logic (e.g., safe number parsing) is duplicated across multiple places, extract it into a shared utility function.

Use consistent naming suffixes and verb choices across related type families (e.g., all event types should follow the same pattern like 'BulkUpdate' / 'BulkDelete' rather than mixing 'Change', 'Updated', 'UpdateChange').

Authentication redirect logic must be scoped to the specific routes that require it; do not place auth guards in always-mounted wrapper components where they can inadvertently redirect anonymous-accessible routes to login.
Always roll back temporary or unstable dependency versions to a stable develop branch version before merging a PR.
Avoid using prototype method calls directly on objects; prefer static methods like Object.keys(obj).forEach() instead.
useEffect dependency arrays must only include values actually used inside the effect; do not add extra dependencies that are not referenced.
Absolute imports should appear before relative imports in the import block.
Use theme helper tokens (e.g., themeHelper('margins.tiny')) for spacing values instead of hardcoded CSS values.
Do not use onSuccess/onError callback patterns for API requests; let exceptions propagate and handle them with try/catch or .catch() at the component/hook level.
API request functions should not handle error notification concerns; error alerting and UI feedback should be the responsibility of the consuming component or hook.
When catching errors in the UI, consider whether the user should be alerted rather than silently logging to the console.
Avoid nested ternary expressions; use if/else blocks, early returns, or helper functions for readability.
Move constant values outside of function bodies and name them using UPPER_SNAKE_CASE.
Functions that use magic numbers or non-obvious numeric thresholds should include documentation explaining what the numbers represent and what the function is expected to return.
Replace raw numeric literals with named constants that convey their meaning.
Use explicit TypeScript return type annotations on functions instead of casting the return value, which provides better type safety and cleaner code.
When conditionally rendering in React, return null rather than an empty wrapper element like <span> or <div> to avoid polluting the DOM.
When using styled-components, define all styles in the styled component definition rather than using inline style props, to keep styling centralized and maintainable.
In JSX, pass string literal props without curly braces (e.g., prop="value" not prop={"value"}) for cleaner syntax.
Use Array.some() instead of filter-and-check-length when you only need to know whether any element matches a condition.
Keep utility/helper functions in separate utility files rather than defining them inside component files, to improve reusability and separation of concerns.
Move constant/static data and derived values that don't depend on props or state outside the React component body to avoid unnecessary recalculation on every render.
Reference colors from the centralized colors.ts (or design token) file rather than defining ad-hoc color values in components.
Do not use className-based styling when styled-components are available; classNames should only be a last resort for unreachable nested elements.
When the same style override appears in multiple views, move it to the shared component that renders the styled element to keep styling DRY.
When a piece of UI is self-contained, extract it into its own component file and render it from the parent, rather than inlining the full markup.
When writing ScalaDoc, document all parameters and include a sentence describing what the method does, not just a subset of the information.
Ensure return types are consistent and intentional across related methods; if callers discard a return value, the method should return Unit to avoid confusion.
Write test descriptions that describe expected behavior in plain language (e.g., 'should return empty list when no items match') rather than referencing method names (e.g., 'should testGetItems').
Wrapper or data classes should not extend RuntimeException unless they genuinely represent throwable errors.
Use Try or Either to represent operations that may fail instead of throwing exceptions, so callers can pattern-match on success/failure.
When a method returns a generic type (e.g., String) that represents something domain-specific, add scaladoc or equivalent documentation explaining what the returned value actually is.
When migrating between SDK versions, verify that serialized representations (toString, JSON, etc.) remain compatible, especially if downstream consumers depend on the format.
Before removing or merging code that appears duplicated, verify with the original author whether the duplication was intentional to avoid silent behavior changes.
When changing how assets are bundled or built, verify the output works in the deployed environment, not just in local development.
Integration tests should use real implementations (e.g., real persisters/databases), not mocks; if mocks are used, the test should be reclassified as a unit test.
Capitalize domain terms (e.g., Dataset, ID) consistently in client-facing error messages, API route descriptions, and user-visible strings.
Only add TODO comments for work that is planned within the current initiative or near-term roadmap; avoid speculative TODOs that may linger in the codebase indefinitely.
Remove redundant words from field names when the context already conveys the meaning (e.g., use `pvcName` instead of `persistentVolumeClaimName` when the type is already a PVC).
Field names must accurately reflect what the value represents; do not name a field `pvId` if it holds a volume name rather than an identifier.
Include a `deletedAt` timestamp field on entities that support soft deletion to maintain an audit trail of when records were removed.
Add telemetry events to CRUD methods so that create, read, update, and delete operations are observable in production.
Add explicit tests that verify authorization checks are enforced, ensuring unauthorized users cannot access protected resources.
Add a comment explaining why a value is intentionally discarded (e.g., `val _ = ...`) so it is not accidentally removed during future refactoring.
When retrieving an entity by ID, throw an error with a descriptive message that includes the ID, rather than silently returning a default or an opaque failure.
When using setTimeout as a fallback, store the timeout ID and cancel it with clearTimeout if the primary operation completes first, to prevent stale side effects.
Assign the return value of `setTimeout()` (the timeout ID) to the variable, not the delay duration; mixing these up makes `clearTimeout` silently fail.
Define static constants (thresholds, config values, timeout durations) outside the React component body so they are not re-created on every render.
Replace magic numbers with named constants that convey intent (e.g., `const MAX_FILE_SIZE_MB = 64` instead of a bare `64`).
Hardcoded numeric constants must have a comment or named constant explaining how the value was chosen.
Every TODO comment in code must reference a JIRA ticket so the work is tracked and not forgotten.
Debug print/println statements must be removed or replaced with proper logging before merging to the main branch.
Enum values in Scala should use camelCase to match the project's naming conventions, even though other languages may use UPPER_SNAKE_CASE.
When catching or re-throwing exceptions, always preserve the original exception as the cause so stack trace and root cause information are not lost.
When setting multiple form or state values, combine them into a single call rather than making redundant sequential calls.
When upgrading libraries that change data serialization or markup formats, verify that existing persisted data still renders or functions correctly.
When introducing a new UI element that could be reused across modules, create a shared component in packages/ui rather than defining it inline in a feature module.

Maintain a consistent ordering of imports in Scala files, typically grouping by package hierarchy (java/scala stdlib first, then third-party, then internal packages) with alphabetical ordering within groups.
Do not re-declare dependencies that are already included transitively through other targets (e.g., if tests-lib already includes common-core and scalatest, don't list them again).
Move reusable cross-cutting concerns like filtering, sorting, and pagination into a commons module and extend them in downstream modules instead of duplicating the logic.
Do not use unexplained magic numbers in code; extract them into named constants or add a comment explaining the rationale so future readers understand the value's origin.
API route definitions should include $ref or enum constraints on parameters to enable automatic API validation rather than accepting unconstrained strings.
Use domain-specific typed enums (e.g., SortableColumn) instead of raw String types for API parameters to enable compile-time safety and API documentation validation.
Remove test data, fixtures, and helper entities that are no longer referenced by any test to keep the test suite lean and avoid confusion about what is actively tested.
Components should define sensible default values for props (e.g., orientation defaulting to 'horizontal') while allowing consumers to override via props.
Style values (colors, spacing, fonts) must come from the theme object, not be hardcoded in component styles.
Write Storybook stories in Component Story Format 3 (CSF3), not MDX, which is no longer supported in Storybook 8.
When a section of JSX represents a distinct, self-contained UI block, extract it into its own component for clarity and testability.
Do not use nested ternary expressions in JSX or logic; use early returns, if/else blocks, or lookup maps for readability.
Pass variant or state information as props to styled-components rather than toggling CSS class names.
Inline logic and render helpers should be extracted into pure functions or standalone components so they can be tested independently.
When wrapping a library component (e.g., Ant Design), maximize use of its built-in API to keep wrapper code minimal rather than reimplementing features.
Define Ant Design Menu items using the items array configuration (MenuItem[]) instead of the deprecated JSX children pattern (<Menu.Item>).
Components must reference the semantic color palette (e.g., colorPalette.textPrimary, colorPalette.bg) rather than specific color constants, to ensure correct behavior when switching themes.
Always import colors from the canonical colors.ts file, not from alternative files like colorsNew.ts, to ensure theme switching works correctly.
When wrapping Ant Design components, extend or pick/omit from the library's prop types instead of defining loose custom prop interfaces.
Use the Ant Design Theme Provider to customize component appearance instead of manually overriding styles in styled-components.
Use the themeHelper utility for accessing design tokens (colors, spacing, fonts) instead of hardcoding values in styled-components.
Always define theme keys in both light and dark mode configurations, even when the values are identical, to ensure flexibility for future design changes.
Always reference colors from the theme palette rather than using hardcoded hex values or deprecated color tokens.
Import UI components from Domino's component library, not directly from Ant Design — Domino wrappers enforce consistent theming and behavior.
Only use useMemo for genuinely expensive computations; avoid wrapping simple derivations that don't benefit from memoization.
Use spacing and dimension tokens from the theme (via themeHelper or theme dimensions) instead of raw rem or px values in styled-components.
Prefer async/await with try/catch over .then()/.catch() promise chains to maintain a consistent async error-handling style across the codebase.
Route-related code used across apps should live in the shared packages directory (e.g., packages/ui/src/core/routes), not in individual app modules.
Prefer adding proper TypeScript generics (e.g., on reduce, map) to eliminate type assertions rather than using `as` casts.
Mock data should use realistic example values that match the shape and format of real data to make tests and development more representative.
Use themeHelper utilities for font-size, margin, and other styling values instead of hardcoding them in styled components.
Replace switch statements or chained conditionals that map values with a dictionary/map lookup for clarity and maintainability.
Avoid inline styles in React components; use styled-components or CSS classes instead.
Use template literal string interpolation instead of string concatenation for readability.
Do not commit secrets, tokens, or credentials (e.g., in .npmrc) to version control; use a secrets management solution instead.
Avoid casting to `any` in TypeScript; find proper type definitions or workarounds to maintain type safety.
Import from canonical module paths (e.g., ui/globalStore/useStore) rather than deprecated aliases that may be removed.
Maintain consistent casing conventions across related identifiers (e.g., if the component is CustomSVG, related types and variables should follow the same casing pattern).
Use a dictionary or mapping for aria-label values so the same icon always gets the same label, enabling reliable QA/automation selectors.
API doc comments for parameters that accept a fixed set of values should enumerate those values explicitly.
When adding fields to a DTO or API response, verify that all downstream clients are tolerant of extra fields before merging.
Never import from a package's dist/build output folder within the same package; use relative paths instead.
Use color values from the design token palette (e.g. colors.palette.danger) instead of hardcoded hex or raw color values.
When making multiple API requests that don't depend on each other, use Promise.all to execute them in parallel.
Use the same established term for a concept at all layers of the application (DDD ubiquitous language); don't invent new names for things that already have official names.
Place configuration settings alongside other related settings rather than dumping them into a monolithic Settings class.
Always use the structured logger for output; never leave raw println statements in production code.
When a file format is no longer supported (e.g. MDX), remove the file rather than updating it to the deprecated format.
Don't repeat parent scope names in nested configuration keys; if a key is already inside a 'workflows.storage' namespace, don't prefix child keys with 'flows' or 'storage' again.
Use themeHelper utilities for colors and styling instead of raw values to ensure consistency with the design system.
Avoid inline styles in React components; use styled-components, CSS modules, or theme utilities instead.
Before creating custom UI elements, check if an existing component (e.g. ActionDropdown.Button) already provides the needed functionality.
Don't render a modal instance per table row; lift modal state to the parent so only one modal is ever mounted regardless of row count.
Use optional chaining with nullish coalescing (`arr[0]?.id ?? ''`) when accessing array elements, even when you expect the array to be non-empty.
Use `reduce` or other functional transforms instead of `let` variables with mutation when building derived values.
Do not import from your own package's published name (e.g., `@domino/ui`) within the same package; use relative imports instead.
Do not copy data from a custom hook (e.g., `useRemote`) into local state via `useEffect`; use the hook's returned data directly.
Remove unused function parameters from the definition instead of suppressing the ESLint rule with a disable comment.
When a component needs multiple fields from a single object, pass the object as one prop rather than spreading its properties into individual props.
Place TypeScript type and interface definitions in a dedicated `types.ts` file, not in `utils.ts` or other non-type files.
Do not use eslint-disable comments to work around type compatibility issues; fix the types so the code is naturally compatible.
Do not leave commented-out code without explanation; either remove it or add a TODO comment explaining why it is kept and when it should be restored.
Frontend filters must not mutate the original API data; keep a single immutable data source and compute filtered views from it using pure functions.
When adding try/catch blocks, ensure the catch path provides visible feedback to the user (e.g., a toast or error message) rather than silently swallowing the error.
Always use strict equality (`===`) instead of loose equality (`==`) for comparisons, unless there is an explicit reason to coerce types.
Use the nullish coalescing operator (??) instead of logical OR (||) for default values to avoid incorrectly treating falsy values like 0 or empty string as missing.
Define named constants (e.g., EMPTY_PLACEHOLDER) for repeated default or sentinel values instead of using inline literals across multiple locations.
Use themeHelper or theme token values for colors and fonts instead of hardcoded hex values or magic numbers in styled components.
Pass select/dropdown options as a data prop (array of objects) rather than as JSX children to keep components declarative and data-driven.
Remove commented-out code before merging; use version control history to retrieve old code instead of leaving dead code in the codebase.
Style values like colors and spacing should reference the design system's theme tokens rather than raw numbers, even when preserving existing code.
When introducing batched database queries, document why the chosen batch size is safe (e.g., won't exceed MongoDB's 16MB BSON document limit) and consider how it scales for large deployments.
When an API schema field has implicit constraints (e.g., nesting depth limits), document those constraints explicitly in the field description everywhere the assumption applies.
API schema fields, especially those with complex or non-obvious formats, should include inline examples to clarify expected values.
Do not leave unused imports, variables, or code in the codebase unless there is a concrete near-term need; remove them to reduce noise.
When a field accepts multiple types or has a default value, add test cases that exercise non-string and non-obvious value types to ensure correct handling.
If a class enforces invariants or throws exceptions, its name should reflect that responsibility (e.g., Checker or Enforcer), not imply a passive role like Logger.
Log messages should include the relevant identifiers (controller name, operation name) and indicate the lifecycle phase (started, completed) to support debugging and tracing.
When fields become optional, write tests for both extremes: all fields provided and no fields provided, not just partial combinations.
Method descriptions and doc comments within a file or module should follow the same punctuation and formatting conventions for consistency.
When defining API endpoints or configuration fields, include example values (e.g., example hostnames, URLs) to make usage clear.
Prefix class and type names with their owning module to prevent confusion with similarly-named concepts from other modules during cross-module refactors.
Use theme helpers and design tokens for styling values (font sizes, colors, spacing) instead of hardcoding raw values like '14px'.
Avoid using explicit `return` statements in Scala; rely on expression-based control flow instead, as `return` has surprising semantics in Scala.
Split React components into separate files rather than defining multiple components (especially controlled and uncontrolled variants) in a single file.
Define default or constant callback functions outside the React component body and pass them as references, rather than defining inline functions in component props.
Use `unknown` instead of `any` in TypeScript when the typed values are not actually accessed, to maintain type safety.
Extract repeated test setup (e.g., creating test fixtures like unauthorized principals) into reusable helper methods to reduce duplication in test files.
When caller context (e.g., a Principal) is already available, pass it through the call chain rather than performing a redundant lookup downstream.
Prefer `find -exec rm -rf {} +` over piping to `xargs` for batch file deletion; it is simpler, faster, and avoids pitfalls like swallowing output with >/dev/null or forgetting -mindepth.
Order import statements with absolute (package) imports before relative imports.
Before writing inline path construction or URL building logic, check for existing utility or generator functions in shared packages and reuse them (exporting if necessary).
When constructing URLs from dynamic values, validate that all parameters are defined before interpolation to avoid generating URLs with literal 'undefined' segments that lead to 404 errors.
Method names should accurately describe the operation they perform; a method that updates a resource must not be named 'create' and vice versa.

When implementing soft-delete for entities that store credentials in an external vault, explicitly consider whether retaining those credentials aligns with user expectations around deletion, and document the decision.
When suppressing compiler warnings, target the specific warning being suppressed rather than using broad suppression annotations that may hide future issues.
Interactions with external services like Vault should be extracted into a dedicated class rather than mixed into the main service implementation, improving testability and separation of concerns.
Mock data and test fixtures should be typed using the types generated from the API schema, not left untyped or manually defined, to ensure mocks stay in sync with the actual API contract.
Remove all debug and testing artifacts (hardcoded test IDs, console.logs, temporary strings) from code before submitting a PR for review.

When a value can be computed from existing props or state, calculate it directly (or use useMemo) rather than syncing it through a useEffect into a separate useState, which adds unnecessary complexity and re-renders.

When a form renders dynamic input types from a schema, extract each input type into its own component early to keep the rendering logic manageable as the number of input types and their validations grow.
Reference color values through the theme helper rather than importing color constants directly, ensuring consistent theming and easier future theme changes.
When changing an infrastructure reference (e.g., username, path, host), audit and update all related configuration points in the same PR.
Use standardized code comments to mark lines that must be updated when cutting a release branch, so they are easy to find and not missed.
When manually converting values one by one (e.g., enum members to constants), create a utility function to automate the conversion instead of doing it individually.
Use explicit names like `pageNumber` instead of ambiguous short names for pagination parameters to improve readability.
Use get-or-create patterns instead of separate get-then-create flows when a missing value should never cause a failure.
Lifecycle methods must come in symmetric pairs (pause/resume, start/stop); do not add one side without the other.
Error messages for state-management code should distinguish between possible invalid states rather than using generic messages.
Do not keep code for hypothetical use cases that are not needed in the current PR; remove unused abstractions to reduce complexity.

Features that only need to run in a specific pod (e.g., dispatcher) should be gated to that pod to avoid unnecessary resource consumption, k8s API load, and risk of unintended side effects when multiplied across all instances.
When watching Kubernetes resources, use field selectors to narrow the watch to only the specific resources needed rather than watching all resources in a namespace.
Any state that can be accessed by multiple threads must have explicit synchronization; use a dedicated lock object and protect both read and write access.
When protecting shared mutable state with locks, synchronize on read as well as write; prefer a dedicated lock object over synchronizing on `this` or the class.
When creating entities that span multiple systems, create the canonical record in the primary datastore first, then use its ID to propagate to external systems like identity providers.
Place important identifiers like IDs as the first (or near-first) parameters in method signatures so they are immediately visible.
Prefix class names with their domain scope (e.g., Idp, Billing) to clearly communicate responsibility and prevent ambiguity.

When parsing strings into structured types (e.g., UUIDs, enums), validate the format at the point of conversion and handle failures explicitly rather than letting them propagate as runtime exceptions.
Remove unused code rather than keeping it around speculatively; if it is not in use and not documented, it should be deleted.
Externalize polling intervals and timeout values as central configuration flags rather than hardcoding them, so they can be tuned without code changes.
When adding polling or repeated cross-service calls, evaluate whether requests can queue up and block other requests; add timeouts to mitigate cascading failures.
Verify that every endpoint has appropriate authentication and authorization checks, especially when copying patterns from existing code that may have different access requirements.
When suppressing deprecation warnings in build files, document which specific deprecations are being silenced and why they cannot be addressed in the current scope.
Keep imports and dependency declarations in a consistent, conventional order within files.
Use white-label / configurable product names instead of hardcoded brand names in UI components.
In Vite apps, use `import.meta.env.MODE` to check the application mode instead of `process.env.NODE_ENV`, and use strict equality (===) for safety.
Use ES module dynamic `import()` instead of CommonJS `require()` for conditional or lazy-loaded modules.
Before adopting a package, verify it supports your project's framework version (e.g., React 18) by checking the package documentation.
Do not add new ESLint rules as 'error' severity without a migration plan — it forces developers to use --no-verify or add excessive exceptions.
When wrapping a component, only declare prop types you actually forward; use Pick to select forwarded props, or use the rest operator to pass through remaining props.
Proactively extract sub-components (e.g., tab contents) into their own files before they grow too large, even if they are small initially.
Use NavTabs for full-page top-level navigation and Segment/toggle for in-context view switching within a section.
Do not declare the same utility or constant in multiple files; consolidate into a single shared location.
Define styled-components in a dedicated `styled.ts` file rather than co-locating them in the component file.
Use Ant Design's `Flex` component for flex layouts instead of creating custom styled divs with flexbox properties.
When a feature is gated behind a feature flag, ensure the flag is checked consistently across all views that render or reference the feature.
Use self-closing JSX syntax (`<Component />`) for components with no children instead of explicit open/close tags.
TypeScript files should use camelCase naming (e.g., getTheme.ts) rather than kebab-case (get-theme.ts) to match the exported function or class name.
Do not repurpose semantic color tokens (e.g., 'color-link') to replace unrelated colors; use the correct semantic token (e.g., 'primary') that matches the element's purpose.
When a React component contains complex logic (e.g., theme setup, side effects), extract it into a separate custom hook to improve readability and reusability.
Remove commented-out code before merging; use version control history to recover old code rather than leaving dead comments in the codebase.
Do not include files that are not imported or referenced anywhere in the codebase; verify that new or modified files are actually used before submitting a PR.
Avoid hardcoding version numbers in file names when the content is not version-specific; use generic names so the file doesn't need renaming with each product version.
Instead of casting to `any`, use TypeScript utility types like `ReturnType<typeof fn>` to derive proper types from existing functions and maintain type safety.
Package.json dependencies must never point to a feature branch when merging to develop or main; always use published package versions or the default branch.
API spec fields (especially those with non-obvious formats like string-encoded arrays) must include concrete examples to clarify expected values and usage.
When a value cannot be determined at runtime, default to the safer outcome (e.g., assume failure rather than success) to avoid silently masking errors.
When a function accepts multiple arguments, pass them as a named object so callers don't need to know the parameter order.
Use React Router hooks instead of the legacy withRouter HOC; higher-order components are no longer necessary with modern React Router.
Use a single Ant Design form instance per logical form scope; multiple instances fragment state management and bypass validation enforcement.
Before creating a new custom hook, check whether an existing hook already covers the use case to avoid duplication and inconsistent behavior.
Before defining new enums or type constants, check the codebase for existing definitions that cover the same domain to prevent duplication and drift.
Do not wrap JSX in a React Fragment when the component returns a single element or when no wrapper is needed.
Name boolean-returning methods to clearly express the predicate they check, including the condition and subject (e.g., areMultipleStoragesRegistered instead of ambiguous alternatives).
When only the count or existence of records is needed, use a count query at the persistence layer instead of fetching and counting full objects.
API endpoint summaries and descriptions must accurately reflect what the endpoint does; update them when the implementation changes.
Extract hardcoded default values and initial state into named constants or a shared initial-state definition to improve readability and reuse.
All form fields must have explicit name attributes to ensure proper form state tracking, validation, and submission.
API schemas should define a type in exactly one place; having the same concept defined at multiple levels (e.g., top-level and inside a validationSchema) creates ambiguity.
Always consume colors from the theme rather than importing them directly from a color constants file, so that palette changes (e.g., theme updates) propagate automatically.
Use a <button> element for actions and a <a> element for navigation; do not style a link to behave as a button or vice versa.
Feature-specific specializations of a shared component should live in their own file, not inside the base component's file.
Do not leave commented-out code in pull requests; remove dead code and rely on version control history if it is needed later.
When a JSX conditional involves multiple chained expressions, extract it into a descriptively named boolean variable defined before the return statement.
When a function's behavior is extended (e.g., adding a filter parameter), update its doc comment to describe the new functionality.
API route descriptions and parameter docs should use consistent casing, spacing, and terminology (e.g., 'data plane' as two words, 'ID' fully capitalized) matching the variable names they describe.
When adding new filtering or query capabilities to an endpoint, include integration or unit tests that verify the filtering behavior.
When a component is used across multiple sections or modules, move it to a shared component library (e.g., domino/ui) to avoid cross-section imports.
Use the 'value' prop to make a form component fully controlled by React state instead of using 'defaultValue' with additional synchronization logic.
Components consumed by multiple modules should be placed in packages/ui rather than owned by a single module, so changes driven by one module's requirements don't break others.
When the same code construction is repeated across a file or module, extract it into a shared utility function.
Avoid nested ternary expressions; use early returns, if/else blocks, or helper functions for multi-branch conditionals to preserve readability.
Group related logic (e.g., all variables for a single feature/flag) into one block rather than scattering it across the function, to improve readability.
Do not memoize (useMemo/useCallback) cheap operations; place lightweight computations directly in JSX or as simple variables instead.
When adding log-and-rethrow patterns, investigate whether something higher in the call stack is swallowing exceptions; fix the root cause rather than papering over it with extra logging.
In Scala pattern matches and destructuring, replace named bindings that are never used with underscore (_) to signal intent and avoid dead-code warnings.
Avoid nesting CSS class selectors inside styled components; apply styles directly to a new or existing styled component instead.
Use styled components instead of inline styles for all styling in React components.
Non-trivial filter/transform functions used inside components should be extracted into separate utility modules for reuse and testability.
When declaring @font-face rules, the font-weight value must reflect the actual weight of each font file so the browser selects the correct face.
When version identifiers are used across multiple files, extract them into a single constants file rather than duplicating string literals.

Name functions to reflect how their return value is used at call sites; if callers assign the result to `currentVersion`, the function should be named `getCurrentThemeVersion`, not something ambiguous.
When creating theme or style variants tied to a specific release, include the release version in the component name (e.g., `AppPanelThemeRelease5`) so the version context is immediately clear.
In frontend files, always place absolute/package imports before relative imports to maintain a consistent and scannable import order.
When a JSX block or expression grows complex, extract it into a descriptively named variable (e.g., `mainContent`) to improve readability and make the render tree scannable.
Avoid proliferating near-identical DTO classes (e.g., StorageDto, StorageInfoDto, StorageViewDto); prefer adding optional fields to a single DTO over creating multiple overlapping types.
When an endpoint returns data filtered by user permissions, explicitly verify that unauthenticated or non-user callers cannot access restricted data, and raise security concerns in review if unclear.
When naming a Map or dictionary variable, encode the key type in the name (e.g., `dataPlaneHwTierMap` when the key is a data plane ID) so readers immediately understand the lookup semantics.

When a field contains items filtered by the requester's permissions, name it to convey that scope (e.g., `accessibleHardwareTiers` instead of `hardwareTiers`) so consumers understand the data is user-scoped.
Use colors from the design system theme rather than hardcoding hex values; when the color palette is available in the theme, components should reference theme tokens.
When a component accumulates multiple derived calculations, extract them into a utility function or module to keep the component focused on rendering.
Instead of using `let` with value overwriting, extract the logic into a function that returns the computed value to avoid mutation and improve clarity.
Event streaming or cursor-based loops must include retry logic so that transient failures (e.g. cursor closure, node failover) are automatically recovered without dropping events.
When consuming a stream that triggers a write per event, consider batching writes (e.g. after N events or within a time window) to reduce write churn.
Name shared resources (collections, queues, tables) with domain-specific prefixes to avoid ambiguity when multiple subsystems use similar concepts.
Add a comment explaining why a retry or reconnection loop exists, including the failure scenarios it handles (e.g. cursor closed, node failover).
Use the project's theme helper or design token system for spacing, padding, and margin values instead of raw pixel literals.
Files named 'util' should contain pure utility functions, not React components. Move components to their own files with component-appropriate names.
Route paths like '/admin' should be defined as constants or derived from configuration, not hardcoded inline in components.

All values used inside a useMemo callback must appear in its dependency array. Instead of reading window.location directly, use values from useLocation() so React can track changes and trigger re-renders correctly.

React hooks must only be called from React function components or custom hooks, never from plain utility functions. Calling hooks from non-hook functions violates the Rules of Hooks and can crash the app.
Hooks must be called at the top level of a component, not inside arrays, objects, or conditional structures. Assign hook return values to variables first, then reference them in data structures.
If a file exports a React component, name it as a component (PascalCase, descriptive of what it renders), not as a utility or render function.
In theme configuration functions, use the colors parameter passed to the function rather than importing the colors module directly, to ensure theming remains configurable.
Component-specific styles should live with the component (CSS modules, styled-components, etc.), not in global CSS files.
When an error occurs in navigation or critical UI paths, provide a meaningful recovery experience (e.g. redirect to an error page) rather than just rendering an inline error message.
Keep domain logic (validation, business rules, entity construction) in the domain layer, not in infrastructure or service implementation layers.
Always use curly braces in Scala control structures and expressions, following the Databricks Scala style guide.
Choose the exception type that most accurately describes the underlying error (e.g., RecordNotFoundException for missing records rather than a generic exception).
When obfuscating or redacting sensitive values, use existing platform redaction mechanisms (e.g., the redacted method on telemetry events) rather than rolling custom obfuscation logic.
When deriving values from optional fields, ensure consistent behavior when the field is unset to avoid mismatched or unexpected output.
Place domain entities in packages that reflect their data source — entities retrieved from an upstream API belong in the upstream package, not in the local entity package.
Be consistent within a file about whether named parameters are used in function calls; don't mix named and positional styles for the same parameter across call sites.
When updating frontend component styles or icons, verify the change renders correctly in both the current and legacy themes.
When a class or model exists in one module, do not recreate an identical version in another module; extract it to a shared location or reuse the existing one.
Remote calls that retrieve configuration or schema data should be cached, even if call frequency seems low today, to guard against future usage growth.
