Before changing a component that has already been migrated to a new pattern, verify its migration status and consult the migration owner rather than rolling it back to the legacy approach.
Keep CSS class selectors flat; avoid nesting classes when possible to maintain readability and reduce specificity issues.
Pass color and fontSize directly to IconResolver via its props rather than creating Styled component wrappers around icons.
In frontend files, always place absolute (library/package) imports before relative imports and apply this consistently across all changed files.
When setting icon or component colors, verify they match the intended design token in the current theme rather than assuming a color name maps to the expected visual.
Use the project's spacing utilities (e.g., getMargins, themeHelper) for margins and padding rather than hardcoding pixel or em values inline.
Express sizes in px rather than em to maintain consistency with the codebase's established convention; do not introduce em without a specific justification.
A styled component's name must describe what it actually renders; do not name a component 'Span' if it renders an icon or other non-span element.
Reference palette tokens (e.g., palette.borderLight) instead of hardcoding hex or RGB color values in styled components.
Do not re-cast a value that has already been cast earlier in the same scope; use the previously cast variable instead.
When an API requires a context object, use a proper default (e.g., DominoContext.uncorrelated) instead of passing null, even if the context is not currently used.
Audit trails should only capture user-initiated actions, not system-driven state changes like automated job status transitions.
Do not introduce additional database queries solely for audit or telemetry purposes; capture audit data from information already available in the call path.
Parameter names should be aligned with their corresponding type names for readability and consistency.
When adding new injection modules, follow the same DI pattern (e.g., @provides with named annotations) used by existing similar modules for consistency.
Import classes directly rather than using intermediate package-qualified access (e.g., `import events.AuditTrailV2Transformer` should be `import AuditTrailV2Transformer`).
When moving a class to a different module, move its corresponding test spec to the same module to keep tests co-located with the code they verify.
When implementing rollback or cleanup stacks, write tests for execution order and for behavior when individual rollback callbacks throw errors.
Expected operational behavior like rollbacks should be logged at info level, not warn; reserve warn/error for genuinely unexpected conditions.
Do not pass another class's logger instance into a utility class; each class should use its own logger, and correlation should be handled via DominoContext.
Critical operational failures (like failed rollbacks) should trigger active admin notifications via supportAlerter.error(), not rely solely on log output which will likely go unnoticed.
When adding support for a new provider or integration, ensure all related feature gates and requirement checks include the new provider to maintain consistent behavior.
Replace useState-driven derived values with useMemo to avoid unnecessary re-renders and simplify component logic; use early-return if-statements over complex ternary chains.
When multiple components or functions share the same data shape, define a shared interface or type alias rather than repeating the structure.
Move complex inline expressions out of JSX and into named variables or functions defined before the return statement for readability.
Wrap inline event handler functions passed as props with useCallback to prevent unnecessary re-renders of child components.
Modal wrapper components should extend the base modal's props interface rather than redeclaring common props like open and onClose.
Use the simplest accurate name (e.g. datasetIds) and only add qualifiers like toInclude/toExclude when a complementary parameter is actually introduced.
When a function's return value is not immediately clear from the signature, add a comment above it describing what it returns.
New routes and pages must include feature flag validation so they can be toggled without code changes.
Reusable UI components like cards should live in packages/ui, not in app-specific module directories.
Do not add extra wrapper div elements when the existing component structure or a Fragment would suffice.
Use generic terms like 'A user' instead of 'A Domino user' in user-facing copy to support white-labeling.
When the same logic is repeated across multiple components, extract it into a shared custom hook.
When a value does not need to trigger a re-render, use useRef instead of useState to avoid unnecessary render cycles.
When using spacing utilities like getMargin, verify the multiplier base value (e.g., BASE = 4px) so the computed result matches the intended pixel value.
When a reviewer identifies a misuse of a utility function, systematically review all call sites for the same mistake rather than fixing only the commented instance.
Use the same CSS unit system (px vs rem) consistently throughout the application; do not introduce a different unit convention in isolated components.
When computing layout values based on viewport size, use a ResizeObserver instead of a one-time measurement so the layout updates when the viewport is resized.
New DynamicField types must check whether the field is editable and only render controls when editable; return null otherwise.
Inline magic values (numbers, strings) used in component logic should be extracted into named constants for clarity and maintainability.
DynamicField component types should remain simple, reusable form controls; complex domain-specific components should be extracted into their own module rather than embedded as a field type.
Do not leave commented-out code in the codebase; remove dead code and rely on version control to recover it if needed.
When adding conditional logic for specific providers or enum values, ensure all variants are handled — including edge cases like 'unknown' or unsupported values.
Name non-function constants (arrays, config objects, column definitions) using UPPER_SNAKE_CASE to distinguish them from functions and variables.
Do not use explicit `return` statements in Scala; instead, restructure control flow (e.g., invert the if condition) so the last expression is the return value.

When declaring types for fields or parameters, match the type used in the upstream definition (e.g., use BigDecimal if the source case class defines BigDecimal, not Int) to avoid silent precision loss.
Define pure utility functions (sorting, filtering, formatting) outside the component body to avoid unnecessary re-creation on each render and improve testability.
Move reusable helper and utility functions out of component files into dedicated utils files to improve discoverability and reuse.
Use the project's design system spacing helpers (e.g., getMargin, getSpacing) instead of raw numeric values to maintain consistent spacing across the UI.
Place constants in companion objects rather than inline in class bodies, and reference existing constants from other modules instead of redefining them.
When modeling JSON Schema enums, support JsValue (not just String) to accommodate numeric and other non-string enum values from upstream schemas.
Always wrap API requests in error handling (try/catch or .catch) to prevent unhandled promise rejections from crashing the component.
Use optional chaining (?.) with nullish coalescing (??) instead of verbose conditional expressions for accessing potentially null/undefined properties.
Use type-safe enum mapping builders (e.g., StrictEnumBiMapBuilder) to validate bijection between related enums at class-load time, ensuring new enum values are never silently missed.
Do not render empty or meaningless DOM elements (e.g., empty spans) when the underlying data is absent; conditionally render only when there is content to display.
Do not create type definitions or intermediate variables for values that are hardcoded inline — pass them directly as object literals.
Access colors through themeHelper (e.g., themeHelper('colors.white')(props)) instead of importing and referencing the colors variable directly.
Always use colors from the theme system rather than hardcoded color values in styled components.
Use object destructuring when accessing multiple properties from props or other objects for cleaner, more readable code.
Use theme spacing utilities like getMargin and getPadding instead of hardcoded margin/padding values in styled components.
Use Ant Design's built-in form capabilities (Form, Form.List, Form.Item shouldUpdate, onFinish handler) instead of building custom form state management with nested conditionals.
Use Ant Design Form's built-in submit handler and form change events rather than manually wiring up submit and change logic.
Do not check the same condition twice in contiguous lines — consolidate duplicate conditional checks into a single block.
Large render blocks and complex JSX fragments within utility files should be extracted into their own separate components.
Use native JavaScript methods (e.g., Array.find, Array.includes) instead of utility library equivalents (Ramda, lodash) when the native API provides the same functionality.
Use JSX boolean prop shorthand — write <Component accordion /> instead of <Component accordion={true} />.
When a function or route helper has many positional parameters, convert to an options object so callers are not sensitive to argument order.
Sort JSX component props alphabetically for consistency and easier scanning during code review.
Keep JSX component props sorted alphabetically for consistency and readability.
Remove commented-out code before merging; use TODOs or tickets to track future work instead of leaving dead code.
Do not add explicit null/undefined checks when the falsy value already produces the correct result (e.g., `undefined !== 'blank'` is already false).
Use an explicit `if` guard clause instead of the TypeScript non-null assertion operator (!) to handle potentially null values.
Wrap expensive computations in lambdas or by-name parameters so they are only evaluated when actually needed.
When adding cross-cutting policies or behaviors, use a no-op/null implementation for modules that should not be affected to prevent unintended side effects.
When a resource's cleanup requires more than `close()` (e.g., `dispose()`), ensure it is explicitly called since `Using` blocks only invoke `close()`/`release()`.
Add a code comment explaining the reasoning behind non-obvious default values and magic numbers (e.g., why a multiplier is 1.2).
Before writing custom utility code, check if an existing library already in the project can handle the same task — it will be documented, tested, and maintained externally.
When pattern matching on enum-like types (e.g., file change types), explicitly consider all variants including less obvious ones like DELETE to avoid silent bugs.
Before implementing new logic, check if an existing method already provides the needed functionality to avoid duplication.
Move validation checks out of API service classes into a dedicated validator domain service (e.g., `ModelDeploymentValidator`).
Methods that don't depend on instance state should be made static by moving them to the companion object.
Regex patterns and other compile-once values should be defined as static fields on the companion object so they are only compiled once.
Avoid accessing tuple fields by positional index (`_1`, `_2`); use a named case class instead for readability and type safety.
When the same logic appears multiple times in a method, extract it into a separate function to reduce duplication.
Each React custom hook should live in its own file rather than being grouped in a shared hooks utility file.
Verify that callback functions passed as props or used as effect dependencies are memoized (e.g., with useCallback) to prevent unnecessary re-renders.
Replace switch statements or chained conditionals that map values (e.g., status to icon) with an object/dictionary lookup.
When multiple components use the same value-to-element mapping, extract the dictionary into a shared constant and reuse it.
Controllers should not contain domain lookup or enrichment logic; encapsulate it in a domain service so the controller remains thin.
Methods that return multiple related fields should use a named domain object (case class) rather than tuples or generic types, so each field's purpose is clear.
When a component is visually and functionally identical to one used elsewhere, extract it into a shared general component rather than duplicating it.
When adding documentation to one function or command in a group, add equivalent documentation to all sibling functions in the same file.
Memoize expensive computations like sorting or filtering with useMemo to avoid re-running them on every React re-render.
Use theme helper tokens (e.g. themeHelper('palette.textPrimary')) instead of hardcoded color values to ensure consistency and support theming.
Before building a new UI element, check if an existing shared component already provides the needed functionality and use it instead.
Maintain correct import ordering with absolute paths before relative paths; verify linting is enforcing import sort rules in all files.
Avoid casting types when the auto-generated API client already provides correct type definitions; only cast when the API types are provably wrong.
Extract magic numbers into named constants (e.g. MAX_TEMPLATES_TO_SHOW) so the intent is clear and the value is reusable.
Do not add @ts-ignore without a justifying comment; audit existing @ts-ignore directives and resolve them via dedicated tech-debt tasks.
When a component is already large, do not add more state or business logic to it; encapsulate new logic within the child component or a dedicated hook.
Remove default values for props or parameters that are always explicitly provided by callers, as dead defaults obscure the actual data flow.
Use styled-components for styling React components instead of inline style objects or style props.
Never define a React component inside another component's render body; extract it to module scope or assign JSX directly to a variable without the arrow function wrapper.
Do not use separate state and useEffect for values that can be directly calculated from existing props or state; use derived/computed values instead.
Never mutate original arrays or objects passed as props or stored in state; clone data before performing filtering or other mutations to prevent React rendering issues.
When a permission is not found or undefined, default to hiding/denying access rather than showing/allowing, to prevent accidental exposure of restricted features.
After filtering children from a parent container (submenu, group), check if the parent has any remaining children and hide it if empty.
Name files to accurately reflect their content scope; a file containing admin-specific logic should be prefixed or named accordingly, not given a generic name.
Keep API changes, dependency updates, and other unrelated modifications in separate PRs to avoid merge conflicts and simplify review.
Always add error handling UI (error states, notifications, or fallback messages) when making API calls so users are informed when something fails.
When two utility functions have identical logic, combine them into a single shared function with optional parameters to handle differences.
When UI visibility depends on async permission data, implement loading and error states to prevent items from flickering or being incorrectly shown while data loads or if the request fails.
Use the project's design system spacing functions (e.g., getMargin) for padding and margin values instead of hardcoded pixel values to maintain consistency.
Avoid explicitly setting CSS properties to their default values (e.g., flex-direction: row) as it adds unnecessary code and can mislead readers into thinking a non-default value is intentional.
Prefer existing utility hooks (e.g., useInterval) over manual setInterval/setTimeout with useEffect cleanup to reduce boilerplate and avoid common timer bugs.
Move styled component definitions out of the component file and into a separate file to keep component logic clean and styles isolated.
Reference traits (interfaces) rather than concrete service implementations to maintain loose coupling and testability.
Before creating a new UI component, check if a similar shared component already exists and consult UX to confirm whether the standard component can be used.
When importing modules from within the same package, use relative import paths instead of absolute paths.
Add comments specifying the expected units for numeric fields (e.g., milliseconds, bytes, seconds) to prevent misinterpretation.
Wrap asynchronous API calls in try/catch blocks and surface errors to the user with appropriate error UI.
Access color values through the themeHelper utility rather than directly accessing theme properties, to ensure consistent theming support.
Never use hardcoded color constants directly; always access colors through the theme context so that values respond correctly to theme selection.
Use px units for margin and padding values to stay consistent with the rest of the codebase, unless there is a specific reason to use relative units.
Declare values that do not change as constants (const) rather than variables to communicate intent and prevent accidental mutation.
When adding environment variables or annotations to Kubernetes workloads, also add corresponding labels so administrators and tooling can easily filter and query workloads.
Do not assume runtime metadata or configuration values will always be defined; guard against missing values with explicit checks or Option types.
Code should be readable and understandable without requiring the author to explain it; refactor complex logic until a reviewer can follow it unaided.
Do not wrap a field in Option when an empty string is a valid value; use a plain String type instead to avoid ambiguity between None and Some("").
When adding artifacts tied to an optional feature (e.g., log files, config entries), gate their creation on whether the feature is enabled to avoid confusing users with irrelevant output.
When user permission state is unknown or a permission check fails, default to the most restrictive access level rather than assuming full privileges.
When a UI component depends on a service call, provide user-visible feedback when that service fails rather than silently swallowing the error.
Use named constants (e.g., Status.CONFLICT) instead of magic numbers (e.g., 409) for HTTP status codes to improve readability and maintainability.
Double-check that authorization checks map to the correct permission level; flipped or mismatched auth checks silently grant or deny the wrong access.
Name parameters that filter query results with a prefix like 'filterBy' to clearly communicate their purpose and distinguish them from identity or lookup parameters.
Extract inline event handler functions into named functions wrapped with useCallback for improved readability and to avoid unnecessary re-renders.
Before creating custom styled components for common layouts (e.g., flex containers), check if an equivalent shared component already exists in the UI package.
Do not hardcode shared values (URLs, support links, config strings) in feature modules; define them in the appropriate shared utility file.
In JSX, omit the explicit `={true}` when passing boolean props; the prop name alone implies true.
Hide pagination controls when there is insufficient data to paginate rather than showing an empty or non-functional paginator.
When modifying a shared or base component, validate the change across all consuming components to ensure no regressions.
Never import from a package's own dist output within that same package; use relative source imports instead.
Prefer built-in component props (e.g. Ant Design's `noStyle`) over custom styled-component overrides to reset or modify component styling.
Do not hardcode URLs that vary across deployments; use the HelpLink component or its hook to resolve environment-specific links.
When a configuration API provides a method like `get` that throws with the key name on missing values, prefer it over manual error handling with `getOptional` plus custom exceptions.
Do not leave commented-out code in the codebase unless it is tracked by a ticket; prefer removing it and referencing the ticket if the code will be needed later.
Remove or revert exploratory dependency or configuration changes that did not resolve the original issue before merging.
Name files containing styled-components as `styled-components.tsx` rather than generic names like `styles.tsx`.
Wrap JSON.parse calls in try/catch even when form validation exists, to guard against edge cases where the value may be undefined or an empty string.
When a periodic task has multiple independent stages, catch NonFatal exceptions in each stage independently so a failure in one does not prevent the others from executing.
When fixing a bug, add a regression test that reproduces the original failure scenario to prevent the bug from reoccurring.
Test names and assertions should use positive statements ('these are supported') rather than negative exclusions ('all except X'), especially when the excluded set may grow independently.
Bug fixes should include a unit test covering the corrected behavior; make methods package-private if needed to enable focused testing.

Instead of changing the behavior of a widely-used method (e.g., adding case-insensitivity), create a new method variant and use it only where needed, with a comment explaining why the variant is necessary.
Do not allow language-specific sentinel values like the string "None" to propagate through the system as valid data; throw an exception or use a proper domain-level default instead.
Validate and normalize external input (e.g., enum string values) at the API layer rather than adding defensive workarounds deeper in the codebase.
Move TypeScript types and interfaces to a dedicated `types` file rather than defining them inline in utility or component files.
Use idiomatic collection operations like `drop(offset).take(limit)` for in-memory pagination instead of manual index arithmetic with `slice`.
Method names should accurately describe their behavior; if a method performs pagination, include that in the name (e.g., `getPaginatedModelDeployments`).
Consolidate related parsing steps (e.g., splitting, field extraction, default handling) into a single function rather than scattering them across the call site.
Never retrieve all records from a datastore on every request; apply filters, pagination, or scoping at the query level to avoid fetching too much data.
Keep imports organized and properly sorted in every file; disorganized imports signal careless code and make files harder to scan.
Render modal instances at the table level (once), not inside each table row; row actions should only trigger the shared modal to open.
Add explicit conditional guards before type-specific operations to make the code's intent clear and prevent unintended execution on wrong types.
Add DEBUG or TRACE level log statements before key decision points and side-effecting operations to aid troubleshooting in production.
Create a dedicated unit test spec class for every new service or domain class introduced.
Function names must accurately describe the scope of their side effects; if a function stops an entire workflow rather than a single task, name it accordingly and document the blast radius.
Choose field names that are unambiguous in context; when a name could be confused with existing fields, either pick a clearer name or add a comment explaining its purpose.
Do not pass arguments to a function that are not declared in its signature; relying on closure scope while appearing to pass a parameter is misleading.
Use JSX boolean shorthand for true props (write `<Comp disabled />` instead of `<Comp disabled={true} />`).
User-facing APIs should accept generalized parameter names and map them to provider-specific equivalents internally, rather than exposing implementation details.
Instead of commenting out code, create a new version of the variable or logic with the desired behavior and use it directly.

When calling an RPC or service method, pass only the essential identifier and assemble the full request from available context on the receiving side, rather than forwarding redundant data the callee already has.
Use CSS rules to handle visual layout concerns like spacing and dividers rather than restructuring JSX or adding JavaScript logic.
When modifying authorization roles and permissions, explicitly verify that each role grants only the intended access and does not inadvertently expose resources to restricted roles.
Remove conditional checks that can never be triggered due to earlier guard clauses, and clean up leftover test or debug code before merging.
When overriding a method to change behavior for one specific case, delegate all other cases to the parent implementation (e.g., super.method) rather than hardcoding every case.
In tests that don't depend on real system behavior (e.g., real clock), use simulated or mocked dependencies to make it clear the test is not coupled to external state.
When consuming external service responses, prefer structured/typed APIs over string parsing to reduce fragility and improve maintainability.
When accessing optional data (e.g., roles, credentials), explicitly handle the case where the value is absent and determine whether absence is a valid state or an error.
When using undocumented or hard-to-find external API endpoints, add a comment explaining where the endpoint came from and where to find related documentation.
Do not introduce dependencies that have not been updated in years; prefer actively maintained libraries to reduce risk from unpatched bugs and vulnerabilities.
Never reference colors directly from the colors map; always access them through the themeHelper to ensure consistent theming and support theme switching.
Use existing utility library functions (e.g., ramda, lodash) for common operations like unique/set/filter instead of writing custom implementations.
Do not extract single-use helper functions; place the logic directly in the JSX where it is consumed to reduce indirection.
When logic is reusable across components or modules, extract it into a shared utility function rather than leaving it inline in a single component.
When you need a variant of an existing utility (e.g., a different ID type), extend the existing utility rather than creating a duplicate with a hardcoded copy of the same value.
When building or transforming a collection into a new structure, use reduce (or equivalent functional method) instead of forEach with mutation.
Extract magic numbers into named constants to improve readability and maintainability.
Use built-in unique ID generation (e.g., Date.now(), crypto.randomUUID()) instead of manually maintaining counter variables for keys.
When a method serves a specific use case within an overloaded domain concept, name it to convey that specific intent rather than reusing a generic name.
Test names should precisely describe the scope being tested so readers can understand coverage without reading the test body.
Never hardcode font family values; use the theme's typography tokens so fonts remain consistent and centrally configurable.
Use existing design system semantic color tokens (e.g., palette.danger) instead of introducing custom hex values; minimize custom colors in the theme.
The theme should remain general-purpose; do not add component-specific one-off colors to the theme object — map to existing semantic tokens instead.
When migrating icon libraries (e.g., AntD to FontAwesome), preserve the original aria-label values to avoid breaking existing integration/E2E tests.
When a color maps to a known semantic role (danger, warning, etc.), use the palette token rather than a raw hex value, even if they resolve to the same color.
Do not explicitly set style properties (fontSize, fontWeight, colors) that already match the theme or framework defaults — remove them to reduce noise and prevent drift.
Before adding a color, spacing, or other design token, check whether the default theme/palette already defines it — avoid duplicating values that are already provided.
When implementing paginated API responses, use the codebase's standardized pagination traits (e.g., PaginatedResponse) rather than creating ad-hoc pagination wrappers.
Use the simplest accurate name for entity types (e.g., "user" not "userAccount") unless disambiguation is genuinely needed.
Be cautious when adding feature-specific changes to shared/generic modules (e.g., telemetry, events) — evaluate whether the additions are truly generic or if they belong in a feature-specific module.
When building a v2 or replacement implementation, create a dedicated test spec file rather than appending tests to the legacy version's spec.
Use the project's standard logging trait (e.g., DominoLoggable) or lazy logger pattern rather than manually creating Logger instances, unless there is a specific reason to use a custom logger name.
Place serialization logic in the domain type's companion object or a dedicated serializer in the same namespace, rather than in consuming services.
Add dedicated serialization tests for any non-trivial custom serialization logic to verify the output matches expectations.
Remove temporary debug logging and print statements before merging, or at minimum reduce them to trace level.
When testing a feature with multiple behaviors or branches (e.g., target and location), add tests covering each path for completeness.
When binding a service or scheduler in a shared injection module, verify which deployment instances actually need it to avoid unnecessary resource consumption.
In test generators, use the most semantically specific generator available (e.g., relativeFilePathGen for a sub-directory) rather than a more general one.
Remove development print statements (println, console.log, etc.) from production code before submitting a PR.
When applying encoding workarounds (like double-encoding for framework bugs), centralize the logic in a single utility so it can be changed in one place when the framework is updated.
When adding encoding logic to shared/common components, ensure already-encoded inputs are not encoded again to prevent corruption of URLs and paths.
When adding customer-facing features or changing operational behavior, update the relevant runbook so on-call engineers have current documentation.
Avoid setting both `data-test` and `testId` props on the same component; use only the one required by your testing framework.
Use the shared `getErrorMessage` utility from `@domino/ui/dist/utils/errorUtil` to parse error messages instead of writing custom error extraction logic.
Maintain consistent import ordering: absolute (third-party and aliased) imports must appear before relative imports.
When a component file contains utility functions, constants, and sub-components alongside the main component, split them into separate files (utils, sub-component, main component).
Use styled-components for all styling instead of inline styles to maintain consistency, reusability, and separation of concerns.
When mocking components in tests, use the shared mock utilities pattern (as in `frontend/packages/mocks`) to retain access to props and callbacks on mocked components.
When mocking data in tests, create generator functions in `frontend/packages/mocks/generators` so the mock data can be reused across test suites.
Prefer immutable array methods like `slice` over mutating methods like `splice`, even when working with copies, to signal immutable intent and prevent accidental mutation bugs.
When intentionally omitting dependencies from a React useEffect dependency array, leave a comment explaining why to prevent future developers from mistakenly adding them.
Avoid creating separate React state for values that can be computed or derived from existing state; use useMemo or inline computation instead.
Before creating custom styled elements (e.g., styled anchor tags, custom link wrappers), check if an existing shared component already provides the needed functionality.
Import styled-components files using namespace imports (`import * as Styles from './styles'`) so styled components are clearly distinguishable as style elements in JSX.
Use theme tokens or palette colors instead of hardcoded color values, and avoid using `!important` overrides in styled-components.
Verify that new exports, components, and utility functions are actually imported and used somewhere; remove any dead code before merging.
Calculate derived state directly from existing values instead of using useState + useEffect; use `const isDisabled = isSubmitting || !definition` rather than syncing state in an effect.
Use theme helper tokens (e.g., margins.tiny, margins.small) instead of hardcoded pixel values in styled components.
When encountering an error, investigate and fix the root cause rather than disabling or working around the failing code path.
When refactoring moves responsibility from one class to another, ensure equivalent unit tests are created for the new owner.
Do not wrap simple or static computations in useMemo; reserve memoization for genuinely expensive calculations or referential equality needs.
Never hardcode documentation or support URLs; use the project's support utils, HelpLink component, or docs base path hook so links stay correct across environments.
When the same conditional logic is used in multiple places, extract it into a shared utility or variable to avoid repetition and reduce divergence risk.
When multiple styled components share the same styles (e.g., icon wrappers), consolidate them into a single reusable styled component.
If a component is already a styled component, define all its styles (including fontSize) in the styled definition rather than passing them as inline style props.

When implementing operations that can be triggered concurrently (e.g., from multiple frontend instances), design for race conditions by detecting and resolving conflicts downstream rather than relying on optimistic checks.
Query methods that filter, scope, and sort results must have tests verifying each dimension: correct entity type, correct scope, correct exclusions, and correct ordering.
When a function's actual behavior is narrower or different from what its name implies, rename it to accurately reflect what it does.
When implementing a feature that touches project-type-specific code paths, verify it works across all supported project types (e.g., DFS and GBP).
When a function is fully replaced by a new implementation, delete the old function rather than leaving dead code.
Name constants to clearly indicate which domain or subsystem they belong to, so readers can understand their purpose without navigating to the definition.
Keep dependency injection framework imports (e.g., Guice) confined to injection/wiring modules rather than spreading them into domain or service classes, to avoid transitive dependency leakage.
When logic is extracted into a new class, that class must have its own dedicated unit tests — do not rely on tests that existed for the original caller.
Every new code path or branch must have a corresponding test case, even if it means adding the test in a follow-up PR.
Do not add fields, configuration, or dependencies to a data model until they are actually needed — defer additions until there is a concrete consumer.
Service implementations that depend on external communication (HTTP, gRPC, etc.) belong in the infrastructure layer, not in the server/application layer.
When naming entity types in events or audit trails, be specific enough to distinguish the granularity level (e.g., a single setting vs. an entire config page).
A field named with a suffix like 'Id' must actually contain an identifier, not a display name or other value — misleading suffixes cause bugs when developers trust the name.
Reference defined constant values (e.g., EventTarget.Project) instead of raw string literals in tests and production code to catch refactoring errors at compile time.
When emitting telemetry or audit trail events for an entity, include all available identifying attributes (e.g., both ID and name) so events are useful for debugging and reporting.
Use TypeScript `interface` rather than `type` for defining React component props, as interface is the team convention and provides better extensibility and error messages.
Order import statements so that absolute (package/library) imports appear before relative (local file) imports.
Prefer styled components over inline style attributes; use colors from the design theme rather than hardcoded values; and replace magic numbers with named constants for readability.
When a common/shared component already exists (e.g., a Link component), use it instead of creating a local duplicate with equivalent functionality.
In React/TypeScript files, leave a blank line between type/interface declarations and component definitions, and between successive styled component definitions, for visual separation.
Use router links instead of anchor tags when navigating to React pages to avoid unnecessary full page reloads.
Use semantic color tokens from the design system (e.g., textPrimary, textSecondary) instead of hardcoded color values in styled components.
Use border color tokens from the theme palette for dividers and borders instead of hardcoded color values.
Use theme spacing helpers (e.g., getMargin) instead of deprecated padding/margin constants for calculating spacing values.
Use the project's themeHelper utility for all styled-component values instead of accessing theme properties directly or using hardcoded values.
Use existing route helper functions to build internal navigation URLs instead of constructing URL strings manually.
Before removing or renaming props on shared/core UI components, verify that no other consumers depend on them, especially when the change won't merge soon.
When replacing legacy Ant Design icons with custom icons, preserve the original aria-label values to avoid breaking existing tests.
When calling setState inside useEffect, ensure the dependency condition is strong enough to prevent infinite render loops.
When a conditional branch sets a value and returns, drop the else clause and let code fall through to improve readability.
Before writing custom useEffect + useState patterns, check if existing hooks (e.g., useOnChange, useRemoteData) already provide the needed state management and callbacks.
When you need to refresh data after a mutation, use the refetch method from useRemoteData instead of duplicating remote state into local state.
When multiple async operations exist in the same component, handle their side effects (e.g., page reload, refetch, notifications) consistently unless there is an explicit reason to differ.
Define event handler functions with useCallback to prevent unnecessary re-creation on every render, especially when passed as props to child components.
If a method's return value can never vary (e.g., always returns true), refactor the return type to reflect actual behavior (e.g., Unit/void) rather than suggesting a branching outcome.
